T1021.004.name: Potential SSH Brute Force Detected on Privileged Account
T1021.004.description:
Identifies multiple consecutive login failures targeting a root user account from the same source address and within a
short time interval. Adversaries will often brute force login attempts on privileged accounts with a common or known
password, in an attempt to gain privileged access to systems.

T1021.004.name: Deprecated - SSH Connection Established Inside A Running Container
T1021.004.description:
This rule detects an incoming SSH connection established inside a running container. Running an ssh daemon inside a
container should be avoided and monitored closely if necessary. If an attacker gains valid credentials they can use it
to gain initial access or establish persistence within a compromi

T1021.004.name: Deprecated - SSH Process Launched From Inside A Container
T1021.004.description:
This rule detects an SSH or SSHD process executed from inside a container. This includes both the client ssh binary and
server ssh daemon process. SSH usage inside a container should be avoided and monitored closely when necessary. With
valid credentials an attacker may move laterally to other conta

T1021.004.name: Deprecated - SSH Authorized Keys File Modified Inside a Container
T1021.004.description:
This rule detects the creation or modification of an authorized_keys or sshd_config file inside a container. The Secure
Shell (SSH) authorized_keys file specifies which users are allowed to log into a server using public key authentication.
Adversaries may modify it to maintain persistence on a vict

T1021.004.name: SSH Authorized Keys File Modification
T1021.004.description:
The Secure Shell (SSH) authorized_keys file specifies which users are allowed to log into a server using public key
authentication. Adversaries may modify it to maintain persistence on a victim host by adding their own public key(s).

T1021.004.name: Remote File Creation in World Writeable Directory
T1021.004.description:
This rule detects the creation of a file in a world-writeable directory through a service that is
commonly used for file transfer. This behavior is often associated with lateral movement and can
be an indicator of an attacker attempting to move laterally within a network.

T1021.004.name: Potential SSH-IT SSH Worm Downloaded
T1021.004.description:
Identifies processes that are capable of downloading files with command line arguments containing URLs to SSH-IT's
autonomous SSH worm. This worm intercepts outgoing SSH connections every time a user uses ssh.

T1021.004.name: SSH Process Launched From Inside A Container
T1021.004.description:
This rule detects an SSH or SSHD process executed from inside a container. This includes both the client ssh binary and
server ssh daemon process. SSH usage inside a container should be avoided and monitored closely when necessary. With
valid credentials an attacker may move laterally to other conta

T1021.004.name: Unusual Remote File Creation
T1021.004.description:
This rule leverages the new_terms rule type to detect file creation via a commonly used file
transfer service while excluding typical remote file creation activity. This behavior is
often linked to lateral movement, potentially indicating an attacker attempting to move
within a network.

T1021.004.name: Modification of OpenSSH Binaries
T1021.004.description:
Adversaries may modify SSH related binaries for persistence or credential access by patching sensitive functions to
enable unauthorized access or by logging SSH credentials for exfiltration.

T1021.004.name: SSH Key Generated via ssh-keygen
T1021.004.description:
This rule identifies the creation of SSH keys using the ssh-keygen tool, which is the standard utility for generating
SSH keys. Users often create SSH keys for authentication with remote services. However, threat actors can exploit this
tool to move laterally across a network or maintain persistence

T1021.004.name: Network Connection Initiated by SSHD Child Process
T1021.004.description:
This rule identifies an egress internet connection initiated by an SSH Daemon child process. This behavior is
indicative of the alteration of a shell configuration file or other mechanism that launches a process when a
new SSH login occurs. Attackers can also backdoor the SSH daemon to allow for per

T1021.004.name: Potential Execution via XZBackdoor
T1021.004.description:
It identifies potential malicious shell executions through remote SSH and detects cases where the sshd service suddenly
terminates soon after successful execution, suggesting suspicious behavior similar to the XZ backdoor.

T1021.004.name: Unusual SSHD Child Process
T1021.004.description:
This rule detects the creation of an unusual SSHD child process through the usage of the `new_terms` rule type.
Attackers may abuse SSH to maintain persistence on a compromised system, or to establish a backdoor for remote access,
potentially resulting in an unusual SSHD child process being created.

T1021.004.name: Remote SSH Login Enabled via systemsetup Command
T1021.004.description: Detects use of the systemsetup command to enable remote SSH Login.

T1021.004.name: Potential Remote Desktop Tunneling Detected
T1021.004.description:
Identifies potential use of an SSH utility to establish RDP over a reverse SSH Tunnel. This can be used by attackers to
enable routing of network packets that would otherwise not reach their intended destination.

T1021.004.name: AWS EC2 Instance Connect SSH Public Key Uploaded
T1021.004.description:
Identifies when a new SSH public key is uploaded to an AWS EC2 instance using the EC2 Instance Connect service. This
action could indicate an adversary attempting to maintain access to the instance. The rule detects the
SendSerialConsoleSSHPublicKey or SendSSHPublicKey API actions, which are logged

T1040.name: Network Sniffing via Tcpdump
T1040.description:
The Tcpdump program ran on a Linux host. Tcpdump is a network monitoring or packet sniffing tool that can be used to
capture insecure credentials or data in motion. Sniffing can also be used to discover details of network services as a
prelude to lateral movement or defense evasion.

T1040.name: AWS EC2 Full Network Packet Capture Detected
T1040.description:
Detects successful creation of an Amazon EC2 Traffic Mirroring session. A session copies full packets from a source
Elastic Network Interface (ENI) to a mirror target (e.g., an ENI or NLB) using a mirror filter (ingress/egress rules).
While used for diagnostics and NDR/IDS tooling, adversaries can a

T1040.name: Azure VNet Full Network Packet Capture Enabled
T1040.description:
Identifies potential full network packet capture in Azure. Packet Capture is an Azure Network Watcher feature that can
be used to inspect network traffic. This feature can potentially be abused to read sensitive data from unencrypted
internal traffic.

T1562.001.name: Attempt to Disable IPTables or Firewall
T1562.001.description:
Adversaries may attempt to disable the iptables or firewall service in an attempt to affect how a host is allowed to
receive or send network traffic.

T1562.001.name: Elastic Agent Service Terminated
T1562.001.description:
Identifies the Elastic endpoint agent has stopped and is no longer running on the host. Adversaries may attempt to
disable security monitoring tools in an attempt to evade detection or prevention capabilities during an intrusion. This
may also indicate an issue with the agent itself and should be ad

T1562.001.name: Tampering with RUNNER_TRACKING_ID in GitHub Actions Runners
T1562.001.description:
This rule detects processes spawned by GitHub Actions runners where "RUNNER_TRACKING_ID" is overridden from its
default "github_*" value. Such tampering has been associated with attempts to evade runner tracking/cleanup on
self-hosted runners, including behavior observed in the Shai-Hulud 2.0 npm wo

T1562.001.name: Attempt to Disable Auditd Service
T1562.001.description:
Adversaries may attempt to disable the Auditd service to evade detection. Auditd is a Linux service that provides system
auditing and logging. Disabling the Auditd service can prevent the system from logging important security events, which
can be used to detect malicious activity.

T1562.001.name: Attempt to Disable IPTables or Firewall
T1562.001.description:
Adversaries may attempt to disable the iptables or firewall service in an attempt to affect how a host is allowed to
receive or send network traffic.

T1562.001.name: Attempt to Disable Syslog Service
T1562.001.description:
Adversaries may attempt to disable the syslog service in an attempt to an attempt to disrupt event logging and evade
detection by security controls.

T1562.001.name: Attempt to Clear Kernel Ring Buffer
T1562.001.description:
Monitors for the deletion of the kernel ring buffer events through dmesg. Attackers may clear kernel ring buffer events
to evade detection after installing a Linux kernel module (LKM).

T1562.001.name: Potential Disabling of AppArmor
T1562.001.description:
This rule monitors for potential attempts to disable AppArmor. AppArmor is a Linux security module that enforces
fine-grained access control policies to restrict the actions and resources that specific applications and processes can
access. Adversaries may disable security tools to avoid possible de

T1562.001.name: Potential Disabling of SELinux
T1562.001.description:
Identifies potential attempts to disable Security-Enhanced Linux (SELinux), which is a Linux kernel security feature to
support access control policies. Adversaries may disable security tools to avoid possible detection of their tools and
activities.

T1562.001.name: Attempt to Clear Logs via Journalctl
T1562.001.description:
This rule monitors for attempts to clear logs using the "journalctl" command on Linux systems. Adversaries may use this
technique to cover their tracks by deleting or truncating log files, making it harder for defenders to investigate their
activities. The rule looks for the execution of "journalctl

T1562.001.name: Kernel Module Removal
T1562.001.description:
Kernel modules are pieces of code that can be loaded and unloaded into the kernel upon demand. They extend the
functionality of the kernel without the need to reboot the system. This rule identifies attempts to remove a kernel
module.

T1562.001.name: SELinux Configuration Creation or Renaming
T1562.001.description:
This rule detects the creation or renaming of the SELinux configuration file. SELinux is a security module that provides
access control security policies. Modifications to the SELinux configuration file may indicate an attempt to impair
defenses by disabling or modifying security tools.

T1562.001.name: SoftwareUpdate Preferences Modification
T1562.001.description:
Identifies changes to the SoftwareUpdate preferences using the built-in defaults command. Adversaries may abuse this in
an attempt to disable security updates.

T1562.001.name: Quarantine Attrib Removed by Unsigned or Untrusted Process
T1562.001.description:
Detects deletion of the quarantine attribute by an unusual process (xattr). In macOS, when applications or programs are
downloaded from the internet, there is a quarantine flag set on the file. This attribute is read by Apple's Gatekeeper
defense program at execution time. An adversary may disable t

T1562.001.name: Potential Privacy Control Bypass via TCCDB Modification
T1562.001.description:
Identifies the use of sqlite3 to directly modify the Transparency, Consent, and Control (TCC) SQLite database. This may
indicate an attempt to bypass macOS privacy controls, including access to sensitive resources like the system camera,
microphone, address book, and calendar.

T1562.001.name: Modification of Safari Settings via Defaults Command
T1562.001.description:
Identifies changes to the Safari configuration using the built-in defaults command. Adversaries may attempt to enable or
disable certain Safari settings, such as enabling JavaScript from Apple Events to ease in the hijacking of the users
browser.

T1562.001.name: Attempt to Unload Elastic Endpoint Security Kernel Extension
T1562.001.description: Identifies attempts to unload the Elastic Endpoint Security kernel extension via the kextunload command.

T1562.001.name: Suspicious Antimalware Scan Interface DLL
T1562.001.description:
Identifies the creation of the Antimalware Scan Interface (AMSI) DLL in an unusual location. This may indicate an
attempt to bypass AMSI by loading a rogue AMSI module instead of the legit one.

T1562.001.name: Potential Antimalware Scan Interface Bypass via PowerShell
T1562.001.description:
Identifies the execution of PowerShell script with keywords related to different Antimalware Scan Interface (AMSI)
bypasses. An adversary may attempt first to disable AMSI before executing further malicious powershell scripts to evade
detection.

T1562.001.name: Modification of AmsiEnable Registry Key
T1562.001.description:
Identifies modifications of the AmsiEnable registry key to 0, which disables the Antimalware Scan Interface (AMSI). An
adversary can modify this key to disable AMSI protections.

T1562.001.name: Windows Defender Disabled via Registry Modification
T1562.001.description:
Identifies modifications to the Windows Defender registry settings to disable the service or set the service to be
started manually.

T1562.001.name: Windows Defender Exclusions Added via PowerShell
T1562.001.description:
Identifies modifications to the Windows Defender configuration settings using PowerShell to add exclusions at the folder
directory or process level.

T1562.001.name: Disabling Windows Defender Security Settings via PowerShell
T1562.001.description: Identifies use of the Set-MpPreference PowerShell command to disable or weaken certain Windows Defender settings.

T1562.001.name: Disabling Lsa Protection via Registry Modification
T1562.001.description:
LSA protecton is provided to prevent nonprotected processes from reading memory and injecting code. This feature provides added security for the credentials that LSA stores and manages.
Adversaries may modify the RunAsPPL registry and wait or initiate a system restart to enable Lsass credentials acc

T1562.001.name: PowerShell Script with Windows Defender Tampering Capabilities
T1562.001.description:
Identifies PowerShell scripts containing cmdlets and parameters that attackers can abuse to disable Windows Defender
features. Attackers can tamper with antivirus to reduce the risk of detection when executing their payloads.

T1562.001.name: DNS Global Query Block List Modified or Disabled
T1562.001.description:
Identifies changes to the DNS Global Query Block List (GQBL), a security feature that prevents the resolution of certain
DNS names often exploited in attacks like WPAD spoofing. Attackers with certain privileges, such as DNSAdmins, can
modify or disable the GQBL, allowing exploitation of hosts runni

T1562.001.name: Scheduled Tasks AT Command Enabled
T1562.001.description:
Identifies attempts to enable the Windows scheduled tasks AT command via the registry. Attackers may use this method to
move laterally or persist locally. The AT command has been deprecated since Windows 8 and Windows Server 2012, but still
exists for backwards compatibility.

T1562.001.name: SolarWinds Process Disabling Services via Registry
T1562.001.description:
Identifies a SolarWinds binary modifying the start type of a service to be disabled. An adversary may abuse this
technique to manipulate relevant security services.

T1562.001.name: Potential Evasion via Filter Manager
T1562.001.description:
The Filter Manager Control Program (fltMC.exe) binary may be abused by adversaries to unload a filter driver and evade
defenses.

T1562.001.name: Disabling User Account Control via Registry Modification
T1562.001.description:
User Account Control (UAC) can help mitigate the impact of malware on Windows hosts. With UAC, apps and tasks always run
in the security context of a non-administrator account, unless an administrator specifically authorizes
administrator-level access to the system. This rule identifies registry val

T1562.001.name: AWS CloudTrail Log Deleted
T1562.001.description:
Detects deletion of an AWS CloudTrail trail via DeleteTrail API. Removing trails is a high-risk action that destroys an
audit control plane and is frequently paired with other destructive or stealthy operations. Validate immediately and
restore compliant logging.

T1562.001.name: AWS CloudTrail Log Suspended
T1562.001.description:
Detects Cloudtrail logging suspension via StopLogging API. Stopping CloudTrail eliminates forward audit visibility and
is a classic defense evasion step before sensitive changes or data theft. Investigate immediately and determine what
occurred during the logging gap.

T1562.001.name: AWS CloudWatch Alarm Deletion
T1562.001.description:
Detects the deletion of one or more Amazon CloudWatch alarms using the "DeleteAlarms" API. CloudWatch alarms are
critical for monitoring metrics and triggering alerts when thresholds are exceeded. An adversary may delete alarms to
impair visibility, silence alerts, and evade detection following mali

T1562.001.name: AWS Config Resource Deletion
T1562.001.description:
Identifies attempts to delete AWS Config resources. AWS Config provides continuous visibility into resource
configuration changes and compliance posture across an account. Deleting Config components can significantly reduce
security visibility and auditability. Adversaries may delete or disable Conf

T1562.001.name: AWS Configuration Recorder Stopped
T1562.001.description:
Identifies when an AWS Config configuration recorder is stopped. AWS Config recorders continuously track and record
configuration changes across supported AWS resources. Stopping the recorder immediately reduces visibility into
infrastructure changes and can be abused by adversaries to evade detecti

T1562.001.name: AWS GuardDuty Detector Deletion
T1562.001.description:
Detects the deletion of an Amazon GuardDuty detector. GuardDuty provides continuous monitoring for malicious or
unauthorized activity across AWS accounts. Deleting the detector disables this visibility, stopping all threat detection
and removing existing findings. Adversaries may delete GuardDuty de

T1562.001.name: AWS CloudWatch Log Group Deletion
T1562.001.description:
Detects the deletion of an Amazon CloudWatch Log Group using the "DeleteLogGroup" API. CloudWatch log groups store
operational and security logs for AWS services and custom applications. Deleting a log group permanently removes all
associated log streams and historical log data, which can eliminate

T1562.001.name: AWS CloudWatch Log Stream Deletion
T1562.001.description:
Detects the deletion of an Amazon CloudWatch log stream using the "DeleteLogStream" API. Deleting a log stream
permanently removes its associated log events and may disrupt security visibility, break audit trails, or suppress
forensic evidence. Adversaries may delete log streams to conceal malicious

T1562.001.name: Azure Diagnostic Settings Deleted
T1562.001.description:
Identifies the deletion of diagnostic settings in Azure, which send platform logs and metrics to different destinations.
An adversary may delete diagnostic settings in an attempt to evade defenses.

T1562.001.name: Azure Kubernetes Services (AKS) Kubernetes Events Deleted
T1562.001.description:
Identifies when events are deleted in Azure Kubernetes. Kubernetes events are objects that log any state changes.
Example events are a container creation, an image pull, or a pod scheduling on a node. An adversary may delete events in
Azure Kubernetes in an attempt to evade detection.

T1562.001.name: Azure VNet Network Watcher Deleted
T1562.001.description:
Identifies the deletion of a Network Watcher in Azure. Network Watchers are used to monitor, diagnose, view metrics, and
enable or disable logs for resources in an Azure virtual network. An adversary may delete a Network Watcher in an
attempt to evade defenses.

T1562.001.name: Azure Resource Group Deleted
T1562.001.description:
Identifies the deletion of a resource group in Azure, which includes all resources within the group. Deletion is
permanent and irreversible. An adversary may delete a resource group in an attempt to evade defenses or intentionally
destroy data.

T1562.001.name: GitHub Protected Branch Settings Changed
T1562.001.description:
This rule detects setting modifications for protected branches of a GitHub repository. Branch protection rules can be
used to enforce certain workflows or requirements before a contributor can push changes to a branch in your repository.
Changes to these protected branch settings should be investiga

T1562.001.name: Application Removed from Blocklist in Google Workspace
T1562.001.description:
Google Workspace administrators may be aware of malicious applications within the Google marketplace and block these
applications for user security purposes. An adversary, with administrative privileges, may remove this application from
the explicit block list to allow distribution of the applicatio

T1562.001.name: Google Workspace Bitlocker Setting Disabled
T1562.001.description:

T1562.001.description:
Detects when the Google Marketplace restrictions are changed to allow any application for users in Google Workspace.
Malicious APKs created by adversaries may be uploaded to the Google marketplace but not installed on devices managed
within Google Workspace. Administrators should set restrictions to

T1562.001.name: M365 Exchange Anti-Phish Policy Deleted
T1562.001.description:
Identifies the deletion of an anti-phishing policy in Microsoft 365. By default, Microsoft 365 includes built-in
features that help protect users from phishing attacks. Anti-phishing polices increase this protection by refining
settings to better detect and prevent attacks.

T1562.001.name: M365 Exchange Anti-Phish Rule Modification
T1562.001.description:
Identifies the modification of an anti-phishing rule in Microsoft 365. By default, Microsoft 365 includes built-in
features that help protect users from phishing attacks. Anti-phishing rules increase this protection by refining
settings to better detect and prevent attacks.

T1562.001.description:

receiving email system to validate that the messages were generated by

T1562.001.name: M365 Exchange Email Safe Link Policy Disabled
T1562.001.description:
Identifies when a Safe Link policy is disabled in Microsoft 365. Safe Link policies for Office applications extend
phishing protection to documents that contain hyperlinks, even after they have been delivered to a user.

T1562.description:
Kernel modules are pieces of code that can be loaded and unloaded into the kernel upon demand. They extend the
functionality of the kernel without the need to reboot the system. This rule identifies attempts to remove a kernel
module.

T1562.name: Kill Command Execution
T1562.description:
This rule detects the execution of kill, pkill, and killall commands on Linux systems. These commands are used to terminate
processes on a system. Attackers may use these commands to kill security tools or other processes to evade detection or
disrupt system operations.

T1562.name: SELinux Configuration Creation or Renaming
T1562.description:
This rule detects the creation or renaming of the SELinux configuration file. SELinux is a security module that provides
access control security policies. Modifications to the SELinux configuration file may indicate an attempt to impair
defenses by disabling or modifying security tools.

T1562.name: Suspicious Kernel Feature Activity
T1562.description:
This rule detects the modification and reading of kernel features through built-in commands. Attackers may collect
information, disable or weaken Linux kernel protections. For example, an attacker may modify ASLR protection by
disabling kernel.randomize_va_space, allow ptrace by setting kernel.yama.

T1562.name: SoftwareUpdate Preferences Modification
T1562.description:
Identifies changes to the SoftwareUpdate preferences using the built-in defaults command. Adversaries may abuse this in
an attempt to disable security updates.

T1562.name: Quarantine Attrib Removed by Unsigned or Untrusted Process
T1562.description:

downloaded from the internet, there is a quarantine flag set on the file. This attribute is read by Apple's Gatekeeper
defense program at execution time. An adversary may disable t

T1562.name: Potential Privacy Control Bypass via TCCDB Modification
T1562.description:
Identifies the use of sqlite3 to directly modify the Transparency, Consent, and Control (TCC) SQLite database. This may
indicate an attempt to bypass macOS privacy controls, including access to sensitive resources like the system camera,
microphone, address book, and calendar.

T1562.name: Modification of Safari Settings via Defaults Command
T1562.description:
Identifies changes to the Safari configuration using the built-in defaults command. Adversaries may attempt to enable or
disable certain Safari settings, such as enabling JavaScript from Apple Events to ease in the hijacking of the users
browser.

T1562.name: Attempt to Unload Elastic Endpoint Security Kernel Extension
T1562.description: Identifies attempts to unload the Elastic Endpoint Security kernel extension via the kextunload command.

T1562.name: Decline in host-based traffic
T1562.description:
A machine learning job has detected a sudden drop in host based traffic. This can be due to a range of security issues, such as a compromised system,
a failed service, or a network misconfiguration.

T1562.name: Kerberos Pre-authentication Disabled for User
T1562.description:
Identifies the modification of an account's Kerberos pre-authentication options. An adversary with
GenericWrite/GenericAll rights over the account can maliciously modify these settings to perform offline password
cracking attacks such as AS-REP roasting.

T1562.name: Suspicious Antimalware Scan Interface DLL
T1562.description:
Identifies the creation of the Antimalware Scan Interface (AMSI) DLL in an unusual location. This may indicate an
attempt to bypass AMSI by loading a rogue AMSI module instead of the legit one.

T1562.name: Potential Antimalware Scan Interface Bypass via PowerShell
T1562.description:
Identifies the execution of PowerShell script with keywords related to different Antimalware Scan Interface (AMSI)
bypasses. An adversary may attempt first to disable AMSI before executing further malicious powershell scripts to evade
detection.

T1562.name: Modification of AmsiEnable Registry Key
T1562.description:
Identifies modifications of the AmsiEnable registry key to 0, which disables the Antimalware Scan Interface (AMSI). An
adversary can modify this key to disable AMSI protections.

T1562.name: Sensitive Audit Policy Sub-Category Disabled
T1562.description:
Identifies attempts to disable auditing for some security sensitive audit policy sub-categories. This is often done by
attackers in an attempt to evade detection and forensics on a system.

T1562.name: Windows Defender Disabled via Registry Modification
T1562.description:
Identifies modifications to the Windows Defender registry settings to disable the service or set the service to be
started manually.

T1562.name: Windows Defender Exclusions Added via PowerShell
T1562.description:
Identifies modifications to the Windows Defender configuration settings using PowerShell to add exclusions at the folder
directory or process level.

T1562.name: Network-Level Authentication (NLA) Disabled
T1562.description:
Identifies the attempt to disable Network-Level Authentication (NLA) via registry modification. Network Level
Authentication (NLA) is a feature on Windows that provides an extra layer of security for Remote Desktop (RDP)
connections, as it requires users to authenticate before allowing a full RDP se

T1562.name: PowerShell Script Block Logging Disabled
T1562.description:
Identifies attempts to disable PowerShell Script Block Logging via registry modification. Attackers may disable this
logging to conceal their activities in the host and evade detection.

T1562.name: Disable Windows Firewall Rules via Netsh
T1562.description:
Identifies use of the netsh.exe to disable or weaken the local firewall. Attackers will use this command line tool to
disable the firewall during troubleshooting or to enable network mobility.

T1562.name: Disabling Windows Defender Security Settings via PowerShell
T1562.description: Identifies use of the Set-MpPreference PowerShell command to disable or weaken certain Windows Defender settings.

T1562.name: Disable Windows Event and Security Logs Using Built-in Tools
T1562.description:
Identifies attempts to disable EventLog via the logman Windows utility, PowerShell, or auditpol. This is often done by
attackers in an attempt to evade detection on a system.

T1562.name: DNS-over-HTTPS Enabled via Registry
T1562.description:
Identifies when a user enables DNS-over-HTTPS. This can be used to hide internet activity or the process of exfiltrating
data. With this enabled, an organization will lose visibility into data such as query type, response, and originating
IP, which are used to determine bad actors.

T1562.name: Remote Desktop Enabled in Windows Firewall by Netsh
T1562.description:
Identifies use of the network shell utility (netsh.exe) to enable inbound Remote Desktop Protocol (RDP) connections in
the Windows Firewall.

T1562.name: Enable Host Network Discovery via Netsh
T1562.description:
Identifies use of the netsh.exe program to enable host discovery via the network. Attackers can use this command-line
tool to weaken the host firewall settings.

T1562.name: IIS HTTP Logging Disabled
T1562.description:
Identifies when Internet Information Services (IIS) HTTP Logging is disabled on a server. An attacker with IIS server
access via a webshell or other mechanism can disable HTTP Logging as an effective anti-forensics measure.

T1562.name: Disabling Lsa Protection via Registry Modification
T1562.description:
LSA protecton is provided to prevent nonprotected processes from reading memory and injecting code. This feature provides added security for the credentials that LSA stores and manages.
Adversaries may modify the RunAsPPL registry and wait or initiate a system restart to enable Lsass credentials acc

T1562.name: Microsoft Windows Defender Tampering
T1562.description:
Identifies when one or more features on Microsoft Defender are disabled. Adversaries may disable or tamper with
Microsoft Defender features to evade detection and conceal malicious behavior.

T1562.name: Potential NetNTLMv1 Downgrade Attack
T1562.description:
Identifies registry modification to force the system to fall back to NTLMv1 for authentication. This modification is
possible with local administrator privileges and is commonly referred to as a `NetNTLMv1 downgrade attack`.

T1562.name: Local Account TokenFilter Policy Disabled
T1562.description:
Identifies registry modification to the LocalAccountTokenFilterPolicy policy. If this value exists (which doesn't by
default) and is set to 1, then remote connections from all local members of Administrators are granted full
high-integrity tokens during negotiation.

T1562.name: PowerShell Script with Windows Defender Tampering Capabilities
T1562.description:
Identifies PowerShell scripts containing cmdlets and parameters that attackers can abuse to disable Windows Defender
features. Attackers can tamper with antivirus to reduce the risk of detection when executing their payloads.

T1562.name: Windows Firewall Disabled via PowerShell
T1562.description:
Identifies when the Windows Firewall is disabled using PowerShell cmdlets, which can help attackers evade network
constraints, like internet and network lateral communication restrictions.

T1562.name: DNS Global Query Block List Modified or Disabled
T1562.description:
Identifies changes to the DNS Global Query Block List (GQBL), a security feature that prevents the resolution of certain
DNS names often exploited in attacks like WPAD spoofing. Attackers with certain privileges, such as DNSAdmins, can
modify or disable the GQBL, allowing exploitation of hosts runni

T1562.name: Potential RemoteMonologue Attack
T1562.description:
Identifies attempt to perform session hijack via COM object registry modification by setting the RunAs value to
Interactive User.

T1562.name: Scheduled Tasks AT Command Enabled
T1562.description:
Identifies attempts to enable the Windows scheduled tasks AT command via the registry. Attackers may use this method to
move laterally or persist locally. The AT command has been deprecated since Windows 8 and Windows Server 2012, but still
exists for backwards compatibility.

T1562.name: SolarWinds Process Disabling Services via Registry
T1562.description:
Identifies a SolarWinds binary modifying the start type of a service to be disabled. An adversary may abuse this
technique to manipulate relevant security services.

T1562.name: Potential Evasion via Filter Manager
T1562.description:
The Filter Manager Control Program (fltMC.exe) binary may be abused by adversaries to unload a filter driver and evade
defenses.

T1562.name: WDAC Policy File by an Unusual Process
T1562.description:
Identifies the creation of a Windows Defender Application Control (WDAC) policy file by an unusual process. Adversaries
may use a secially crafted WDAC policy to restrict the execution of security products.

T1562.name: Potential Evasion via Windows Filtering Platform
T1562.description:
Identifies multiple Windows Filtering Platform block events and where the process name is related to an endpoint
security software. Adversaries may add malicious WFP rules to prevent Endpoint security from sending telemetry.

in the security context of a non-administrator account, unless an administrator specifically authorizes
administrator-level access to the system. This rule identifies registry val

T1562.name: AWS CloudTrail Log Suspended
T1562.description:

is a classic defense evasion step before sensitive changes or data theft. Investigate immediately and determine what

T1562.name: AWS CloudWatch Alarm Deletion

critical for monitoring metrics and triggering alerts when thresholds are exceeded. An adversary may delete alarms to
impair visibility, silence alerts, and evade detection following mali

T1562.name: AWS Config Resource Deletion
T1562.description:
Identifies attempts to delete AWS Config resources. AWS Config provides continuous visibility into resource
configuration changes and compliance posture across an account. Deleting Config components can significantly reduce
security visibility and auditability. Adversaries may delete or disable Conf

T1562.name: AWS Configuration Recorder Stopped
T1562.description:
Identifies when an AWS Config configuration recorder is stopped. AWS Config recorders continuously track and record
configuration changes across supported AWS resources. Stopping the recorder immediately reduces visibility into
infrastructure changes and can be abused by adversaries to evade detecti

T1562.name: AWS VPC Flow Logs Deletion
T1562.description: Identifies the deletion of one or more flow logs in AWS Elastic Compute Cloud (EC2). An adversary may delete flow logs in an attempt to evade defenses.

T1562.name: AWS EC2 Network Access Control List Deletion
T1562.description: Identifies the deletion of an Amazon Elastic Compute Cloud (EC2) network access control list (ACL) or one of its ingress/egress entries.

T1562.name: Deprecated - AWS ElastiCache Security Group Created
T1562.description:
Identifies when an ElastiCache security group has been created. Amazon EC2-Classic and ElastiCache CacheSecurityGroups
have been retired. Modern ElastiCache deployments run in a VPC and use standard EC2 security groups instead. This rule
should be retained only for historical log analysis on legacy

T1562.name: Deprecated - AWS ElastiCache Security Group Modified or Deleted
T1562.description:
Identifies when an ElastiCache security group has been modified or deleted. Amazon EC2-Classic and ElastiCache
CacheSecurityGroups have been retired. Modern ElastiCache deployments run in a VPC and use standard EC2 security groups
instead. This rule should be retained only for historical log analysi

T1562.name: AWS GuardDuty Detector Deletion
T1562.description:
Detects the deletion of an Amazon GuardDuty detector. GuardDuty provides continuous monitoring for malicious or
unauthorized activity across AWS accounts. Deleting the detector disables this visibility, stopping all threat detection
and removing existing findings. Adversaries may delete GuardDuty de

T1562.name: AWS Route 53 Resolver Query Log Configuration Deleted
T1562.description:
Identifies the deletion of an Amazon Route 53 Resolver Query Log Configuration. Resolver query logs provide critical
visibility into DNS activity across VPCs, including lookups made by EC2 instances, containers, Lambda functions, and
other AWS resources. Deleting a query log configuration immediatel

T1562.name: AWS S3 Bucket Configuration Deletion
T1562.description:
Identifies the deletion of critical Amazon S3 bucket configurations such as bucket policies, lifecycle configurations
or encryption settings. These actions are typically administrative but may also represent
adversarial attempts to remove security controls, disable data retention mechanisms, or conc

T1562.name: AWS S3 Bucket Expiration Lifecycle Configuration Added
T1562.description:
Identifies the addition of an expiration lifecycle configuration to an Amazon S3 bucket. S3 lifecycle rules can
automatically delete or transition objects after a defined period. Adversaries can abuse them by configuring
auto-deletion of logs, forensic evidence, or sensitive objects to cover their t

T1562.name: AWS S3 Bucket Server Access Logging Disabled
T1562.description:
Identifies when server access logging is disabled for an Amazon S3 bucket. Server access logs provide a detailed record
of requests made to an S3 bucket. When server access logging is disabled for a bucket, it could indicate an adversary's
attempt to impair defenses by disabling logs that contain ev

T1562.name: AWS SQS Queue Purge
T1562.description:
Identifies when an AWS Simple Queue Service (SQS) queue is purged. Purging an SQS queue permanently deletes all messages
currently in the queue. Adversaries may use this action to disrupt application workflows, destroy operational data, or
impair monitoring and alerting by removing messages that con

T1562.name: Insecure AWS EC2 VPC Security Group Ingress Rule Added
T1562.description: Identifies when a specified inbound (ingress) rule is added or adjusted for a VPC security group in AWS EC2. This rule detects when a security group rule is added that allows traffic from any IP address or from a specific IP address to common remote access ports, such as 22 (SSH) or 3389 (RDP). Adve

T1562.name: AWS WAF Access Control List Deletion
T1562.description:
Identifies the deletion of an AWS Web Application Firewall (WAF) Web ACL. Web ACLs are the core enforcement objects in
AWS WAF, defining which traffic is inspected, allowed, or blocked for protected applications. Deleting a Web ACL removes
all associated rules, protections, and logging configuration

T1562.name: AWS WAF Rule or Rule Group Deletion
T1562.description:
Identifies the deletion of an AWS Web Application Firewall (WAF) rule or rule group. WAF rules and rule groups enforce
critical protections for web applications by filtering malicious HTTP requests, blocking known attack patterns, and
enforcing access controls. Deleting these rules—even briefly—can

T1562.name: AWS CloudWatch Log Group Deletion
T1562.description:
Detects the deletion of an Amazon CloudWatch Log Group using the "DeleteLogGroup" API. CloudWatch log groups store
operational and security logs for AWS services and custom applications. Deleting a log group permanently removes all
associated log streams and historical log data, which can eliminate

T1562.name: AWS CloudWatch Log Stream Deletion
T1562.description:
Detects the deletion of an Amazon CloudWatch log stream using the "DeleteLogStream" API. Deleting a log stream
permanently removes its associated log events and may disrupt security visibility, break audit trails, or suppress
forensic evidence. Adversaries may delete log streams to conceal malicious

T1562.name: AWS EC2 Network Access Control List Creation
T1562.description: Identifies the creation of an AWS EC2 network access control list (ACL) or an entry in a network ACL with a specified rule number. Adversaries may exploit ACLs to establish persistence or exfiltrate data by creating permissive rules.

T1562.name: AWS EC2 Security Group Configuration Change
T1562.description: Identifies a change to an AWS Security Group Configuration. A security group is like a virtual firewall, and modifying configurations may allow unauthorized access. Threat actors may abuse this to establish persistence, exfiltrate data, or pivot in an AWS environment.

T1562.name: Azure Event Hub Deleted
T1562.description:
Identifies an Event Hub deletion in Azure. An Event Hub is an event processing service that ingests and processes large
volumes of events and data. An adversary may delete an Event Hub in an attempt to evade detection.

T1562.name: Azure Diagnostic Settings Deleted
T1562.description:
Identifies the deletion of diagnostic settings in Azure, which send platform logs and metrics to different destinations.
An adversary may delete diagnostic settings in an attempt to evade defenses.

T1562.name: Azure Kubernetes Services (AKS) Kubernetes Events Deleted
T1562.description:
Identifies when events are deleted in Azure Kubernetes. Kubernetes events are objects that log any state changes.
Example events are a container creation, an image pull, or a pod scheduling on a node. An adversary may delete events in
Azure Kubernetes in an attempt to evade detection.

T1562.name: Azure VNet Firewall Policy Deleted
T1562.description:
Identifies the deletion of a firewall policy in Azure. An adversary may delete a firewall policy in an attempt to evade
defenses and/or to eliminate barriers to their objective.

T1562.name: Azure VNet Firewall Front Door WAF Policy Deleted
T1562.description:
Identifies the deletion of a Frontdoor Web Application Firewall (WAF) Policy in Azure. An adversary may delete a
Frontdoor Web Application Firewall (WAF) Policy in an attempt to evade defenses and/or to eliminate barriers to their
objective.

T1562.name: Azure VNet Network Watcher Deleted
T1562.description:
Identifies the deletion of a Network Watcher in Azure. Network Watchers are used to monitor, diagnose, view metrics, and
enable or disable logs for resources in an Azure virtual network. An adversary may delete a Network Watcher in an
attempt to evade defenses.

T1562.name: Azure Diagnostic Settings Alert Suppression Rule Created or Modified
T1562.description:
Identifies the creation of suppression rules in Azure. Suppression rules are a mechanism used to suppress alerts
previously identified as false positives or too noisy to be in production. This mechanism can be abused or mistakenly
configured, resulting in defense evasions and loss of security visibi

T1562.name: Azure Resource Group Deleted
T1562.description:
Identifies the deletion of a resource group in Azure, which includes all resources within the group. Deletion is
permanent and irreversible. An adversary may delete a resource group in an attempt to evade defenses or intentionally
destroy data.

T1562.name: GCP Firewall Rule Creation
T1562.description:
Identifies when a firewall rule is created in Google Cloud Platform (GCP) for Virtual Private Cloud (VPC) or App Engine.
These firewall rules can be configured to allow or deny connections to or from virtual machine (VM) instances or
specific applications. An adversary may create a new firewall rule

T1562.name: GCP Firewall Rule Deletion
T1562.description:
Identifies when a firewall rule is deleted in Google Cloud Platform (GCP) for Virtual Private Cloud (VPC) or App Engine.
These firewall rules can be configured to allow or deny connections to or from virtual machine (VM) instances or
specific applications. An adversary may delete a firewall rule in

T1562.name: GCP Firewall Rule Modification
T1562.description:
Identifies when a firewall rule is modified in Google Cloud Platform (GCP) for Virtual Private Cloud (VPC) or App
Engine. These firewall rules can be modified to allow or deny connections to or from virtual machine (VM) instances or
specific applications. An adversary may modify an existing firewall

T1562.name: GCP Logging Bucket Deletion
T1562.description:
Identifies a Logging bucket deletion in Google Cloud Platform (GCP). Log buckets are containers that store and organize
log data. A deleted bucket stays in a pending state for 7 days, and Logging continues to route logs to the bucket during
that time. To stop routing logs to a deleted bucket, you ca

T1562.name: GCP Logging Sink Deletion
T1562.description:
Identifies a Logging sink deletion in Google Cloud Platform (GCP). Every time a log entry arrives, Logging compares the
log entry to the sinks in that resource. Each sink whose filter matches the log entry writes a copy of the log entry to
the sink's export destination. An adversary may delete a Log

T1562.name: GCP Pub/Sub Subscription Deletion
T1562.description:
Identifies the deletion of a subscription in Google Cloud Platform (GCP). In GCP, the publisher-subscriber relationship
(Pub/Sub) is an asynchronous messaging service that decouples event-producing and event-processing services. A
subscription is a named resource representing the stream of messages

T1562.name: GCP Pub/Sub Topic Deletion
T1562.description:
Identifies the deletion of a topic in Google Cloud Platform (GCP). In GCP, the publisher-subscriber relationship
(Pub/Sub) is an asynchronous messaging service that decouples event-producing and event-processing services. A publisher
application creates and sends messages to a topic. Deleting a topi

T1562.name: GCP Virtual Private Cloud Network Deletion
T1562.description:
Identifies when a Virtual Private Cloud (VPC) network is deleted in Google Cloud Platform (GCP). A VPC network is a
virtual version of a physical network within a GCP project. Each VPC network has its own subnets, routes, and firewall,
as well as other elements. An adversary may delete a VPC network

T1562.name: GCP Virtual Private Cloud Route Creation
T1562.description:
Identifies when a virtual private cloud (VPC) route is created in Google Cloud Platform (GCP). Google Cloud routes
define the paths that network traffic takes from a virtual machine (VM) instance to other destinations. These
destinations can be inside a Google VPC network or outside it. An adversary

T1562.name: GCP Virtual Private Cloud Route Deletion
T1562.description:
Identifies when a Virtual Private Cloud (VPC) route is deleted in Google Cloud Platform (GCP). Google Cloud routes
define the paths that network traffic takes from a virtual machine (VM) instance to other destinations. These
destinations can be inside a Google VPC network or outside it. An adversary

T1562.name: GitHub Protected Branch Settings Changed
T1562.description:
This rule detects setting modifications for protected branches of a GitHub repository. Branch protection rules can be
used to enforce certain workflows or requirements before a contributor can push changes to a branch in your repository.
Changes to these protected branch settings should be investiga

T1562.name: Application Removed from Blocklist in Google Workspace
T1562.description:
Google Workspace administrators may be aware of malicious applications within the Google marketplace and block these
applications for user security purposes. An adversary, with administrative privileges, may remove this application from
the explicit block list to allow distribution of the applicatio

T1562.name: Domain Added to Google Workspace Trusted Domains
T1562.description:
Detects when a domain is added to the list of trusted Google Workspace domains. An adversary may add a trusted domain in
order to collect and exfiltrate data from their target’s organization with less restrictive security controls.

T1562.name: Google Workspace Bitlocker Setting Disabled
T1562.description:
Google Workspace administrators whom manage Windows devices and have Windows device management enabled may also enable
BitLocker drive encryption to mitigate unauthorized data access on lost or stolen computers. Adversaries with valid
account access may disable BitLocker to access sensitive data on

T1562.name: Google Workspace Restrictions for Marketplace Modified to Allow Any App
T1562.description:
Detects when the Google Marketplace restrictions are changed to allow any application for users in Google Workspace.
Malicious APKs created by adversaries may be uploaded to the Google marketplace but not installed on devices managed
within Google Workspace. Administrators should set restrictions to

T1562.name: M365 Exchange Anti-Phish Policy Deleted
T1562.description:
Identifies the deletion of an anti-phishing policy in Microsoft 365. By default, Microsoft 365 includes built-in
features that help protect users from phishing attacks. Anti-phishing polices increase this protection by refining
settings to better detect and prevent attacks.

T1562.name: M365 Exchange Anti-Phish Rule Modification
T1562.description:
Identifies the modification of an anti-phishing rule in Microsoft 365. By default, Microsoft 365 includes built-in
features that help protect users from phishing attacks. Anti-phishing rules increase this protection by refining
settings to better detect and prevent attacks.

T1562.name: M365 Exchange DKIM Signing Configuration Disabled
T1562.description:
Identifies when a DomainKeys Identified Mail (DKIM) signing configuration is disabled in Microsoft 365. With DKIM in
Microsoft 365, messages that are sent from Exchange Online will be cryptographically signed. This will allow the
receiving email system to validate that the messages were generated by

T1562.name: M365 Exchange DLP Policy Deleted
T1562.description:
Identifies when a Data Loss Prevention (DLP) policy is removed in Microsoft 365. An adversary may remove a DLP policy to
evade existing DLP monitoring.

T1562.name: M365 Exchange Email Safe Link Policy Disabled
T1562.description:
Identifies when a Safe Link policy is disabled in Microsoft 365. Safe Link policies for Office applications extend
phishing protection to documents that contain hyperlinks, even after they have been delivered to a user.

T1562.name: M365 Exchange Mailbox Audit Logging Bypass Added
T1562.description:
Detects the occurrence of mailbox audit bypass associations. The mailbox audit is responsible for logging specified
mailbox events (like accessing a folder or a message or permanently deleting a message). However, actions taken by some
authorized accounts, such as accounts used by third-party tools

T1562.name: M365 Exchange Malware Filter Policy Deleted
T1562.description:
Identifies when a malware filter policy has been deleted in Microsoft 365. A malware filter policy is used to alert
administrators that an internal user sent a message that contained malware. This may indicate an account or machine
compromise that would need to be investigated. Deletion of a malware

T1562.name: M365 Exchange Malware Filter Rule Modified
T1562.description:
Identifies when a malware filter rule has been deleted or disabled in Microsoft 365. An adversary or insider threat may
want to modify a malware filter rule to evade detection.

T1562.name: M365 Exchange Email Safe Attachment Rule Disabled
T1562.description:
Identifies when a safe attachment rule is disabled in Microsoft 365. Safe attachment rules can extend malware
protections to include routing all messages and attachments without a known malware signature to a special hypervisor
environment. An adversary or insider threat may disable a safe attachmen

T1562.name: M365 Teams Custom Application Interaction Enabled
T1562.description:
Identifies when custom applications are allowed in Microsoft Teams. If an organization requires applications other than
those available in the Teams app store, custom applications can be developed as packages and uploaded. An adversary may
abuse this behavior to establish persistence in an environme

T1562.name: M365 Teams External Access Enabled
T1562.description:
Identifies when external access is enabled in Microsoft Teams. External access lets Teams and Skype for Business users
communicate with other users that are outside their organization. An adversary may enable external access or add an
allowed domain to exfiltrate data or maintain persistence in an e

T1562.name: Attempt to Deactivate an Okta Network Zone
T1562.description:
Detects attempts to deactivate an Okta network zone. Okta network zones can be configured to limit or restrict access to
a network based on IP addresses or geolocations. An adversary may attempt to modify, delete, or deactivate an Okta
network zone in order to remove or weaken an organization's secu

T1562.name: Attempt to Delete an Okta Network Zone
T1562.description:
Detects attempts to delete an Okta network zone. Okta network zones can be configured to limit or restrict access to a
network based on IP addresses or geolocations. An adversary may attempt to modify, delete, or deactivate an Okta network
zone in order to remove or weaken an organization's security

T1562.name: Attempt to Deactivate an Okta Policy
T1562.description:
Detects attempts to deactivate an Okta policy. An adversary may attempt to deactivate an Okta policy in order to weaken
an organization's security controls. For example, an adversary may attempt to deactivate an Okta multi-factor
authentication (MFA) policy in order to weaken the authentication requ

T1562.name: Attempt to Deactivate an Okta Policy Rule
T1562.description:
Detects attempts to deactivate a rule within an Okta policy. An adversary may attempt to deactivate a rule within an
Okta policy in order to remove or weaken an organization's security controls.

T1562.name: Attempt to Delete an Okta Policy
T1562.description:
Detects attempts to delete an Okta policy. An adversary may attempt to delete an Okta policy in order to weaken an
organization's security controls. For example, an adversary may attempt to delete an Okta multi-factor authentication
(MFA) policy in order to weaken the authentication requirements for

T1562.name: Attempt to Delete an Okta Policy Rule
T1562.description:
Detects attempts to delete a rule within an Okta policy. An adversary may attempt to delete an Okta policy rule in order
to weaken an organization's security controls.

T1562.name: Attempt to Modify an Okta Network Zone
T1562.description:
Detects attempts to modify an Okta network zone. Okta network zones can be configured to limit or restrict access to a
network based on IP addresses or geolocations. An adversary may attempt to modify, delete, or deactivate an Okta network
zone in order to remove or weaken an organization's security

T1562.name: Attempt to Modify an Okta Policy
T1562.description:
Detects attempts to modify an Okta policy. An adversary may attempt to modify an Okta policy in order to weaken an
organization's security controls. For example, an adversary may attempt to modify an Okta multi-factor authentication
(MFA) policy in order to weaken the authentication requirements for

T1562.name: Attempt to Modify an Okta Policy Rule
T1562.description:
Detects attempts to modify a rule within an Okta policy. An adversary may attempt to modify an Okta policy rule in order
to weaken an organization's security controls.

T1140.name: Base64 Encoding/Decoding Activity
T1140.description: Adversaries may encode/decode data in an attempt to evade detection by host- or network-based security controls.

T1140.name: Hex Encoding/Decoding Activity
T1140.description: Adversaries may encode/decode data in an attempt to evade detection by host- or network-based security controls.

T1140.name: ROT Encoded Python Script Execution
T1140.description:
Identifies the execution of a Python script that uses the ROT cipher for letters substitution. Adversaries may
use this method to encode and obfuscate part of their malicious code in legit python packages.

T1140.name: Command Line Obfuscation via Whitespace Padding
T1140.description:
Identifies process execution events where the command line value contains a long sequence of whitespace characters or
multiple occurrences of contiguous whitespace. Attackers may attempt to evade signature-based detections by padding
their malicious command with unnecessary whitespace characters. Th

T1140.name: Base16 or Base32 Encoding/Decoding Activity
T1140.description: Adversaries may encode/decode data in an attempt to evade detection by host- or network-based security controls.

T1140.name: Unusual Base64 Encoding/Decoding Activity
T1140.description:
This rule leverages ESQL to detect unusual base64 encoding/decoding activity on Linux systems. Attackers may use base64
encoding/decoding to obfuscate data, such as command and control traffic or payloads, to evade detection by host- or
network-based security controls. ESQL rules have limited fields

T1140.name: Potential Hex Payload Execution via Command-Line
T1140.description:
This rule detects when a process executes a command line containing hexadecimal characters. Malware authors may use
hexadecimal encoding to obfuscate their payload and evade detection.

T1140.name: Potential Hex Payload Execution via Common Utility
T1140.description:
This rule detects potential hex payload execution on Linux systems. Adversaries may use hex encoding to obfuscate
payloads and evade detection mechanisms.

T1140.name: Base64 Decoded Payload Piped to Interpreter
T1140.description:
This rule detects when a base64 decoded payload is piped to an interpreter on Linux systems. Adversaries may use
base64 encoding to obfuscate data and pipe it to an interpreter to execute malicious code. This technique may
be used to evade detection by host- or network-based security controls.

T1140.name: Multi-Base64 Decoding Attempt from Suspicious Location
T1140.description:
This rule detects the execution of multiple base64 decoding commands to decode data. multi-decoded
data is suspicious, and may be used by attackers to obfuscate malicious payloads or commands.

T1140.name: Suspicious Content Extracted or Decompressed via Funzip
T1140.description:
Identifies when suspicious content is extracted from a file and subsequently decompressed using the funzip utility.
Malware may execute the tail utility using the "-c" option to read a sequence of bytes from the end of a file. The
output from tail can be piped to funzip in order to decompress malici

T1140.name: Encoded Executable Stored in the Registry
T1140.description:
Identifies registry write modifications to hide an encoded portable executable. This could be indicative of adversary
defense evasion by avoiding the storing of malicious content directly on disk.

T1140.name: PowerShell Suspicious Payload Encoded and Compressed
T1140.description:
Identifies the use of .NET functionality for decompression and base64 decoding combined in PowerShell scripts, which
malware and security tools heavily use to deobfuscate payloads and load them directly in memory to bypass defenses.

T1140.name: PowerShell Script with Encryption/Decryption Capabilities
T1140.description:
Identifies the use of Cmdlets and methods related to encryption/decryption of files in PowerShell scripts, which malware
and offensive security tools can abuse to encrypt data or decrypt payloads to bypass security solutions.

T1140.name: Potential PowerShell Obfuscated Script
T1140.description:
Identifies scripts that contain patterns and known methods that obfuscate PowerShell code. Attackers can use obfuscation
techniques to bypass PowerShell security protections such as Antimalware Scan Interface (AMSI).

T1140.name: Potential PowerShell Obfuscation via Invalid Escape Sequences
T1140.description:
Identifies PowerShell scripts that use invalid escape sequences as a form of obfuscation. This technique introduces
backticks (`) between characters in a way that does not correspond to valid PowerShell escape sequences, breaking up
strings and bypassing pattern-based detections while preserving exe

T1140.name: Potential PowerShell Obfuscation via Backtick-Escaped Variable Expansion
T1140.description:
Identifies PowerShell scripts that use backtick-escaped characters inside ${} variable expansion as a form of
obfuscation. These methods are designed to evade static analysis and bypass security protections such as the Antimalware
Scan Interface (AMSI).

T1140.name: Potential PowerShell Obfuscation via Character Array Reconstruction
T1140.description:
Identifies PowerShell scripts that use character arrays and runtime string reconstruction as a form of obfuscation. This
technique breaks strings into individual characters, often using constructs like char[] with index-based access or
joining logic. These methods are designed to evade static analys

T1140.name: Potential PowerShell Obfuscation via Concatenated Dynamic Command Invocation
T1140.description:
Identifies PowerShell scripts that use concatenated strings within dynamic command invocation (&() or .()) as a form of
obfuscation. These methods are designed to evade static analysis and bypass security protections such as the Antimalware
Scan Interface (AMSI).

T1140.name: Potential PowerShell Obfuscation via High Numeric Character Proportion
T1140.description:
Identifies PowerShell scripts with a disproportionately high number of numeric characters, often indicating the presence
of obfuscated or encoded payloads. This behavior is typical of obfuscation methods involving byte arrays, character code
manipulation, or embedded encoded strings used to deliver

T1140.name: Potential Dynamic IEX Reconstruction via Environment Variables
T1140.description:
Identifies PowerShell scripts that reconstruct the IEX (Invoke-Expression) command at runtime using indexed slices of
environment variables. This technique leverages character access and join operations to build execution logic
dynamically, bypassing static keyword detection and evading defenses suc

T1140.name: Dynamic IEX Reconstruction via Method String Access
T1140.description:
Identifies PowerShell scripts that reconstruct the IEX (Invoke-Expression) command by accessing and indexing the string
representation of method references. This obfuscation technique uses constructs like ''.IndexOf.ToString() to expose
method metadata as a string, then extracts specific characters

T1140.name: PowerShell Obfuscation via Negative Index String Reversal
T1140.description:
Identifies PowerShell scripts that use negative index ranges to reverse the contents of a string or array at runtime as
a form of obfuscation. This technique avoids direct use of reversal functions by iterating through array elements in
reverse order. These methods are designed to evade static analy

T1140.name: Potential PowerShell Obfuscation via Reverse Keywords
T1140.description:
Identifies PowerShell scripts that use reversed strings as a form of obfuscation. These methods are designed to evade
static analysis and bypass security protections such as the Antimalware Scan Interface (AMSI).

T1140.name: Potential PowerShell Obfuscation via String Concatenation
T1140.description:
Identifies PowerShell scripts that use string concatenation as a form of obfuscation. These methods are designed to
evade static analysis and bypass security protections such as the Antimalware Scan Interface (AMSI).

T1140.name: Potential PowerShell Obfuscation via String Reordering
T1140.description:
Identifies PowerShell scripts that use string reordering and runtime reconstruction techniques as a form of obfuscation.
These methods are designed to evade static analysis and bypass security protections such as the Antimalware Scan
Interface (AMSI).

T1140.name: Potential PowerShell Obfuscation via Special Character Overuse
T1140.description:
Identifies PowerShell scripts with an unusually high proportion of whitespace and special characters, often indicative
of obfuscation. This behavior is commonly associated with techniques such as SecureString encoding, formatting
obfuscation, or character-level manipulation designed to bypass static

T1140.name: Suspicious CertUtil Commands
T1140.description:
Identifies suspicious commands being used with certutil.exe. CertUtil is a native Windows component which is part of
Certificate Services. CertUtil is often abused by attackers to live off the land for stealthier command and control or
data exfiltration.

T1027.name: Base64 Encoding/Decoding Activity
T1027.description: Adversaries may encode/decode data in an attempt to evade detection by host- or network-based security controls.

T1027.name: Hex Encoding/Decoding Activity
T1027.description: Adversaries may encode/decode data in an attempt to evade detection by host- or network-based security controls.

T1027.description:
Detects when GenAI tools spawn compilers or packaging tools to generate executables. Attackers leverage local LLMs to

are particularly high-risk as they create standalone executabl

T1027.name: GenAI Process Performing Encoding/Chunking Prior to Network Activity
T1027.description:
Detects when GenAI processes perform encoding or chunking (base64, gzip, tar, zip) followed by outbound network
activity. This sequence indicates data preparation for exfiltration. Attackers encode or compress sensitive data before
transmission to obfuscate contents and evade detection. Legitimate G

T1027.description: Adversaries may encode/decode data in an attempt to evade detection by host- or network-based security controls.

T1027.description:
This rule leverages ESQL to detect unusual base64 encoding/decoding activity on Linux systems. Attackers may use base64

network-based security controls. ESQL rules have limited fields

T1027.name: Potential Hex Payload Execution via Command-Line
T1027.description:
This rule detects when a process executes a command line containing hexadecimal characters. Malware authors may use
hexadecimal encoding to obfuscate their payload and evade detection.

T1027.name: Potential Hex Payload Execution via Common Utility
T1027.description:
This rule detects potential hex payload execution on Linux systems. Adversaries may use hex encoding to obfuscate
payloads and evade detection mechanisms.

T1027.name: Base64 Decoded Payload Piped to Interpreter
T1027.description:
This rule detects when a base64 decoded payload is piped to an interpreter on Linux systems. Adversaries may use
base64 encoding to obfuscate data and pipe it to an interpreter to execute malicious code. This technique may
be used to evade detection by host- or network-based security controls.

T1027.name: Multi-Base64 Decoding Attempt from Suspicious Location
T1027.description:
This rule detects the execution of multiple base64 decoding commands to decode data. multi-decoded
data is suspicious, and may be used by attackers to obfuscate malicious payloads or commands.

T1027.name: Suspicious Content Extracted or Decompressed via Funzip
T1027.description:
Identifies when suspicious content is extracted from a file and subsequently decompressed using the funzip utility.
Malware may execute the tail utility using the "-c" option to read a sequence of bytes from the end of a file. The
output from tail can be piped to funzip in order to decompress malici

T1027.name: Suspicious .NET Code Compilation
T1027.description:
Identifies executions of .NET compilers with suspicious parent processes, which can indicate an attacker's attempt to
compile code after delivery in order to bypass security mechanisms.

T1027.name: Microsoft Build Engine Started an Unusual Process
T1027.description:
An instance of MSBuild, the Microsoft Build Engine, started a PowerShell script or the Visual C# Command Line Compiler.
This technique is sometimes used to deploy a malicious payload using the Build Engine.

T1027.name: Command Obfuscation via Unicode Modifier Letters
T1027.description:
Identifies the presence of unicode modifier letters in the process command_line. Adversaries sometimes replace ASCII characters
with visually similar Unicode modifier letters or combining marks to evade simple string-based detections.

T1027.name: PowerShell Suspicious Payload Encoded and Compressed
T1027.description:
Identifies the use of .NET functionality for decompression and base64 decoding combined in PowerShell scripts, which
malware and security tools heavily use to deobfuscate payloads and load them directly in memory to bypass defenses.

T1027.name: PowerShell Script with Encryption/Decryption Capabilities
T1027.description:
Identifies the use of Cmdlets and methods related to encryption/decryption of files in PowerShell scripts, which malware
and offensive security tools can abuse to encrypt data or decrypt payloads to bypass security solutions.

T1027.name: Potential PowerShell Obfuscated Script
T1027.description:
Identifies scripts that contain patterns and known methods that obfuscate PowerShell code. Attackers can use obfuscation
techniques to bypass PowerShell security protections such as Antimalware Scan Interface (AMSI).

T1027.name: Potential PowerShell Obfuscation via Invalid Escape Sequences
T1027.description:

backticks (`) between characters in a way that does not correspond to valid PowerShell escape sequences, breaking up
strings and bypassing pattern-based detections while preserving exe

T1027.name: Potential PowerShell Obfuscation via Backtick-Escaped Variable Expansion
T1027.description:
Identifies PowerShell scripts that use backtick-escaped characters inside ${} variable expansion as a form of
obfuscation. These methods are designed to evade static analysis and bypass security protections such as the Antimalware
Scan Interface (AMSI).

T1027.name: Potential PowerShell Obfuscation via Character Array Reconstruction
T1027.description:

technique breaks strings into individual characters, often using constructs like char[] with index-based access or
joining logic. These methods are designed to evade static analys

T1027.name: Potential PowerShell Obfuscation via Concatenated Dynamic Command Invocation
T1027.description:
Identifies PowerShell scripts that use concatenated strings within dynamic command invocation (&() or .()) as a form of
obfuscation. These methods are designed to evade static analysis and bypass security protections such as the Antimalware
Scan Interface (AMSI).

T1027.name: Potential PowerShell Obfuscation via High Numeric Character Proportion
T1027.description:
Identifies PowerShell scripts with a disproportionately high number of numeric characters, often indicating the presence
of obfuscated or encoded payloads. This behavior is typical of obfuscation methods involving byte arrays, character code

T1027.name: Potential Dynamic IEX Reconstruction via Environment Variables

T1027.name: PowerShell Obfuscation via Negative Index String Reversal
T1027.description:

a form of obfuscation. This technique avoids direct use of reversal functions by iterating through array elements in
reverse order. These methods are designed to evade static analy

T1027.name: Potential PowerShell Obfuscation via Reverse Keywords
T1027.description:
Identifies PowerShell scripts that use reversed strings as a form of obfuscation. These methods are designed to evade
static analysis and bypass security protections such as the Antimalware Scan Interface (AMSI).

T1027.name: Potential PowerShell Obfuscation via String Concatenation
T1027.description:
Identifies PowerShell scripts that use string concatenation as a form of obfuscation. These methods are designed to
evade static analysis and bypass security protections such as the Antimalware Scan Interface (AMSI).

T1027.name: Potential PowerShell Obfuscation via String Reordering
T1027.description:
Identifies PowerShell scripts that use string reordering and runtime reconstruction techniques as a form of obfuscation.
These methods are designed to evade static analysis and bypass security protections such as the Antimalware Scan
Interface (AMSI).

T1027.name: Potential PowerShell Obfuscation via Special Character Overuse
T1027.description:
Identifies PowerShell scripts with an unusually high proportion of whitespace and special characters, often indicative
of obfuscation. This behavior is commonly associated with techniques such as SecureString encoding, formatting
obfuscation, or character-level manipulation designed to bypass static

T1027.name: Suspicious HTML File Creation
T1027.description:
Identifies the execution of a browser process to open an HTML file with high entropy and size. Adversaries may smuggle
data and files past content filters by hiding malicious payloads inside of seemingly benign HTML files.

T1055.name: Suspicious Process from Conhost
T1055.description: Identifies a suspicious Conhost child process which may be an indication of code injection activity.

T1055.name: Privilege Escalation via GDB CAP_SYS_PTRACE
T1055.description:
Identifies instances where GDB (granted the CAP_SYS_PTRACE capability) is executed, after which the user's access is

T1055.name: Root Network Connection via GDB CAP_SYS_PTRACE
T1055.description:

connection is initiated by UID/GID 0 (root). In Linux, the CAP_SYS_PTRACE capability grants a process the ability to use
the ptrace system call, which is typically used for debugging a

T1055.name: Network Connection via Sudo Binary
T1055.description:

reverse shell shellcode is injected into a process run with elevated permissions via "sudo". Attackers may attempt to
inject shellcode into processes running as root, to escalate pr

T1055.name: Potential Sudo Token Manipulation via Process Injection
T1055.description:

manipulation attack is performed by injecting into a process that h

T1055.name: Unusual Linux Network Activity
T1055.description:
Identifies Linux processes that do not usually use the network but have unexpected network activity, which can indicate
command-and-control, lateral movement, persistence, or data exfiltration activity. A process with unusual network
activity can denote process exploitation or injection, where the p

T1055.name: Unusual Windows Network Activity
T1055.description:
Identifies Windows processes that do not usually use the network but have unexpected network activity, which can
indicate command-and-control, lateral movement, persistence, or data exfiltration activity. A process with unusual
network activity can denote process exploitation or injection, where the

T1055.name: Process Injection - Detected - Elastic Endgame
T1055.description:
Elastic Endgame detected Process Injection. Click the Elastic Endgame icon in the event.module column or the link in the
rule.reference column for additional information.

T1055.name: Process Injection - Prevented - Elastic Endgame
T1055.description:
Elastic Endgame prevented Process Injection. Click the Elastic Endgame icon in the event.module column or the link in
the rule.reference column for additional information.

T1055.name: Suspicious Communication App Child Process
T1055.description:
Identifies suspicious child processes of communications apps, which can indicate a potential masquerading as the
communication app or the exploitation of a vulnerability on the application causing it to execute code.

T1055.name: Process Injection by the Microsoft Build Engine
T1055.description:
An instance of MSBuild, the Microsoft Build Engine, created a thread in another process. This technique is sometimes
used to evade detection or elevate privileges.

T1055.name: Suspicious .NET Reflection via PowerShell
T1055.description:
Detects the use of Reflection.Assembly to load PEs and DLLs in memory in PowerShell scripts. Attackers use this method
to load executables and DLLs without writing to the disk, bypassing security solutions.

T1055.name: Potential Process Injection via PowerShell
T1055.description:
Detects the use of Windows API functions that are commonly abused by malware and security tools to load malicious code
or inject it into remote processes.

T1055.name: Suspicious Managed Code Hosting Process
T1055.description:
Identifies a suspicious managed code hosting process which could indicate code injection or other form of suspicious
code execution.

T1055.name: Suspicious Process Access via Direct System Call
T1055.description:
Identifies suspicious process access events from an unknown memory region. Endpoint security solutions usually hook
userland Windows APIs in order to decide if the code that is being executed is malicious or not. It's possible to bypass
hooked functions by writing malicious functions that call sysca

T1055.name: Suspicious Process Creation CallTrace
T1055.description:
Identifies when a process is created and immediately accessed from an unknown memory code region and by the same parent
process. This may indicate a code injection attempt.

T1055.name: Suspicious Zoom Child Process
T1055.description:
A suspicious Zoom child process was detected, which may indicate an attempt to run unnoticed. Verify process details
such as command line, network connections, file writes and associated file signature details as well.

T1055.name: Unusual Child Process from a System Virtual Process
T1055.description: Identifies a suspicious child process of the Windows virtual system process, which could indicate code injection.

T1055.name: Suspicious Portable Executable Encoded in Powershell Script
T1055.description:
Detects the presence of a portable executable (PE) in a PowerShell script by looking for its encoded header. Attackers
embed PEs into PowerShell scripts to inject them into memory, avoiding defences by not writing to disk.

T1055.name: Conhost Spawned By Suspicious Parent Process
T1055.description:
Detects when the Console Window Host (conhost.exe) process is spawned by a suspicious parent process, which could be
indicative of code injection.

T1055.name: Unusual Parent-Child Relationship
T1055.description:
Identifies Windows programs run from unexpected parent processes. This could indicate masquerading or other strange
activity on a system.

T1055.name: Unusual Service Host Child Process - Childless Service
T1055.description:
Identifies unusual child processes of Service Host (svchost.exe) that traditionally do not spawn any child processes.
This may indicate a code injection or an equivalent form of exploitation.

T1055.name: Memory Threat - Detected - Elastic Defend
T1055.description:

memory signature detections only, and does not include prevent

T1055.name: Memory Threat - Prevented- Elastic Defend
T1055.description:

allows you to immediately begin investigating your Endpoint memory signature alerts. This rule identifies Elastic Defend
