T1059.name: Tampering with RUNNER_TRACKING_ID in GitHub Actions Runners
T1059.description:
This rule detects processes spawned by GitHub Actions runners where "RUNNER_TRACKING_ID" is overridden from its
default "github_*" value. Such tampering has been associated with attempts to evade runner tracking/cleanup on
self-hosted runners, including behavior observed in the Shai-Hulud 2.0 npm wo

T1059.name: Web Server Potential Command Injection Request
T1059.description:
This rule detects potential command injection attempts via web server requests by identifying URLs that contain
suspicious patterns commonly associated with command execution payloads. Attackers may exploit vulnerabilities in web
applications to inject and execute arbitrary commands on the server, o

T1059.name: Unusual Base64 Encoding/Decoding Activity
T1059.description:
This rule leverages ESQL to detect unusual base64 encoding/decoding activity on Linux systems. Attackers may use base64
encoding/decoding to obfuscate data, such as command and control traffic or payloads, to evade detection by host- or
network-based security controls. ESQL rules have limited fields

T1059.name: Proxy Shell Execution via Busybox
T1059.description:
Detects the execution of a shell through Busybox. Attackers may use this technique to execute shells while attempting to
evade detection.

T1059.name: Curl or Wget Egress Network Connection via LoLBin
T1059.description:
This rule detects the execution of curl or wget binaries through a GTFOBin (living-off-the-land) technique in Linux
environments. Attackers may exploit these utilities to download and execute malicious files from the internet while
attempting to evade detection. The rule specifically targets binarie

T1059.name: Potential Hex Payload Execution via Command-Line
T1059.description:
This rule detects when a process executes a command line containing hexadecimal characters. Malware authors may use
hexadecimal encoding to obfuscate their payload and evade detection.

T1059.name: Potential Hex Payload Execution via Common Utility
T1059.description:
This rule detects potential hex payload execution on Linux systems. Adversaries may use hex encoding to obfuscate
payloads and evade detection mechanisms.

T1059.name: Base64 Decoded Payload Piped to Interpreter
T1059.description:
This rule detects when a base64 decoded payload is piped to an interpreter on Linux systems. Adversaries may use
base64 encoding to obfuscate data and pipe it to an interpreter to execute malicious code. This technique may
be used to evade detection by host- or network-based security controls.

T1059.name: Kill Command Execution
T1059.description:
This rule detects the execution of kill, pkill, and killall commands on Linux systems. These commands are used to terminate
processes on a system. Attackers may use these commands to kill security tools or other processes to evade detection or
disrupt system operations.

T1059.name: Dynamic Linker (ld.so) Creation
T1059.description:
This rule detects the creation of the dynamic linker (ld.so). The dynamic linker is used to load shared libraries
needed by an executable. Attackers may attempt to replace the dynamic linker with a malicious version to execute
arbitrary code.

T1059.name: Multi-Base64 Decoding Attempt from Suspicious Location
T1059.description:
This rule detects the execution of multiple base64 decoding commands to decode data. multi-decoded
data is suspicious, and may be used by attackers to obfuscate malicious payloads or commands.

T1059.name: File Creation in /var/log via Suspicious Process
T1059.description:
This rule detects the creation of files in the /var/log/ directory via process executables located in world-writeable
locations or via hidden processes. Attackers may attempt to hide their activities by creating files in the /var/log/
directory, which is commonly used for logging system events.

T1059.name: Egress Connection from Entrypoint in Container
T1059.description:
This rule identifies a sequence of events where a process named `entrypoint.sh` is started in a container, followed by a
network connection attempt. This sequence indicates a potential egress connection from an entrypoint in a container. An
entrypoint is a command or script specified in the Dockerfi

T1059.name: Process Started with Executable Stack
T1059.description:
This rule monitors the syslog log file for messages related to instances of processes that are started with an executable
stack. This can be an indicator of a process that is attempting to execute code from the stack, which can be a security risk.

T1059.name: File Creation, Execution and Self-Deletion in Suspicious Directory
T1059.description:
This rule monitors for the creation of a file, followed by its execution and self-deletion in a short timespan within a
directory often used for malicious purposes by threat actors. This behavior is often used by malware to execute
malicious code and delete itself to hide its tracks.

T1059.name: File Made Executable via Chmod Inside A Container
T1059.description:
This rule detects when chmod or chown are used to add the execute permission to a file inside a container. Modifying file
permissions to make a file executable could indicate malicious activity, as an attacker may attempt to run unauthorized
or malicious code inside the container.

T1059.name: File Transfer or Listener Established via Netcat
T1059.description:
A netcat process is engaging in network activity on a Linux host. Netcat is often used as a persistence mechanism by
exporting a reverse shell or by serving a shell on a listening port. Netcat is also sometimes used for data
exfiltration.

T1059.name: Potential Upgrade of Non-interactive Shell
T1059.description:
Identifies when a non-interactive terminal (tty) is being upgraded to a fully interactive shell. Attackers may upgrade a
simple reverse shell to a fully interactive tty after obtaining initial access to a host, in order to obtain a more
stable connection.

T1059.name: Kubernetes Direct API Request via Curl or Wget
T1059.description:
This rule monitors for the execution of curl or wget commands that directly access Kubernetes API endpoints,
which may indicate an attempt to interact with Kubernetes resources in a potentially unauthorized manner. This
technique is often used by adversaries to gather information about the Kubernete

T1059.name: Netcat Listener Established via rlwrap
T1059.description:
Monitors for the execution of a netcat listener via rlwrap. rlwrap is a 'readline wrapper', a small utility that uses
the GNU Readline library to allow the editing of keyboard input for any command. This utility can be used in conjunction
with netcat to gain a more stable reverse shell.

T1059.name: Network Connection from Binary with RWX Memory Region
T1059.description:
Monitors for the execution of a unix binary with read, write and execute memory region permissions, followed by a
network connection. The mprotect() system call is used to change the access protections on a region of memory that has
already been allocated. This syscall allows a process to modify the

T1059.name: Network Connection via Recently Compiled Executable
T1059.description:
This rule monitors a sequence involving a program compilation event followed by its execution and a subsequent network
connection event. This behavior can indicate the set up of a reverse tcp connection to a command-and-control server.
Attackers may spawn reverse shells to establish persistence onto

T1059.name: Interactive Terminal Spawned via Perl
T1059.description:
Identifies when a terminal (tty) is spawned via Perl. Attackers may upgrade a simple reverse shell to a fully
interactive tty after obtaining initial access to a host.

T1059.name: Privileged Docker Container Creation
T1059.description:
This rule leverages the new_terms rule type to identify the creation of a potentially unsafe docker container from an
unusual parent process. Attackers can use the `--privileged` flag to create containers with escalated privileges, which
can lead to trivial privilege escalation, docker escaping and

T1059.name: Process Backgrounded by Unusual Parent
T1059.description:
This rule identifies processes that are backgrounded by an unusual parent process. This behavior may indicate a process
attempting to evade detection by hiding its parent process.

T1059.name: Process Started from Process ID (PID) File
T1059.description:
Identifies a new process starting from a process ID (PID), lock or reboot file within the temporary file storage
paradigm (tmpfs) directory /var/run directory. On Linux, the PID files typically hold the process ID to track previous
copies running and manage other tasks. Certain Linux malware use the

T1059.name: Binary Executed from Shared Memory Directory
T1059.description:
Identifies the execution of a binary by root in Linux shared memory directories: (/dev/shm/, /run/shm/, /var/run/,
/var/lock/). This activity is to be considered highly abnormal and should be investigated. Threat actors have placed
executables used for persistence on high-uptime servers in these dir

T1059.name: Interactive Terminal Spawned via Python
T1059.description:
Identifies when a terminal (tty) is spawned via Python. Attackers may upgrade a simple reverse shell to a fully
interactive tty after obtaining initial access to a host.

T1059.name: Web Server Spawned via Python
T1059.description:
This rule identifies when a web server is spawned via Python. Attackers may use Python to spawn a web server to
exfiltrate/infiltrate data or to move laterally within a network.

T1059.name: Potential Code Execution via Postgresql
T1059.description:
This rule monitors for suspicious activities that may indicate an attacker attempting to execute arbitrary code within a
PostgreSQL environment. Attackers can execute code via PostgreSQL as a result of gaining unauthorized access to a public
facing PostgreSQL database or exploiting vulnerabilities,

T1059.name: Linux Restricted Shell Breakout via Linux Binary(s)
T1059.description:
Identifies the abuse of a Linux binary to break out of a restricted shell or environment by spawning an interactive
system shell. The activity of spawning a shell from a binary is not common behavior for a user or system administrator,
and may indicate an attempt to evade detection, increase capabil

T1059.name: Openssl Client or Server Activity
T1059.description:
This rule identifies when the openssl client or server is used to establish a connection. Attackers may use openssl to
establish a secure connection to a remote server or to create a secure server to receive connections. This activity may
be used to exfiltrate data or establish a command and control

T1059.name: Potential Reverse Shell via Background Process
T1059.description:
Monitors for the execution of background processes with process arguments capable of opening a socket in the /dev/tcp
channel. This may indicate the creation of a backdoor reverse connection, and should be investigated further.

T1059.name: Potential Reverse Shell via Child
T1059.description:
This detection rule identifies suspicious network traffic patterns associated with TCP reverse shell activity. This
activity consists of a network event that is followed by the creation of a shell process with suspicious command line
arguments. An attacker may establish a Linux TCP reverse shell to

T1059.name: Potential Reverse Shell via Java
T1059.description:
This detection rule identifies the execution of a Linux shell process from a Java JAR application post an incoming
network connection. This behavior may indicate reverse shell activity via a Java application.

T1059.name: Potential Reverse Shell via Suspicious Child Process
T1059.description:
This detection rule detects the creation of a shell through a suspicious process chain. Any reverse shells spawned by
the specified utilities that are initialized from a single process followed by a network connection attempt will be
captured through this rule. Attackers may spawn reverse shells to

T1059.name: Potential Meterpreter Reverse Shell
T1059.description:
This detection rule identifies a sample of suspicious Linux system file reads used for system fingerprinting, leveraged
by the Metasploit Meterpreter shell to gather information about the target that it is executing its shell on. Detecting
this pattern is indicative of a successful meterpreter shell

T1059.name: Potential Reverse Shell via Suspicious Binary
T1059.description:
This detection rule detects the creation of a shell through a chain consisting of the execution of a suspicious binary
(located in a commonly abused location or executed manually) followed by a network event and ending with a shell being
spawned. Stageless reverse tcp shells display this behaviour.

T1059.name: Potential Reverse Shell
T1059.description:
This detection rule identifies suspicious network traffic patterns associated with TCP reverse shell activity. This
activity consists of a parent-child relationship where a network event is followed by the creation of a shell process.
An attacker may establish a Linux TCP reverse shell to gain remot

T1059.name: Potential Reverse Shell via UDP
T1059.description:
This detection rule identifies suspicious network traffic patterns associated with UDP reverse shell activity. This
activity consists of a sample of an execve, socket and connect syscall executed by the same process, where the
auditd.data.a0-1 indicate a UDP connection, ending with an egress connect

T1059.name: Suspicious Content Extracted or Decompressed via Funzip
T1059.description:
Identifies when suspicious content is extracted from a file and subsequently decompressed using the funzip utility.
Malware may execute the tail utility using the "-c" option to read a sequence of bytes from the end of a file. The
output from tail can be piped to funzip in order to decompress malici

T1059.name: Suspicious System Commands Executed by Previously Unknown Executable
T1059.description:
This rule monitors for the execution of several commonly used system commands executed by a previously unknown
executable located in commonly abused directories. An alert from this rule can indicate the presence of potentially
malicious activity, such as the execution of unauthorized or suspicious p

T1059.name: Suspicious Mining Process Creation Event
T1059.description:
Identifies service creation events of common mining services, possibly indicating the infection of a system with a
cryptominer.

T1059.name: Suspicious Named Pipe Creation
T1059.description:
This rule detects the creation of unusually labeled named pipes (FIFOs) by the mkfifo command, which is often used by
attackers to establish persistence on a target system or to execute commands in the background. Through the new_terms
rule type, this rule can identify uncommon process command lines

T1059.name: Pod or Container Creation with Suspicious Command-Line
T1059.description:
This rule detects the creation of pods or containers that execute suspicious commands often associated with persistence or
privilege escalation techniques. Attackers may use container orchestration tools like kubectl or container runtimes like
docker to create pods or containers that run shell comma

T1059.name: System Binary Path File Permission Modification
T1059.description:
This rule identifies file permission modification events on files located in common system binary paths. Adversaries may attempt to
hide their payloads in the default Linux system directories, and modify the file permissions of these payloads prior to execution.

T1059.name: BPF filter applied using TC
T1059.description:
Detects when the tc (transmission control) binary is utilized to set a BPF (Berkeley Packet Filter) on a network
interface. Tc is used to configure Traffic Control in the Linux kernel. It can shape, schedule, police and drop traffic.
A threat actor can utilize tc to set a bpf filter on an interface

T1059.name: Unknown Execution of Binary with RWX Memory Region
T1059.description:
Monitors for the execution of a previously unknown unix binary with read, write and execute memory region permissions.
The mprotect() system call is used to change the access protections on a region of memory that has already been
allocated. This syscall allows a process to modify the permissions of

T1059.name: Unusual Interactive Process Launched in a Container
T1059.description:
This rule detects when an unusual interactive process is launched inside a container. Interactive processes are typically
run in the foreground and require user input, which is unusual behavior for a containerized environment. This activity
could indicate an attacker attempting to gain access to the

T1059.name: Unusual Execution from Kernel Thread (kthreadd) Parent
T1059.description:
This rule detects suspicious child process from the kernel thread (kthreadd) parent process. Attackers may execute payloads from
kernel space via kthreadd to perform actions on the host and evade detection. Through the usage of the new_terms rule type, this
rule can identify uncommon child processes

T1059.name: Suspicious Path Invocation from Command Line
T1059.description:
This rule detects the execution of a PATH variable in a command line invocation by a shell process. This behavior
is unusual and may indicate an attempt to execute a command from a non-standard location. This technique may be
used to evade detection or perform unauthorized actions on the system.

T1059.name: Unusual Pkexec Execution
T1059.description:
This rule detects the execution of the `pkexec` command by a shell process. The `pkexec` command is used to execute
programs as another user, typically as the superuser. Through the `new_terms` rule type, unusual executions of `pkexec`
are identified, and may indicate an attempt to escalate privileg

T1059.name: Memory Swap Modification
T1059.description:
This rule detects memory swap modification events on Linux systems. Memory swap modification can be used to manipulate
the system's memory and potentially impact the system's performance. This behavior is commonly observed in malware that
deploys miner software such as XMRig.

T1059.name: Potential Malware-Driven SSH Brute Force Attempt
T1059.description:
This detection identifies a Linux host that has potentially been infected with malware and is being used to conduct
brute-force attacks against external systems over SSH (port 22 and common alternative SSH ports). The detection looks
for a high volume of outbound connection attempts to non-private I

T1059.name: Suspicious APT Package Manager Execution
T1059.description:
Detects suspicious process events executed by the APT package manager, potentially indicating persistence through an APT
backdoor. In Linux, APT (Advanced Package Tool) is a command-line utility used for handling packages on Debian-based
systems, providing functions for installing, updating, upgradi

T1059.name: Boot File Copy
T1059.description:
This rule detects the process of copying or moving files from or to the `/boot` directory on Linux systems. The `/boot`
directory contains files that are essential for the system to boot, such as the kernel and initramfs images. Attackers
may copy or move files to the `/boot` directory to modify the

T1059.name: Unusual D-Bus Daemon Child Process
T1059.description:
This rule detects when an unusual child process is spawned from the `dbus-daemon` parent process. The `dbus-daemon`
process is a message bus system that provides a way for applications to talk to each other. Attackers may abuse this
process to execute malicious code or escalate privileges.

T1059.name: Dracut Module Creation
T1059.description:
This rule detects the creation of Dracut module files on Linux systems. Dracut is a tool used to generate an initramfs
image that is used to boot the system. Dracut modules are scripts that are executed during the initramfs image
generation process. Attackers may create malicious Dracut modules to e

T1059.name: Git Hook Command Execution
T1059.description:
This rule detects the execution of a potentially malicious process from a Git hook. Git hooks are scripts that Git
executes before or after events such as: commit, push, and receive. An attacker can abuse Git hooks to execute arbitrary
commands on the system and establish persistence.

T1059.name: Git Hook Created or Modified
T1059.description:
This rule detects the creation or modification of a Git hook file on a Linux system. Git hooks are scripts that Git
executes before or after events such as commit, push, and receive. They are used to automate tasks, enforce policies,
and customize Git's behavior. Attackers can abuse Git hooks to mai

T1059.name: Git Hook Egress Network Connection
T1059.description:
This rule detects a suspicious egress network connection attempt from a Git hook script. Git hooks are scripts that Git
executes before or after events such as: commit, push, and receive. An attacker can abuse these features to execute
arbitrary commands on the system, establish persistence or to in

T1059.name: Git Hook Child Process
T1059.description:
This rule detects child processes spawned by Git hooks. Git hooks are scripts that Git executes before or after events
such as commit, push, and receive. The rule identifies child processes spawned by Git hooks that are not typically
spawned by the Git process itself. This behavior may indicate an a

T1059.name: Manual Dracut Execution
T1059.description:
This rule detects manual execution of the `dracut` command on Linux systems. Dracut is a tool used to generate an
initramfs image that is used to boot the system. Attackers may use `dracut` to create a custom initramfs image that
includes malicious code or backdoors, allowing them to maintain persis

T1059.name: NetworkManager Dispatcher Script Creation
T1059.description:
This rule detects the creation of a NetworkManager dispatcher script on a Linux system. NetworkManager dispatcher
scripts are shell scripts that NetworkManager executes when network interfaces change state. Attackers can abuse
NetworkManager dispatcher scripts to maintain persistence on a system by

T1059.name: Python Path File (pth) Creation
T1059.description:
This rule detects the creation of .pth files in system-wide and user-specific Python package
directories, which can be abused for persistent code execution. .pth files automatically
execute Python code when the interpreter starts, making them a stealthy persistence mechanism.
Monitoring these paths

T1059.name: Simple HTTP Web Server Connection
T1059.description:
This rule detects connections accepted by a simple HTTP web server in Python and PHP built-in modules. Adversaries may
create simple HTTP web servers to establish persistence on a compromised system by uploading a reverse or command shell
payload to the server web root, allowing them to regain remot

T1059.name: Simple HTTP Web Server Creation
T1059.description:
This rule detects the creation of a simple HTTP web server using PHP or Python built-in modules. Adversaries may create
simple HTTP web servers to establish persistence on a compromised system by uploading a reverse or command shell payload
to the server web root, allowing them to regain remote acce

T1059.name: Python Site or User Customize File Creation
T1059.description:
This rule detects the creation and modification of sitecustomize.py and usercustomize.py, which
Python automatically executes on startup. Attackers can exploit these files for persistence by
injecting malicious code. The rule monitors system-wide, user-specific, and virtual environment
locations to

T1059.name: Initramfs Unpacking via unmkinitramfs
T1059.description:
This rule detects the unpacking of an initramfs image using the `unmkinitramfs` command on Linux systems. The
`unmkinitramfs` command is used to extract the contents of an initramfs image, which is used to boot the system.
Attackers may use `unmkinitramfs` to unpack an initramfs image and modify its

T1059.name: Unusual Process Spawned from Web Server Parent
T1059.description:
This rule detects unusual processes spawned from a web server parent process by identifying low frequency counts of
process spawning activity. Unusual process spawning activity may indicate an attacker attempting to establish
persistence, execute malicious commands, or establish command and control

T1059.name: Unusual Command Execution from Web Server Parent
T1059.description:
This rule detects potential command execution from a web server parent process on a Linux host. Adversaries may attempt
to execute commands from a web server parent process to blend in with normal web server activity and evade detection.
This behavior is commonly observed in web shell attacks where

T1059.name: Uncommon Destination Port Connection by Web Server
T1059.description:
This rule identifies unusual destination port network activity originating from a web server process. The
rule is designed to detect potential web shell activity or unauthorized communication from a web server
process to external systems.

T1059.name: Unusual Web Server Command Execution
T1059.description:
This rule leverages the "new_terms" rule type to detect unusual command executions originating from web server processes on Linux systems.
Attackers may exploit web servers to maintain persistence on a compromised system, often resulting in atypical command executions. As
command execution from web

T1059.name: Root Network Connection via GDB CAP_SYS_PTRACE
T1059.description:
Identifies instances where GDB (granted the CAP_SYS_PTRACE capability) is executed, after which an outbound network
connection is initiated by UID/GID 0 (root). In Linux, the CAP_SYS_PTRACE capability grants a process the ability to use
the ptrace system call, which is typically used for debugging a

T1059.name: Potential Shell via Wildcard Injection Detected
T1059.description:
This rule monitors for the execution of a set of linux binaries, that are potentially vulnerable to wildcard injection,
with suspicious command line flags followed by a shell spawn event. Linux wildcard injection is a type of security
vulnerability where attackers manipulate commands or input contai

T1059.name: Execution via Electron Child Process Node.js Module
T1059.description:
Identifies attempts to execute a child process from within the context of an Electron application using the
child_process Node.js module. Adversaries may abuse this technique to inherit permissions from parent processes.

T1059.name: Suspicious Installer Package Spawns Network Event
T1059.description:
Detects the execution of a MacOS installer package with an abnormal child process (e.g bash) followed immediately by a
network connection via a suspicious process (e.g curl). Threat actors will build and distribute malicious MacOS
installer packages, which have a .pkg extension, many times imitating

T1059.name: Suspicious Automator Workflows Execution
T1059.description:
Identifies the execution of the Automator Workflows process followed by a network connection from it's XPC service.
Adversaries may drop a custom workflow template that hosts malicious JavaScript for Automation (JXA) code as an
alternative to using osascript.

T1059.name: Apple Script Execution followed by Network Connection
T1059.description:
Detects execution via the Apple script interpreter (osascript) followed by a network connection from the same process
within a short time period. Adversaries may use malicious scripts for execution and command and control.

T1059.name: Shell Execution via Apple Scripting
T1059.description:
Identifies the execution of the shell process (sh) via scripting (JXA or AppleScript). Adversaries may use the
doShellScript functionality in JXA or do shell script in AppleScript to execute system commands.

T1059.name: Creation of Hidden Login Item via Apple Script
T1059.description:
Identifies the execution of osascript to create a hidden login item. This may indicate an attempt to persist a malicious
program while concealing its presence.

T1059.name: Persistence via Folder Action Script
T1059.description:
Detects modification of a Folder Action script. A Folder Action script is executed when the folder to which it is
attached has items added or removed, or when its window is opened, closed, moved, or resized. Adversaries may abuse this
feature to establish persistence by utilizing a malicious script.

T1059.name: Apple Scripting Execution with Administrator Privileges
T1059.description:
Identifies execution of the Apple script interpreter (osascript) without a password prompt and with administrator
privileges.

T1059.name: Execution with Explicit Credentials via Scripting
T1059.description:
Identifies execution of the security_authtrampoline process via a scripting interpreter. This occurs when programs use
AuthorizationExecute-WithPrivileges from the Security.framework to run another program with root privileges. It should
not be run by itself, as this is a sign of execution with expl

T1059.name: Suspicious Powershell Script
T1059.description:
A machine learning job detected a PowerShell script with unusual data characteristics, such as obfuscation, that may be
a characteristic of malicious PowerShell script text blocks.

T1059.name: React2Shell (CVE-2025-55182) Exploitation Attempt
T1059.description:
This rule detects exploitation attempts targeting CVE-2025-55182, a critical remote code execution vulnerability in
React Server Components (RSC) Flight protocol. The vulnerability allows attackers to execute arbitrary code on the
server by sending specially crafted deserialization payloads that exp

T1059.name: React2Shell Network Security Alert
T1059.description:
This rule identifies network security alerts related to CVE-2025-55182 exploitation attempts from different network security
integrations. CVE-2025-55182 is a critical remote code execution vulnerability in React Server Components (RSC) Flight protocol.
The vulnerability allows attackers to execute

T1059.name: Exporting Exchange Mailbox via PowerShell
T1059.description:
Identifies the use of the Exchange PowerShell cmdlet, New-MailBoxExportRequest, to export the contents of a primary
mailbox or archive to a .pst file. Adversaries may target user email to collect sensitive information.

T1059.name: PowerShell Suspicious Script with Audio Capture Capabilities
T1059.description: Detects PowerShell scripts that can record audio, a common feature in popular post-exploitation tooling.

T1059.name: PowerShell Suspicious Script with Clipboard Retrieval Capabilities
T1059.description:
Detects PowerShell scripts that can get the contents of the clipboard, which attackers can abuse to retrieve sensitive
information like credentials, messages, etc.

T1059.name: PowerShell Keylogging Script
T1059.description:
Detects the use of Win32 API Functions that can be used to capture user keystrokes in PowerShell scripts. Attackers use
this technique to capture user input, looking for credentials and/or other valuable data.

T1059.name: PowerShell Mailbox Collection Script
T1059.description:
Detects PowerShell scripts that can be used to collect data from mailboxes. Adversaries may target user email to collect
sensitive information.

T1059.name: PowerShell Suspicious Script with Screenshot Capabilities
T1059.description:
Detects PowerShell scripts that can take screenshots, which is a common feature in post-exploitation kits and remote
access tools (RATs).

T1059.name: PowerShell Script with Webcam Video Capture Capabilities
T1059.description:
Detects PowerShell scripts that can be used to record webcam video. Attackers can capture this information to extort or
spy on victims.

T1059.name: Remote File Download via PowerShell
T1059.description: Identifies powershell.exe being used to download an executable file from an untrusted remote destination.

T1059.name: Remote File Download via Script Interpreter
T1059.description:
Identifies built-in Windows script interpreters (cscript.exe or wscript.exe) being used to download an executable file
from a remote destination.

T1059.name: PowerShell Invoke-NinjaCopy script
T1059.description:
Detects PowerShell scripts that contain the default exported functions used on Invoke-NinjaCopy. Attackers can use
Invoke-NinjaCopy to read SYSTEM files that are normally locked, such as the NTDS.dit file or registry hives.

T1059.name: PowerShell Kerberos Ticket Dump
T1059.description:
Detects PowerShell scripts that have the capability of dumping Kerberos tickets from LSA, which potentially indicates an
attacker's attempt to acquire credentials for lateral movement.

T1059.name: PowerShell MiniDump Script
T1059.description:
This rule detects PowerShell scripts capable of dumping process memory using WindowsErrorReporting or Dbghelp.dll
MiniDumpWriteDump. Attackers can use this tooling to dump LSASS and get access to credentials.

T1059.name: Potential PowerShell Pass-the-Hash/Relay Script
T1059.description:
Detects PowerShell scripts that can execute pass-the-hash (PtH) attacks, intercept and relay NTLM challenges, and carry
out other man-in-the-middle (MitM) attacks.

T1059.name: PowerShell Kerberos Ticket Request
T1059.description:
Detects PowerShell scripts that have the capability of requesting kerberos tickets, which is a common step in
Kerberoasting toolkits to crack service accounts.

T1059.name: PowerShell Script with Veeam Credential Access Capabilities
T1059.description:
Identifies PowerShell scripts that can access and decrypt Veeam credentials stored in MSSQL databases. Attackers can use
Veeam Credentials to target backups as part of destructive operations such as Ransomware attacks.

T1059.name: Veeam Backup Library Loaded by Unusual Process
T1059.description:
Identifies potential credential decrypt operations by PowerShell or unsigned processes using the Veeam.Backup.Common.dll
library. Attackers can use Veeam Credentials to target backups as part of destructive operations such as Ransomware
attacks.

T1059.name: Potential Veeam Credential Access Command
T1059.description:
Identifies commands that can access and decrypt Veeam credentials stored in MSSQL databases. Attackers can use Veeam
Credentials to target backups as part of destructive operations such as Ransomware attacks.

T1059.name: Potential Antimalware Scan Interface Bypass via PowerShell
T1059.description:
Identifies the execution of PowerShell script with keywords related to different Antimalware Scan Interface (AMSI)
bypasses. An adversary may attempt first to disable AMSI before executing further malicious powershell scripts to evade
detection.

T1059.name: Clearing Windows Console History
T1059.description:
Identifies when a user attempts to clear console history. An adversary may clear the command history of a compromised
account to conceal the actions undertaken during an intrusion.

T1059.name: Windows Defender Exclusions Added via PowerShell
T1059.description:
Identifies modifications to the Windows Defender configuration settings using PowerShell to add exclusions at the folder
directory or process level.

T1059.name: Disabling Windows Defender Security Settings via PowerShell
T1059.description: Identifies use of the Set-MpPreference PowerShell command to disable or weaken certain Windows Defender settings.

T1059.name: Suspicious .NET Code Compilation
T1059.description:
Identifies executions of .NET compilers with suspicious parent processes, which can indicate an attacker's attempt to
compile code after delivery in order to bypass security mechanisms.

T1059.name: Microsoft Build Engine Started by a Script Process
T1059.description:
An instance of MSBuild, the Microsoft Build Engine, was started by a script or the Windows command interpreter. This
behavior is unusual and is sometimes used by malicious payloads.

T1059.name: Suspicious .NET Reflection via PowerShell
T1059.description:
Detects the use of Reflection.Assembly to load PEs and DLLs in memory in PowerShell scripts. Attackers use this method
to load executables and DLLs without writing to the disk, bypassing security solutions.

T1059.name: PowerShell Suspicious Payload Encoded and Compressed
T1059.description:
Identifies the use of .NET functionality for decompression and base64 decoding combined in PowerShell scripts, which
malware and security tools heavily use to deobfuscate payloads and load them directly in memory to bypass defenses.

T1059.name: PowerShell Script with Windows Defender Tampering Capabilities
T1059.description:
Identifies PowerShell scripts containing cmdlets and parameters that attackers can abuse to disable Windows Defender
features. Attackers can tamper with antivirus to reduce the risk of detection when executing their payloads.

T1059.name: Potential PowerShell Obfuscated Script
T1059.description:
Identifies scripts that contain patterns and known methods that obfuscate PowerShell code. Attackers can use obfuscation
techniques to bypass PowerShell security protections such as Antimalware Scan Interface (AMSI).

T1059.name: Potential PowerShell Obfuscation via Invalid Escape Sequences
T1059.description:
Identifies PowerShell scripts that use invalid escape sequences as a form of obfuscation. This technique introduces
backticks (`) between characters in a way that does not correspond to valid PowerShell escape sequences, breaking up
strings and bypassing pattern-based detections while preserving exe

T1059.name: Potential PowerShell Obfuscation via Backtick-Escaped Variable Expansion
T1059.description:
Identifies PowerShell scripts that use backtick-escaped characters inside ${} variable expansion as a form of
obfuscation. These methods are designed to evade static analysis and bypass security protections such as the Antimalware
Scan Interface (AMSI).

T1059.name: Potential PowerShell Obfuscation via Character Array Reconstruction
T1059.description:
Identifies PowerShell scripts that use character arrays and runtime string reconstruction as a form of obfuscation. This
technique breaks strings into individual characters, often using constructs like char[] with index-based access or
joining logic. These methods are designed to evade static analys

T1059.name: Potential PowerShell Obfuscation via Concatenated Dynamic Command Invocation
T1059.description:
Identifies PowerShell scripts that use concatenated strings within dynamic command invocation (&() or .()) as a form of
obfuscation. These methods are designed to evade static analysis and bypass security protections such as the Antimalware
Scan Interface (AMSI).

T1059.name: Potential PowerShell Obfuscation via High Numeric Character Proportion
T1059.description:
Identifies PowerShell scripts with a disproportionately high number of numeric characters, often indicating the presence
of obfuscated or encoded payloads. This behavior is typical of obfuscation methods involving byte arrays, character code
manipulation, or embedded encoded strings used to deliver

T1059.name: Potential Dynamic IEX Reconstruction via Environment Variables
T1059.description:
Identifies PowerShell scripts that reconstruct the IEX (Invoke-Expression) command at runtime using indexed slices of
environment variables. This technique leverages character access and join operations to build execution logic
dynamically, bypassing static keyword detection and evading defenses suc

T1059.name: Dynamic IEX Reconstruction via Method String Access
T1059.description:
Identifies PowerShell scripts that reconstruct the IEX (Invoke-Expression) command by accessing and indexing the string
representation of method references. This obfuscation technique uses constructs like ''.IndexOf.ToString() to expose
method metadata as a string, then extracts specific characters

T1059.name: PowerShell Obfuscation via Negative Index String Reversal
T1059.description:
Identifies PowerShell scripts that use negative index ranges to reverse the contents of a string or array at runtime as
a form of obfuscation. This technique avoids direct use of reversal functions by iterating through array elements in
reverse order. These methods are designed to evade static analy

T1059.name: Potential PowerShell Obfuscation via Reverse Keywords
T1059.description:
Identifies PowerShell scripts that use reversed strings as a form of obfuscation. These methods are designed to evade
static analysis and bypass security protections such as the Antimalware Scan Interface (AMSI).

T1059.name: Potential PowerShell Obfuscation via String Concatenation
T1059.description:
Identifies PowerShell scripts that use string concatenation as a form of obfuscation. These methods are designed to
evade static analysis and bypass security protections such as the Antimalware Scan Interface (AMSI).

T1059.name: Potential PowerShell Obfuscation via String Reordering
T1059.description:
Identifies PowerShell scripts that use string reordering and runtime reconstruction techniques as a form of obfuscation.
These methods are designed to evade static analysis and bypass security protections such as the Antimalware Scan
Interface (AMSI).

T1059.name: Potential PowerShell Obfuscation via Special Character Overuse
T1059.description:
Identifies PowerShell scripts with an unusually high proportion of whitespace and special characters, often indicative
of obfuscation. This behavior is commonly associated with techniques such as SecureString encoding, formatting
obfuscation, or character-level manipulation designed to bypass static

T1059.name: Potential Process Injection via PowerShell
T1059.description:
Detects the use of Windows API functions that are commonly abused by malware and security tools to load malicious code
or inject it into remote processes.

T1059.name: Windows Firewall Disabled via PowerShell
T1059.description:
Identifies when the Windows Firewall is disabled using PowerShell cmdlets, which can help attackers evade network
constraints, like internet and network lateral communication restrictions.

T1059.name: Suspicious Execution from a Mounted Device
T1059.description:
Identifies when a script interpreter or signed binary is launched via a non-standard working directory. An attacker may
use this technique to evade defenses.

T1059.name: Suspicious Execution via Windows Subsystem for Linux
T1059.description:
Detects Linux Bash commands from the the Windows Subsystem for Linux. Adversaries may enable and use WSL for Linux to
avoid detection.

T1059.name: PowerShell Share Enumeration Script
T1059.description:
Detects scripts that contain PowerShell functions, structures, or Windows API functions related to windows share
enumeration activities. Attackers, mainly ransomware groups, commonly identify and inspect network shares, looking for
critical information for encryption and/or exfiltration.

T1059.name: PowerShell Suspicious Discovery Related Windows API Functions
T1059.description:
This rule detects the use of discovery-related Windows API functions in PowerShell Scripts. Attackers can use these
functions to perform various situational awareness related activities, like enumerating users, shares, sessions, domain
trusts, groups, etc.

T1059.name: Command Execution via SolarWinds Process
T1059.description: A suspicious SolarWinds child process (Cmd.exe or Powershell.exe) was detected.

T1059.name: Command Prompt Network Connection
T1059.description:
Identifies cmd.exe making a network connection. Adversaries could abuse cmd.exe to download or execute malware from a
remote URL.

T1059.name: Svchost spawning Cmd
T1059.description: Identifies a suspicious parent child process relationship with cmd.exe descending from svchost.exe

T1059.name: Unusual Parent Process for cmd.exe
T1059.description: Identifies a suspicious parent child process relationship with cmd.exe descending from an unusual process.

T1059.name: Command Shell Activity Started via RunDLL32
T1059.description: Identifies command shell activity started via RunDLL32, which is commonly abused by attackers to host malicious code.

T1059.name: Delayed Execution via Ping
T1059.description:
Identifies the execution of commonly abused Windows utilities via a delayed Ping execution. This behavior is often
observed during malware installation and is consistent with an attacker attempting to evade detection.

T1059.name: Execution from Unusual Directory - Command Line
T1059.description:
Identifies process execution from suspicious default Windows directories. This may be abused by adversaries to hide
malware in trusted paths.

T1059.name: Suspicious Execution with NodeJS
T1059.description: Identifies suspicious execution patterns using NodeJS interpeter like process path and arguments.

T1059.name: Potential PowerShell HackTool Script by Author
T1059.description:
Detects known PowerShell offensive tooling author's name in PowerShell scripts. Attackers commonly use out-of-the-box
offensive tools without modifying the code, which may still contain the author artifacts. This rule identifies common
author handles found in popular PowerShell scripts used for red

T1059.name: Potential PowerShell HackTool Script by Function Names
T1059.description:
Detects known PowerShell offensive tooling functions names in PowerShell scripts. Attackers commonly use out-of-the-box
offensive tools without modifying the code. This rule aim is to take advantage of that.

T1059.name: Potential Malicious PowerShell Based on Alert Correlation
T1059.description: Identifies PowerShell script blocks associated with multiple distinct detections, indicating likely malicious behavior.

T1059.name: Suspicious Portable Executable Encoded in Powershell Script
T1059.description:
Detects the presence of a portable executable (PE) in a PowerShell script by looking for its encoded header. Attackers
embed PEs into PowerShell scripts to inject them into memory, avoiding defences by not writing to disk.

T1059.name: PowerShell PSReflect Script
T1059.description:
Detects the use of PSReflect in PowerShell scripts. Attackers leverage PSReflect as a library that enables PowerShell to
access win32 API functions.

T1059.name: Command and Scripting Interpreter via Windows Scripts
T1059.description: Identifies PowerShell.exe or Cmd.exe execution spawning from Windows Script Host processes Wscript.exe.

T1059.name: Potential Command Shell via NetCat
T1059.description:
Identifies potential attempt to execute via a reverse shell using the netcat utility to execute Windows commands using
the default interpreters like Cmd.exe and Powershell.

T1059.name: Outbound Scheduled Task Activity via PowerShell
T1059.description:
Identifies the PowerShell process loading the Task Scheduler COM DLL followed by an outbound RPC network connection
within a short time period. This may indicate lateral movement or remote discovery via scheduled tasks.

T1059.name: Windows Script Execution from Archive
T1059.description:
Identifies attempts to execute Jscript/Vbscript files from an archive file. The use of archives is a common delivery method
of malicious scripts.

T1059.name: Suspicious Cmd Execution via WMI
T1059.description:
Identifies suspicious command execution (cmd) via Windows Management Instrumentation (WMI) on a remote host. This could
be indicative of adversary lateral movement.

T1059.name: Suspicious PowerShell Engine ImageLoad
T1059.description:
Identifies the PowerShell engine being invoked by unexpected processes. Rather than executing PowerShell functionality
with powershell.exe, some attackers do this to operate more stealthily.

T1059.name: Conhost Spawned By Suspicious Parent Process
T1059.description:
Detects when the Console Window Host (conhost.exe) process is spawned by a suspicious parent process, which could be
indicative of code injection.

T1059.name: Microsoft Management Console File from Unusual Path
T1059.description:
Identifies attempts to open a Microsoft Management Console File from untrusted paths. Adversaries may use MSC files for
initial access and execution.

T1059.name: Suspicious Windows Command Shell Arguments
T1059.description:
Identifies the execution of the Windows Command Shell process (cmd.exe) with suspicious argument values. This behavior
is often observed during malware installation.

T1059.name: Potential Fake CAPTCHA Phishing Attack
T1059.description:
Identifies potential fake CAPTCHA phishing attack based on PowerShell or Cmd argument values. Adversaries employ this
technique via compromised websites with browser injects, posing either as fake CAPTCHAs to access the site or as a page
loading error requiring a fix to display the page. The victim

T1059.name: Potential Execution via FileFix Phishing Attack
T1059.description:
Identifies the execution of Windows commands or downloaded files via the browser's dialog box. Adversaries may use phishing
to instruct the victim to copy and paste malicious commands for execution via crafted phsihing web pages.

T1059.name: Suspicious Windows Powershell Arguments
T1059.description:
Identifies the execution of PowerShell with suspicious argument values. This behavior is often observed during malware
installation leveraging PowerShell.

T1059.name: Execution of a Downloaded Windows Script
T1059.description:
Identifies the creation of a Windows script downloaded from the internet followed by the execution of a scripting utility.
Adversaries may use Windows script files for initial access and execution.

T1059.name: Volume Shadow Copy Deletion via PowerShell
T1059.description:
Identifies the use of the Win32_ShadowCopy class and related cmdlets to achieve shadow copy deletion. This commonly
occurs in tandem with ransomware or other destructive attacks.

T1059.name: Suspicious JetBrains TeamCity Child Process
T1059.description:
Identifies suspicious processes being spawned by the JetBrain TeamCity process. This activity could be related to
JetBrains remote code execution vulnerabilities.

T1059.name: Windows Script Executing PowerShell
T1059.description:
Identifies a PowerShell process launched by either cscript.exe or wscript.exe. Observing Windows scripting processes
executing a PowerShell script, may be indicative of malicious activity.

T1059.name: Windows Script Interpreter Executing Process via WMI
T1059.description:
Identifies use of the built-in Windows script interpreters (cscript.exe or wscript.exe) being used to execute a process
via Windows Management Instrumentation (WMI). This may be indicative of malicious activity.

T1059.name: Microsoft Exchange Worker Spawning Suspicious Processes
T1059.description:
Identifies suspicious processes being spawned by the Microsoft Exchange Server worker process (w3wp). This activity may
indicate exploitation activity or access to an existing web shell backdoor.

T1059.name: Suspicious MS Office Child Process
T1059.description:
Identifies suspicious child processes of frequently targeted Microsoft Office applications (Word, PowerPoint, Excel).
These child processes are often launched during exploitation of Office applications or from documents with malicious
macros.

T1059.name: Suspicious MS Outlook Child Process
T1059.description:
Identifies suspicious child processes of Microsoft Outlook. These child processes are often associated with spear
phishing activity.

T1059.name: Windows Server Update Service Spawning Suspicious Processes
T1059.description:
Identifies suspicious processes being spawned by the Windows Server Update Service. This activity may indicate exploitation
activity or access to an existing web shell backdoor.

T1059.name: Suspicious Explorer Child Process
T1059.description:
Identifies a suspicious Windows explorer child process. Explorer.exe can be abused to launch malicious scripts or
executables from a trusted parent process.

T1059.name: ScreenConnect Server Spawning Suspicious Processes
T1059.description:
Identifies suspicious processes being spawned by the ScreenConnect server process (ScreenConnect.Service.exe). This
activity may indicate exploitation activity or access to an existing web shell backdoor.

T1059.name: Incoming Execution via PowerShell Remoting
T1059.description:
Identifies remote execution via Windows PowerShell remoting. Windows PowerShell remoting allows a user to run any
Windows PowerShell command on one or more remote computers. This could be an indication of lateral movement.

T1059.name: Scheduled Task Created by a Windows Script
T1059.description:
A scheduled task was created by a Windows script via cscript.exe, wscript.exe or powershell.exe. This can be abused by
an adversary to establish persistence.

T1059.name: New ActiveSyncAllowedDeviceID Added via PowerShell
T1059.description:
Identifies the use of the Exchange PowerShell cmdlet, Set-CASMailbox, to add a new ActiveSync allowed device.
Adversaries may target user email to collect sensitive information.

T1059.name: System Shells via Services
T1059.description:
Windows services typically run as SYSTEM and can be used as a privilege escalation opportunity. Malware or penetration
testers may run a shell as a service to gain SYSTEM permissions.

T1059.name: Execution via MSSQL xp_cmdshell Stored Procedure
T1059.description:
Identifies execution via MSSQL xp_cmdshell stored procedure. Malicious users may attempt to elevate their privileges by
using xp_cmdshell, which is disabled by default, thus, it's important to review the context of it's use.

T1059.name: Web Shell Detection: Script Process Child of Common Web Processes
T1059.description: Identifies suspicious commands executed via a web server, which may suggest a vulnerability and remote shell access.

T1059.name: PowerShell Script with Token Impersonation Capabilities
T1059.description:
Detects scripts that contain PowerShell functions, structures, or Windows API functions related to token
impersonation/theft. Attackers may duplicate then impersonate another user's token to escalate privileges and bypass
access controls.

T1059.name: Service Control Spawned via Script Interpreter
T1059.description:
Identifies Service Control (sc.exe) spawning from script interpreter processes to create, modify, or start services.
This can potentially indicate an attempt to elevate privileges or maintain persistence.

T1059.name: Entra ID PowerShell Sign-in
T1059.description:
Identifies a sign-in using the Azure Active Directory PowerShell module. PowerShell for Azure Active Directory allows
for managing settings from the command line, which is intended for users who are members of an admin role.

T1059.name: GitHub Actions Unusual Bot Push to Repository
T1059.description:
Detects when the github-actions[bot] pushes code to a repository where it has not performed this behavior before in a certain time window. This may
indicate a supply chain attack where malicious code running in a CI workflow attempts to modify repository contents,
such as injecting backdoor workflow

T1059.name: GitHub Actions Workflow Modification Blocked
T1059.description:
Detects when a GitHub Actions workflow attempts to create or modify workflow files in a protected branch but is blocked
due to insufficient permissions. This behavior is indicative of a supply chain attack where a malicious package or
compromised CI/CD pipeline attempts to inject persistent backdoor

T1059.001.name: PowerShell spawning Cmd
T1059.001.description: Identifies a suspicious parent child process relationship with cmd.exe descending from PowerShell.exe.

T1059.001.name: Command Line Obfuscation via Whitespace Padding
T1059.001.description:
Identifies process execution events where the command line value contains a long sequence of whitespace characters or
multiple occurrences of contiguous whitespace. Attackers may attempt to evade signature-based detections by padding
their malicious command with unnecessary whitespace characters. Th

T1059.001.name: Suspicious Powershell Script
T1059.001.description:
A machine learning job detected a PowerShell script with unusual data characteristics, such as obfuscation, that may be
a characteristic of malicious PowerShell script text blocks.

T1059.001.name: Exporting Exchange Mailbox via PowerShell
T1059.001.description:
Identifies the use of the Exchange PowerShell cmdlet, New-MailBoxExportRequest, to export the contents of a primary
mailbox or archive to a .pst file. Adversaries may target user email to collect sensitive information.

T1059.001.name: PowerShell Suspicious Script with Audio Capture Capabilities
T1059.001.description: Detects PowerShell scripts that can record audio, a common feature in popular post-exploitation tooling.

T1059.001.name: PowerShell Suspicious Script with Clipboard Retrieval Capabilities
T1059.001.description:
Detects PowerShell scripts that can get the contents of the clipboard, which attackers can abuse to retrieve sensitive
information like credentials, messages, etc.

T1059.001.name: PowerShell Keylogging Script
T1059.001.description:
Detects the use of Win32 API Functions that can be used to capture user keystrokes in PowerShell scripts. Attackers use
this technique to capture user input, looking for credentials and/or other valuable data.

T1059.001.name: PowerShell Mailbox Collection Script
T1059.001.description:
Detects PowerShell scripts that can be used to collect data from mailboxes. Adversaries may target user email to collect
sensitive information.

T1059.001.name: PowerShell Suspicious Script with Screenshot Capabilities
T1059.001.description:
Detects PowerShell scripts that can take screenshots, which is a common feature in post-exploitation kits and remote
access tools (RATs).

T1059.001.name: PowerShell Script with Webcam Video Capture Capabilities
T1059.001.description:
Detects PowerShell scripts that can be used to record webcam video. Attackers can capture this information to extort or
spy on victims.

T1059.001.name: Remote File Download via PowerShell
T1059.001.description: Identifies powershell.exe being used to download an executable file from an untrusted remote destination.

T1059.001.name: PowerShell Invoke-NinjaCopy script
T1059.001.description:
Detects PowerShell scripts that contain the default exported functions used on Invoke-NinjaCopy. Attackers can use
Invoke-NinjaCopy to read SYSTEM files that are normally locked, such as the NTDS.dit file or registry hives.

T1059.001.name: PowerShell Kerberos Ticket Dump
T1059.001.description:
Detects PowerShell scripts that have the capability of dumping Kerberos tickets from LSA, which potentially indicates an
attacker's attempt to acquire credentials for lateral movement.

T1059.001.name: PowerShell MiniDump Script
T1059.001.description:
This rule detects PowerShell scripts capable of dumping process memory using WindowsErrorReporting or Dbghelp.dll
MiniDumpWriteDump. Attackers can use this tooling to dump LSASS and get access to credentials.

T1059.001.name: Potential PowerShell Pass-the-Hash/Relay Script
T1059.001.description:
Detects PowerShell scripts that can execute pass-the-hash (PtH) attacks, intercept and relay NTLM challenges, and carry
out other man-in-the-middle (MitM) attacks.

T1059.001.name: PowerShell Kerberos Ticket Request
T1059.001.description:
Detects PowerShell scripts that have the capability of requesting kerberos tickets, which is a common step in
Kerberoasting toolkits to crack service accounts.

T1059.001.name: PowerShell Script with Veeam Credential Access Capabilities
T1059.001.description:
Identifies PowerShell scripts that can access and decrypt Veeam credentials stored in MSSQL databases. Attackers can use
Veeam Credentials to target backups as part of destructive operations such as Ransomware attacks.

T1059.001.name: Veeam Backup Library Loaded by Unusual Process
T1059.001.description:
Identifies potential credential decrypt operations by PowerShell or unsigned processes using the Veeam.Backup.Common.dll
library. Attackers can use Veeam Credentials to target backups as part of destructive operations such as Ransomware
attacks.

T1059.001.name: Potential Veeam Credential Access Command
T1059.001.description:
Identifies commands that can access and decrypt Veeam credentials stored in MSSQL databases. Attackers can use Veeam
Credentials to target backups as part of destructive operations such as Ransomware attacks.

T1059.001.name: Potential Antimalware Scan Interface Bypass via PowerShell
T1059.001.description:
Identifies the execution of PowerShell script with keywords related to different Antimalware Scan Interface (AMSI)
bypasses. An adversary may attempt first to disable AMSI before executing further malicious powershell scripts to evade
detection.

T1059.001.name: Clearing Windows Console History
T1059.001.description:
Identifies when a user attempts to clear console history. An adversary may clear the command history of a compromised
account to conceal the actions undertaken during an intrusion.

T1059.001.name: Windows Defender Exclusions Added via PowerShell
T1059.001.description:
Identifies modifications to the Windows Defender configuration settings using PowerShell to add exclusions at the folder
directory or process level.

T1059.001.name: Disabling Windows Defender Security Settings via PowerShell
T1059.001.description: Identifies use of the Set-MpPreference PowerShell command to disable or weaken certain Windows Defender settings.

T1059.001.name: Microsoft Build Engine Started by a Script Process
T1059.001.description:
An instance of MSBuild, the Microsoft Build Engine, was started by a script or the Windows command interpreter. This
behavior is unusual and is sometimes used by malicious payloads.

T1059.001.name: Suspicious .NET Reflection via PowerShell
T1059.001.description:
Detects the use of Reflection.Assembly to load PEs and DLLs in memory in PowerShell scripts. Attackers use this method
to load executables and DLLs without writing to the disk, bypassing security solutions.

T1059.001.name: PowerShell Suspicious Payload Encoded and Compressed
T1059.001.description:
Identifies the use of .NET functionality for decompression and base64 decoding combined in PowerShell scripts, which
malware and security tools heavily use to deobfuscate payloads and load them directly in memory to bypass defenses.

T1059.001.name: PowerShell Script with Windows Defender Tampering Capabilities
T1059.001.description:
Identifies PowerShell scripts containing cmdlets and parameters that attackers can abuse to disable Windows Defender
features. Attackers can tamper with antivirus to reduce the risk of detection when executing their payloads.

T1059.001.name: Potential PowerShell Obfuscated Script
T1059.001.description:
Identifies scripts that contain patterns and known methods that obfuscate PowerShell code. Attackers can use obfuscation
techniques to bypass PowerShell security protections such as Antimalware Scan Interface (AMSI).

T1059.001.description:

T1059.001.description:

T1059.001.description:

T1059.001.description:
Identifies PowerShell scripts that use string concatenation as a form of obfuscation. These methods are designed to
evade static analysis and bypass security protections such as the Antimalware Scan Interface (AMSI).

T1059.001.name: Potential PowerShell Obfuscation via String Reordering
T1059.001.description:
Identifies PowerShell scripts that use string reordering and runtime reconstruction techniques as a form of obfuscation.
These methods are designed to evade static analysis and bypass security protections such as the Antimalware Scan
Interface (AMSI).

T1059.001.name: Potential PowerShell Obfuscation via Special Character Overuse
T1059.001.description:
Identifies PowerShell scripts with an unusually high proportion of whitespace and special characters, often indicative
of obfuscation. This behavior is commonly associated with techniques such as SecureString encoding, formatting
obfuscation, or character-level manipulation designed to bypass static

T1059.001.name: Potential Process Injection via PowerShell
T1059.001.description:
Detects the use of Windows API functions that are commonly abused by malware and security tools to load malicious code
or inject it into remote processes.

T1059.001.name: Windows Firewall Disabled via PowerShell
T1059.001.description:
Identifies when the Windows Firewall is disabled using PowerShell cmdlets, which can help attackers evade network
constraints, like internet and network lateral communication restrictions.

T1059.001.name: Suspicious Execution from a Mounted Device
T1059.001.description:
Identifies when a script interpreter or signed binary is launched via a non-standard working directory. An attacker may
use this technique to evade defenses.

T1059.001.name: PowerShell Share Enumeration Script
T1059.001.description:
Detects scripts that contain PowerShell functions, structures, or Windows API functions related to windows share
enumeration activities. Attackers, mainly ransomware groups, commonly identify and inspect network shares, looking for
critical information for encryption and/or exfiltration.

T1059.001.name: PowerShell Suspicious Discovery Related Windows API Functions
T1059.001.description:
This rule detects the use of discovery-related Windows API functions in PowerShell Scripts. Attackers can use these
functions to perform various situational awareness related activities, like enumerating users, shares, sessions, domain
trusts, groups, etc.

T1059.001.name: Command Execution via SolarWinds Process
T1059.001.description: A suspicious SolarWinds child process (Cmd.exe or Powershell.exe) was detected.

T1059.001.name: Command Shell Activity Started via RunDLL32
T1059.001.description: Identifies command shell activity started via RunDLL32, which is commonly abused by attackers to host malicious code.

T1059.001.name: Delayed Execution via Ping
T1059.001.description:
Identifies the execution of commonly abused Windows utilities via a delayed Ping execution. This behavior is often
observed during malware installation and is consistent with an attacker attempting to evade detection.

T1059.001.name: Potential PowerShell HackTool Script by Author
T1059.001.description:
Detects known PowerShell offensive tooling author's name in PowerShell scripts. Attackers commonly use out-of-the-box
offensive tools without modifying the code, which may still contain the author artifacts. This rule identifies common
author handles found in popular PowerShell scripts used for red

T1059.001.name: Potential PowerShell HackTool Script by Function Names
T1059.001.description:
Detects known PowerShell offensive tooling functions names in PowerShell scripts. Attackers commonly use out-of-the-box
offensive tools without modifying the code. This rule aim is to take advantage of that.

T1059.001.name: Potential Malicious PowerShell Based on Alert Correlation
T1059.001.description: Identifies PowerShell script blocks associated with multiple distinct detections, indicating likely malicious behavior.

T1059.001.name: Suspicious Portable Executable Encoded in Powershell Script
T1059.001.description:
Detects the presence of a portable executable (PE) in a PowerShell script by looking for its encoded header. Attackers
embed PEs into PowerShell scripts to inject them into memory, avoiding defences by not writing to disk.

T1059.001.name: PowerShell PSReflect Script
T1059.001.description:
Detects the use of PSReflect in PowerShell scripts. Attackers leverage PSReflect as a library that enables PowerShell to
access win32 API functions.

T1059.001.name: Command and Scripting Interpreter via Windows Scripts
T1059.001.description: Identifies PowerShell.exe or Cmd.exe execution spawning from Windows Script Host processes Wscript.exe.

T1059.001.name: Potential Command Shell via NetCat
T1059.001.description:
Identifies potential attempt to execute via a reverse shell using the netcat utility to execute Windows commands using
the default interpreters like Cmd.exe and Powershell.

T1059.001.name: Outbound Scheduled Task Activity via PowerShell
T1059.001.description:
Identifies the PowerShell process loading the Task Scheduler COM DLL followed by an outbound RPC network connection
within a short time period. This may indicate lateral movement or remote discovery via scheduled tasks.

T1059.001.name: Suspicious PowerShell Engine ImageLoad
T1059.001.description:
Identifies the PowerShell engine being invoked by unexpected processes. Rather than executing PowerShell functionality
with powershell.exe, some attackers do this to operate more stealthily.

T1059.001.name: Potential Fake CAPTCHA Phishing Attack
T1059.001.description:
Identifies potential fake CAPTCHA phishing attack based on PowerShell or Cmd argument values. Adversaries employ this
technique via compromised websites with browser injects, posing either as fake CAPTCHAs to access the site or as a page
loading error requiring a fix to display the page. The victim

T1059.001.name: Potential Execution via FileFix Phishing Attack
T1059.001.description:
Identifies the execution of Windows commands or downloaded files via the browser's dialog box. Adversaries may use phishing
to instruct the victim to copy and paste malicious commands for execution via crafted phsihing web pages.

T1059.001.name: Suspicious Windows Powershell Arguments
T1059.001.description:
Identifies the execution of PowerShell with suspicious argument values. This behavior is often observed during malware
installation leveraging PowerShell.

T1059.001.name: Volume Shadow Copy Deletion via PowerShell
T1059.001.description:
Identifies the use of the Win32_ShadowCopy class and related cmdlets to achieve shadow copy deletion. This commonly
occurs in tandem with ransomware or other destructive attacks.

T1059.001.name: Suspicious JetBrains TeamCity Child Process
T1059.001.description:
Identifies suspicious processes being spawned by the JetBrain TeamCity process. This activity could be related to
JetBrains remote code execution vulnerabilities.

T1059.001.name: Windows Script Executing PowerShell
T1059.001.description:
Identifies a PowerShell process launched by either cscript.exe or wscript.exe. Observing Windows scripting processes
executing a PowerShell script, may be indicative of malicious activity.

T1059.001.name: Microsoft Exchange Worker Spawning Suspicious Processes
T1059.001.description:
Identifies suspicious processes being spawned by the Microsoft Exchange Server worker process (w3wp). This activity may
indicate exploitation activity or access to an existing web shell backdoor.

T1059.001.name: Suspicious MS Office Child Process
T1059.001.description:
Identifies suspicious child processes of frequently targeted Microsoft Office applications (Word, PowerPoint, Excel).
These child processes are often launched during exploitation of Office applications or from documents with malicious
macros.

T1059.001.name: Suspicious MS Outlook Child Process
T1059.001.description:
Identifies suspicious child processes of Microsoft Outlook. These child processes are often associated with spear
phishing activity.

T1059.001.name: Windows Server Update Service Spawning Suspicious Processes
T1059.001.description:
Identifies suspicious processes being spawned by the Windows Server Update Service. This activity may indicate exploitation
activity or access to an existing web shell backdoor.

T1059.001.name: Suspicious Explorer Child Process
T1059.001.description:
Identifies a suspicious Windows explorer child process. Explorer.exe can be abused to launch malicious scripts or
executables from a trusted parent process.

T1059.001.name: ScreenConnect Server Spawning Suspicious Processes
T1059.001.description:
Identifies suspicious processes being spawned by the ScreenConnect server process (ScreenConnect.Service.exe). This
activity may indicate exploitation activity or access to an existing web shell backdoor.

T1059.001.name: Incoming Execution via PowerShell Remoting
T1059.001.description:
Identifies remote execution via Windows PowerShell remoting. Windows PowerShell remoting allows a user to run any
Windows PowerShell command on one or more remote computers. This could be an indication of lateral movement.

T1059.001.name: Scheduled Task Created by a Windows Script
T1059.001.description:
A scheduled task was created by a Windows script via cscript.exe, wscript.exe or powershell.exe. This can be abused by
an adversary to establish persistence.

T1059.001.name: New ActiveSyncAllowedDeviceID Added via PowerShell
T1059.001.description:
Identifies the use of the Exchange PowerShell cmdlet, Set-CASMailbox, to add a new ActiveSync allowed device.
Adversaries may target user email to collect sensitive information.

T1059.001.name: System Shells via Services
T1059.001.description:
Windows services typically run as SYSTEM and can be used as a privilege escalation opportunity. Malware or penetration
testers may run a shell as a service to gain SYSTEM permissions.

T1059.001.name: Web Shell Detection: Script Process Child of Common Web Processes
T1059.001.description: Identifies suspicious commands executed via a web server, which may suggest a vulnerability and remote shell access.

T1059.001.name: PowerShell Script with Token Impersonation Capabilities
T1059.001.description:
Detects scripts that contain PowerShell functions, structures, or Windows API functions related to token
impersonation/theft. Attackers may duplicate then impersonate another user's token to escalate privileges and bypass
access controls.

T1059.001.name: Service Control Spawned via Script Interpreter
T1059.001.description:
Identifies Service Control (sc.exe) spawning from script interpreter processes to create, modify, or start services.
This can potentially indicate an attempt to elevate privileges or maintain persistence.

T1059.001.name: Entra ID PowerShell Sign-in
T1059.001.description:
Identifies a sign-in using the Azure Active Directory PowerShell module. PowerShell for Azure Active Directory allows
for managing settings from the command line, which is intended for users who are members of an admin role.

T1609.name: Deprecated - Container Management Utility Run Inside A Container
T1609.description:
This rule detects when a container management binary is run from inside a container. These binaries are critical
components of many containerized environments, and their presence and execution in unauthorized containers could
indicate compromise or a misconfiguration.

T1609.name: Deprecated - Interactive Exec Command Launched Against A Running Container
T1609.description:
This rule detects interactive 'exec' events launched against a container using the 'exec' command. Using the 'exec'
command in a pod allows a user to establish a temporary shell session and execute any process/command inside the
container. This rule specifically targets higher-risk interactive comma

T1609.name: Privileged Container Creation with Host Directory Mount
T1609.description:
This rule detects the creation of privileged containers that mount host directories into the container's filesystem.
Such configurations can be exploited by attackers to escape the container isolation and gain access to the host system,
potentially leading to privilege escalation and lateral movemen

T1609.name: Container Management Utility Run Inside A Container
T1609.description:
This rule detects when a container management binary is run from inside a container. These binaries are critical
components of many containerized environments, and their presence and execution in unauthorized containers could
indicate compromise or a misconfiguration.

T1609.name: Kubectl Apply Pod from URL
T1609.description:
This rule detects the execution of the "kubectl apply" command with a URL argument. This command is often used to
apply configurations or deploy resources in a Kubernetes cluster. Attackers may use this command to deploy malicious
pods or modify existing ones, potentially leading to unauthorized acc

T1609.name: Privileged Docker Container Creation
T1609.description:
This rule leverages the new_terms rule type to identify the creation of a potentially unsafe docker container from an
unusual parent process. Attackers can use the `--privileged` flag to create containers with escalated privileges, which
can lead to trivial privilege escalation, docker escaping and

T1609.name: Pod or Container Creation with Suspicious Command-Line
T1609.description:
This rule detects the creation of pods or containers that execute suspicious commands often associated with persistence or
privilege escalation techniques. Attackers may use container orchestration tools like kubectl or container runtimes like
docker to create pods or containers that run shell comma

T1609.name: Kubernetes User Exec into Pod
T1609.description:
This rule detects a user attempt to establish a shell session into a pod using the 'exec' command. Using the 'exec'
command in a pod allows a user to establish a temporary shell session and execute any process/commands in the pod. An
adversary may call bash to gain a persistent interactive shell whi

T1222.002.name: Deprecated - File Made Executable via Chmod Inside A Container
T1222.002.description:
This rule detects when chmod is used to add the execute permission to a file inside a container. Modifying file
permissions to make a file executable could indicate malicious activity, as an attacker may attempt to run unauthorized
or malicious code inside the container.

T1222.002.name: Access Control List Modification via setfacl
T1222.002.description: This rule detects Linux Access Control List (ACL) modification via the setfacl command.

T1222.002.name: File made Immutable by Chattr
T1222.002.description:
Detects a file being made immutable using the chattr binary. Making a file immutable means it cannot be deleted or
renamed, no link can be created to this file, most of the file's metadata can not be modified, and the file can not be
opened in write mode. Threat actors will commonly utilize this to

T1222.002.name: File Made Executable via Chmod Inside A Container
T1222.002.description:
This rule detects when chmod or chown are used to add the execute permission to a file inside a container. Modifying file
permissions to make a file executable could indicate malicious activity, as an attacker may attempt to run unauthorized
or malicious code inside the container.

T1222.name: Deprecated - File Made Executable via Chmod Inside A Container
T1222.description:
This rule detects when chmod is used to add the execute permission to a file inside a container. Modifying file
permissions to make a file executable could indicate malicious activity, as an attacker may attempt to run unauthorized
or malicious code inside the container.

T1222.name: Access Control List Modification via setfacl
T1222.description: This rule detects Linux Access Control List (ACL) modification via the setfacl command.

T1222.name: File made Immutable by Chattr
T1222.description:
Detects a file being made immutable using the chattr binary. Making a file immutable means it cannot be deleted or
renamed, no link can be created to this file, most of the file's metadata can not be modified, and the file can not be
opened in write mode. Threat actors will commonly utilize this to

T1222.name: File Permission Modification in Writable Directory
T1222.description:
Identifies file permission modifications in common writable directories by a non-root user. Adversaries often drop files
or payloads into a writable directory and change permissions prior to execution.

T1222.name: File Made Executable via Chmod Inside A Container
T1222.description:
This rule detects when chmod or chown are used to add the execute permission to a file inside a container. Modifying file
permissions to make a file executable could indicate malicious activity, as an attacker may attempt to run unauthorized
or malicious code inside the container.

T1222.name: Adding Hidden File Attribute via Attrib
