T1087.name: Enumeration Command Spawned via WMIPrvSE
T1087.description:
Identifies native Windows host and network enumeration commands spawned by the Windows Management Instrumentation
Provider Service (WMIPrvSE).

T1087.name: Mounting Hidden or WebDav Remote Shares
T1087.description:
Identifies the use of net.exe to mount a WebDav or hidden remote share. This may indicate lateral movement or
preparation for data exfiltration.

T1087.name: AWS IAM Principal Enumeration via UpdateAssumeRolePolicy
T1087.description:
Detects repeated failed attempts to update an IAM role’s trust policy in an AWS account, consistent with role and user
enumeration techniques. In this technique, an attacker who controls credentials in the current account repeatedly calls
UpdateAssumeRolePolicy on a single role, cycling through gues

T1087.name: AWS STS GetCallerIdentity API Called for the First Time
T1087.description:
An adversary with access to a set of compromised credentials may attempt to verify that the credentials are valid and
determine what account they are using. This rule looks for the first time an identity has called the
STS GetCallerIdentity API, which may be an indicator of compromised credentials.

T1087.name: Entra ID Sign-in BloodHound Suite User-Agent Detected
T1087.description:
Identifies potential enumeration activity using AzureHound, SharpHound, or BloodHound across Microsoft cloud services.
These tools are often used by red teamers and adversaries to map users, groups, roles, applications, and access
relationships within Microsoft Entra ID (Azure AD) and Microsoft 365.

T1087.name: Entra ID Sign-in TeamFiltration User-Agent Detected
T1087.description:
Identifies potential enumeration or password spraying activity using TeamFiltration tool. TeamFiltration is an
open-source enumeration, password spraying and exfiltration tool designed for Entra ID and Microsoft 365. Adversaries
are known to use TeamFiltration in-the-wild to enumerate users, groups,

T1059.002.name: Apple Script Execution followed by Network Connection
T1059.002.description:
Detects execution via the Apple script interpreter (osascript) followed by a network connection from the same process
within a short time period. Adversaries may use malicious scripts for execution and command and control.

T1059.002.name: Creation of Hidden Login Item via Apple Script
T1059.002.description:
Identifies the execution of osascript to create a hidden login item. This may indicate an attempt to persist a malicious
program while concealing its presence.

T1566.001.name: Suspicious macOS MS Office Child Process
T1566.001.description:
Identifies suspicious child processes of frequently targeted Microsoft Office applications (Word, PowerPoint, and
Excel). These child processes are often launched during exploitation of Office applications or by documents with
malicious macros.

T1566.001.name: Unusual Network Destination Domain Name
T1566.001.description:
A machine learning job detected an unusual network destination domain name. This can be due to initial access,
persistence, command-and-control, or exfiltration activity. For example, when a user clicks on a link in a phishing
email or opens a malicious document, a request may be sent to download an

T1566.001.name: Network Traffic to Rare Destination Country
T1566.001.description:
A machine learning job detected a rare destination country name in the network logs. This can be due to initial access,
persistence, command-and-control, or exfiltration activity. For example, when a user clicks on a link in a phishing
email or opens a malicious document, a request may be sent to do

T1566.001.name: Downloaded Shortcut Files
T1566.001.description:
Identifies .lnk shortcut file downloaded from outside the local network. These shortcut files are commonly used in
phishing campaigns.

T1566.001.name: Downloaded URL Files
T1566.001.description:
Identifies .url shortcut files downloaded from outside the local network. These shortcut files are commonly used in
phishing campaigns.

T1566.001.name: Unusual Execution via Microsoft Common Console File
T1566.001.description:
Identifies the execution of a child process from a Microsoft Common Console file. Adversaries may embed a malicious
command in an MSC file in order to trick victims into executing malicious commands.

T1566.001.name: Execution of File Written or Modified by Microsoft Office
T1566.001.description:
Identifies an executable created by a Microsoft Office application and subsequently executed. These processes are often
launched via scripts inside documents or during exploitation of Microsoft Office applications.

T1566.001.name: Deprecated - Execution of File Written or Modified by PDF Reader
T1566.001.description:
Identifies a suspicious file that was written by a PDF reader application and subsequently executed. These processes are
often launched via exploitation of PDF applications.

T1566.001.name: Suspicious PDF Reader Child Process
T1566.001.description:
Identifies suspicious child processes of PDF reader applications. These child processes are often launched via
exploitation of PDF applications or social engineering.

T1566.001.name: Potential Fake CAPTCHA Phishing Attack
T1566.001.description:
Identifies potential fake CAPTCHA phishing attack based on PowerShell or Cmd argument values. Adversaries employ this
technique via compromised websites with browser injects, posing either as fake CAPTCHAs to access the site or as a page
loading error requiring a fix to display the page. The victim

T1566.001.name: Potential Execution via FileFix Phishing Attack
T1566.001.description:
Identifies the execution of Windows commands or downloaded files via the browser's dialog box. Adversaries may use phishing
to instruct the victim to copy and paste malicious commands for execution via crafted phsihing web pages.

T1566.001.name: Suspicious HTML File Creation
T1566.001.description:
Identifies the execution of a browser process to open an HTML file with high entropy and size. Adversaries may smuggle
data and files past content filters by hiding malicious payloads inside of seemingly benign HTML files.

T1566.001.name: Suspicious Execution from INET Cache
T1566.001.description:
Identifies the execution of a process with arguments pointing to the INetCache Folder. Adversaries may deliver malicious
content via WININET during initial access.

T1566.001.name: Suspicious Execution via Microsoft Office Add-Ins
T1566.001.description:
Identifies execution of common Microsoft Office applications to launch an Office Add-In from a suspicious path or with
an unusual parent process. This may indicate an attempt to get initial access via a malicious phishing MS Office Add-In.

T1566.001.name: Remote Desktop File Opened from Suspicious Path
T1566.001.description:
Identifies attempts to open a remote desktop file from suspicious paths. Adversaries may abuse RDP files for initial
access.

T1566.001.name: Windows Script Executing PowerShell
T1566.001.description:
Identifies a PowerShell process launched by either cscript.exe or wscript.exe. Observing Windows scripting processes
executing a PowerShell script, may be indicative of malicious activity.

T1566.001.name: Windows Script Interpreter Executing Process via WMI
T1566.001.description:
Identifies use of the built-in Windows script interpreters (cscript.exe or wscript.exe) being used to execute a process
via Windows Management Instrumentation (WMI). This may be indicative of malicious activity.

T1566.001.name: Suspicious MS Office Child Process
T1566.001.description:
Identifies suspicious child processes of frequently targeted Microsoft Office applications (Word, PowerPoint, Excel).
These child processes are often launched during exploitation of Office applications or from documents with malicious
macros.

T1566.001.name: Suspicious MS Outlook Child Process
T1566.001.description:
Identifies suspicious child processes of Microsoft Outlook. These child processes are often associated with spear
phishing activity.

T1566.001.name: Potential CVE-2025-33053 Exploitation
T1566.001.description: Identifies a suspicious Diagnostics Utility for Internet Explorer child process. This may indicate the successful exploitation of the vulnerability CVE-2025-33053.

T1566.001.name: Suspicious Explorer Child Process
T1566.001.description:
Identifies a suspicious Windows explorer child process. Explorer.exe can be abused to launch malicious scripts or
executables from a trusted parent process.

T1566.001.name: M365 Security Compliance Email Reported by User as Malware or Phish
T1566.001.description:

stay ahead of scammers and threat actors, as security products can be bypassed, and the user can still receive a
malicious message. Educating users to report suspicious messages can

T1566.description:
Identifies the execution of a hosted XSL script using the Microsoft.XMLDOM COM interface via Microsoft Office processes.

T1566.name: Entra ID OAuth Device Code Flow with Concurrent Sign-ins
T1566.description:

T1566.description:

T1566.description: Identifies device code authentication with an Azure broker client for Entra ID. Adversaries abuse Primary Refresh Tokens (PRTs) to bypass multi-factor authentication (MFA) and gain unauthorized access to Azure resources. PRTs are used in Conditional Access policies to enforce device-based controls.

T1566.description:

T1566.name: Google Workspace Object Copied to External Drive with App Consent
T1566.description:
Detects when a user copies a Google spreadsheet, form, document or script from an external drive. Sequence logic has

T1566.name: M365 Identity OAuth Flow by Rare Client to Microsoft Graph
T1566.description:
Identifies sign-ins on behalf of a principal user to the Microsoft Graph API from multiple IPs using the Microsoft
Authentication Broker or Visual Studio Code application. This behavior may indicate an adversary using a phished OAuth
refresh token.

T1566.name: M365 Threat Intelligence Signal
T1566.description:
Identifies a Microsoft 365 audit log generated for Threat Intelligence signals by Microsoft Defender for Office 365.
Signals generated may relate to services such as Exchange Online, SharePoint Online, OneDrive for Business and others.

malicious message. Educating users to report suspicious messages can

T1566.name: Okta FastPass Phishing Detection
T1566.description: Detects when Okta FastPass prevents a user from authenticating to a phishing website.

T1550.003.name: Potential Kerberos Attack via Bifrost
T1550.003.description:
Identifies use of Bifrost, a known macOS Kerberos pentesting tool, which can be used to dump cached Kerberos tickets or
attempt unauthorized authentication techniques such as pass-the-ticket/hash and kerberoasting.

T1550.003.name: Suspicious Kerberos Authentication Ticket Request
T1550.003.description:
Correlates network connections to the standard Kerberos port by an unusual process from the source machine with a Kerberos
authentication ticket request from the target domain controller.

T1021.002.name: Attempt to Mount SMB Share via Command Line
T1021.002.description:
Identifies the execution of macOS built-in commands to mount a Server Message Block (SMB) network share. Adversaries may
use valid accounts to interact with a remote network share using SMB.

T1021.002.name: Windows Registry File Creation in SMB Share
T1021.002.description:
Identifies the creation or modification of a medium-size registry hive file on a Server Message Block (SMB) share, which
may indicate an exfiltration attempt of a previously dumped Security Account Manager (SAM) registry hive for credential
extraction on an attacker-controlled system.

T1021.002.name: PsExec Network Connection
T1021.002.description:
Identifies use of the SysInternals tool PsExec.exe making a network connection. This could be an indication of lateral
movement.

T1021.002.name: Suspicious Execution from a WebDav Share
T1021.002.description:
Identifies attempt to execute Windows scripts from a remote WebDav Share. Adversaries may abuse this method to evade
dropping malicious files to victim file system.

T1021.002.name: Potential Ransomware Behavior - Note Files by System
T1021.002.description:
This rule identifies the creation of multiple files with same name and over SMB by the same user. This behavior may indicate the 
successful remote execution of a ransomware dropping file notes to different folders.

T1021.002.name: Suspicious File Renamed via SMB
T1021.002.description:
Identifies an incoming SMB connection followed by a suspicious file rename operation. This may indicate a remote
ransomware attack via the SMB protocol.

T1021.002.name: Potential Ransomware Note File Dropped via SMB
T1021.002.description:
Identifies an incoming SMB connection followed by the creation of a file with a name similar to ransomware note files.
This may indicate a remote ransomware attack via the SMB protocol.

T1021.002.name: NullSessionPipe Registry Modification
T1021.002.description:
Identifies NullSessionPipe registry modifications that specify which pipes can be accessed anonymously. This could be
indicative of adversary lateral movement preparation by making the added pipe available to everyone.

T1021.002.name: SMB Connections via LOLBin or Untrusted Process
T1021.002.description:
Identifies potentially suspicious processes that are not trusted or living-off-the-land binaries (LOLBin) making Server
Message Block (SMB) network connections over port 445. Windows File Sharing is typically implemented over SMB, which
communicates between hosts using port 445. Legitimate connectio

T1021.002.name: Potential Lateral Tool Transfer via SMB Share
T1021.002.description:
Identifies the creation or change of a Windows executable file over network shares. Adversaries may transfer tools or
other files between systems in a compromised environment.

T1021.002.name: Remote Execution via File Shares
T1021.002.description:
Identifies the execution of a file that was created by the virtual system process. This may indicate lateral movement
via network file shares.

T1021.002.name: Mounting Hidden or WebDav Remote Shares
T1021.002.description:
Identifies the use of net.exe to mount a WebDav or hidden remote share. This may indicate lateral movement or
preparation for data exfiltration.

T1021.002.name: Remote File Copy to a Hidden Share
T1021.002.description:
Identifies a remote file copy attempt to a hidden network share. This may indicate lateral movement or data staging
activity.

T1543.001.name: Launch Service Creation and Immediate Loading
T1543.001.description:
An adversary can establish persistence by installing a new launch agent that executes at login by using launchd or
launchctl to load a plist into the appropriate directories.

T1543.001.name: Suspicious Hidden Child Process of Launchd
T1543.001.description:
Identifies the execution of a launchd child process with a hidden file. An adversary can establish persistence by
installing a new logon item, launch agent, or daemon that executes upon login.

T1543.001.name: Creation of Hidden Launch Agent or Daemon
T1543.001.description:
Identifies the creation of a hidden launch agent or daemon. An adversary may establish persistence by installing a new
launch agent or daemon which executes at login.

T1647.name: Creation of Hidden Login Item via Apple Script
T1647.description:
Identifies the execution of osascript to create a hidden login item. This may indicate an attempt to persist a malicious
program while concealing its presence.

T1647.name: Potential Persistence via Login Hook
T1647.description:
Identifies the creation or modification of the login window property list (plist). Adversaries may modify plist files to
run a program during system boot or user login for persistence.

T1547.002.name: Authorization Plugin Modification
T1547.002.description:
Authorization plugins are used to extend the authorization services API and implement mechanisms that are not natively
supported by the OS, such as multi-factor authentication with third party software. Adversaries may abuse this feature
to persist and/or collect clear text credentials as they trave

T1547.002.name: Potential LSA Authentication Package Abuse
T1547.002.description:
Adversaries can use the autostart mechanism provided by the Local Security Authority (LSA) authentication packages for
privilege escalation or persistence by placing a reference to a binary in the Windows registry. The binary will then be
executed by SYSTEM when the authentication packages are loade

T1546.014.name: Emond Rules Creation or Modification
T1546.014.description:
Identifies the creation or modification of the Event Monitor Daemon (emond) rules. Adversaries may abuse this service by
writing a rule to execute commands when a defined event occurs, such as system start up or user authentication.

T1546.014.name: Suspicious Emond Child Process
T1546.014.description:
Identifies the execution of a suspicious child process of the Event Monitor Daemon (emond). Adversaries may abuse this
service by writing a rule to execute commands when a defined event occurs, such as system start up or user
authentication.

T1546.002.name: Unexpected Child Process of macOS Screensaver Engine
T1546.002.description:
Identifies when a child process is spawned by the screensaver engine process, which is consistent with an attacker's
malicious payload being executed after the screensaver activated on the endpoint. An adversary can maintain persistence
on a macOS endpoint by creating a malicious screensaver (.saver

T1546.002.name: Uncommon Registry Persistence Change
T1546.002.description:
Detects changes to registry persistence keys that are not commonly used or modified by legitimate programs. This could
be an indication of an adversary's attempt to persist in a stealthy manner.

T1548.004.name: Execution with Explicit Credentials via Scripting
T1548.004.description:
Identifies execution of the security_authtrampoline process via a scripting interpreter. This occurs when programs use
AuthorizationExecute-WithPrivileges from the Security.framework to run another program with root privileges. It should
not be run by itself, as this is a sign of execution with expl

T1078.002.name: Spike in Successful Logon Events from a Source IP
T1078.002.description:
A machine learning job found an unusually large spike in successful authentication events from a particular source IP
address. This can be due to password spraying, user enumeration or brute force activity.

T1078.002.name: Rare User Logon
T1078.002.description:
A machine learning job found an unusual user name in the authentication logs. An unusual user name is one way of
detecting credentialed access by means of a new or dormant user account. An inactive user account (because the user has
left the organization) that becomes active may be due to credential

T1078.002.name: Unusual Windows Username
T1078.002.description:
A machine learning job detected activity for a username that is not normally active, which can indicate unauthorized
changes, activity by unauthorized users, lateral movement, or compromised credentials. In many organizations, new
usernames are not often created apart from specific types of system a

T1078.002.name: FirstTime Seen Account Performing DCSync
T1078.002.description:
This rule identifies when a User Account starts the Active Directory Replication Process for the first time. Attackers
can use the DCSync technique to get credential information of individual accounts or the entire domain, thus
compromising the entire domain.

T1078.002.name: Potential Credential Access via DCSync
T1078.002.description:
This rule identifies when a User Account starts the Active Directory Replication Process. Attackers can use the DCSync
technique to get credential information of individual accounts or the entire domain, thus compromising the entire
domain.

T1078.002.name: Kerberos Pre-authentication Disabled for User
T1078.002.description:
Identifies the modification of an account's Kerberos pre-authentication options. An adversary with
GenericWrite/GenericAll rights over the account can maliciously modify these settings to perform offline password
cracking attacks such as AS-REP roasting.

T1078.002.name: Access to a Sensitive LDAP Attribute
T1078.002.description:
Identify access to sensitive Active Directory object attributes that contains credentials and decryption keys such as
unixUserPassword, ms-PKI-AccountCredentials and msPKI-CredentialRoamingTokens.

T1078.002.name: AdminSDHolder Backdoor
T1078.002.description:
Detects modifications in the AdminSDHolder object. Attackers can abuse the SDProp process to implement a persistent
backdoor in Active Directory. SDProp compares the permissions on protected objects with those defined on the
AdminSDHolder object. If the permissions on any of the protected accounts a

T1078.002.name: AdminSDHolder SDProp Exclusion Added
T1078.002.description:
Identifies a modification on the dsHeuristics attribute on the bit that holds the configuration of groups excluded from
the SDProp process. The SDProp compares the permissions on protected objects with those defined on the AdminSDHolder
object. If the permissions on any of the protected accounts and

T1078.002.name: Delegated Managed Service Account Modification by an Unusual User
T1078.002.description:
Detects modifications in the msDS-ManagedAccountPrecededByLink attribute of a delegated managed service account by an unusual
subject account. Attackers can abuse this attribute to take over the permission of a target account and inherit it's permissions
allowing them to further elevate privileges.

T1078.002.name: dMSA Account Creation by an Unusual User
T1078.002.description:
Detects the creation of a delegated Managed Service Account by an unusual subject account. Attackers can abuse the dMSA
account migration feature to elevate privileges abusing weak persmission allowing users child objects rights or
msDS-DelegatedManagedServiceAccount rights.

T1078.002.name: Potential Privileged Escalation via SamAccountName Spoofing
T1078.002.description:
Identifies a suspicious computer account name rename event, which may indicate an attempt to exploit CVE-2021-42278 to
elevate privileges from a standard domain user to a user with domain admin privileges. CVE-2021-42278 is a security
vulnerability that allows potential attackers to impersonate a do

T1078.002.name: Remote Computer Account DnsHostName Update
T1078.002.description:
Identifies the remote update to a computer account's DnsHostName attribute. If the new value set is a valid domain
controller DNS hostname and the subject computer name is not a domain controller, then it's highly likely a preparation
step to exploit CVE-2022-26923 in an attempt to elevate privilege

T1016.name: Unusual Linux Network Configuration Discovery
T1016.description:
Looks for commands related to system network configuration discovery from an unusual user context. This can be due to
uncommon troubleshooting activity or due to a compromised account. A compromised account may be used by a threat actor
to engage in system network configuration discovery in order to

T1016.name: Active Directory Discovery using AdExplorer
T1016.description:
This rule detects the use of ADExplorer utility. Active Directory Explorer (AD Explorer) is an advanced Active Directory (AD)
viewer and editor. AD Explorer also includes the ability to save snapshots of an AD database for off-line viewing and comparisons.
Adversaries may abuse this utility to perfo

T1016.name: AdFind Command Activity
T1016.description:
This rule detects the Active Directory query tool, AdFind.exe. AdFind has legitimate purposes, but it is frequently
leveraged by threat actors to perform post-exploitation Active Directory reconnaissance. The AdFind tool has been
observed in Trickbot, Ryuk, Maze, and FIN6 campaigns. For Winlogbeat,

T1016.name: System Public IP Discovery via DNS Query
T1016.description: Identifies DNS queries to known public IP address lookup web services. Malwares tend to perform this action to assess potential targets.

T1016.name: Enumeration Command Spawned via WMIPrvSE
T1016.description:
Identifies native Windows host and network enumeration commands spawned by the Windows Management Instrumentation
Provider Service (WMIPrvSE).

T1049.name: Unusual Linux Network Connection Discovery
T1049.description:
Looks for commands related to system network connection discovery from an unusual user context. This can be due to
uncommon troubleshooting activity or due to a compromised account. A compromised account may be used by a threat actor
to engage in system network connection discovery in order to incre

T1498.name: Spike in host-based traffic
T1498.description:
A machine learning job has detected a sudden spike in host based traffic. This can be due to a range of security issues, such as a compromised system, DDoS attacks,
malware infections, privilege escalation, or data exfiltration.

T1498.name: Spike in Firewall Denies
T1498.description:
A machine learning job detected an unusually large spike in network traffic that was denied by network access control
lists (ACLs) or firewall rules. Such a burst of denied traffic is usually caused by either 1) a mis-configured
application or firewall or 2) suspicious or malicious activity. Unsucce

T1498.name: Spike in Network Traffic
T1498.description:
A machine learning job detected an unusually large spike in network traffic. Such a burst of traffic, if not caused by a
surge in business activity, can be due to suspicious or malicious activity. Large-scale data exfiltration may produce a
burst of network traffic; this could also be due to unusual

T1498.name: Possible Okta DoS Attack
T1498.description:
Detects possible Denial of Service (DoS) attacks against an Okta organization. An adversary may attempt to disrupt an
organization's business operations by performing a DoS attack against its Okta service.

T1499.name: Spike in host-based traffic
T1499.description:
A machine learning job has detected a sudden spike in host based traffic. This can be due to a range of security issues, such as a compromised system, DDoS attacks,
malware infections, privilege escalation, or data exfiltration.

T1499.name: Spike in Firewall Denies
T1499.description:
A machine learning job detected an unusually large spike in network traffic that was denied by network access control
lists (ACLs) or firewall rules. Such a burst of denied traffic is usually caused by either 1) a mis-configured
application or firewall or 2) suspicious or malicious activity. Unsucce

T1499.name: Decline in host-based traffic
T1499.description:
A machine learning job has detected a sudden drop in host based traffic. This can be due to a range of security issues, such as a compromised system,
a failed service, or a network misconfiguration.

T1499.name: Possible Okta DoS Attack
T1499.description:
Detects possible Denial of Service (DoS) attacks against an Okta organization. An adversary may attempt to disrupt an
organization's business operations by performing a DoS attack against its Okta service.

T1590.name: Spike in Firewall Denies
T1590.description:
A machine learning job detected an unusually large spike in network traffic that was denied by network access control
lists (ACLs) or firewall rules. Such a burst of denied traffic is usually caused by either 1) a mis-configured
application or firewall or 2) suspicious or malicious activity. Unsucce

T1566.002.name: Unusual Network Destination Domain Name
T1566.002.description:
A machine learning job detected an unusual network destination domain name. This can be due to initial access,
persistence, command-and-control, or exfiltration activity. For example, when a user clicks on a link in a phishing
email or opens a malicious document, a request may be sent to download an

T1566.002.name: Network Traffic to Rare Destination Country
T1566.002.description:
A machine learning job detected a rare destination country name in the network logs. This can be due to initial access,
persistence, command-and-control, or exfiltration activity. For example, when a user clicks on a link in a phishing
email or opens a malicious document, a request may be sent to do

T1566.002.name: Downloaded Shortcut Files
T1566.002.description:
Identifies .lnk shortcut file downloaded from outside the local network. These shortcut files are commonly used in
phishing campaigns.

T1566.002.name: Downloaded URL Files
T1566.002.description:
Identifies .url shortcut files downloaded from outside the local network. These shortcut files are commonly used in
phishing campaigns.

T1566.002.name: Unusual Execution via Microsoft Common Console File
T1566.002.description:
Identifies the execution of a child process from a Microsoft Common Console file. Adversaries may embed a malicious
command in an MSC file in order to trick victims into executing malicious commands.

T1566.002.name: Execution of File Written or Modified by Microsoft Office
T1566.002.description:
Identifies an executable created by a Microsoft Office application and subsequently executed. These processes are often
launched via scripts inside documents or during exploitation of Microsoft Office applications.

T1566.002.name: Deprecated - Execution of File Written or Modified by PDF Reader
T1566.002.description:
Identifies a suspicious file that was written by a PDF reader application and subsequently executed. These processes are
often launched via exploitation of PDF applications.

T1566.002.name: Suspicious HTML File Creation
T1566.002.description:
Identifies the execution of a browser process to open an HTML file with high entropy and size. Adversaries may smuggle
data and files past content filters by hiding malicious payloads inside of seemingly benign HTML files.

T1566.002.name: Potential Remote File Execution via MSIEXEC
T1566.002.description:
Identifies the execution of the built-in Windows Installer, msiexec.exe, to install a remote package. Adversaries may
abuse msiexec.exe to launch local or network accessible MSI files.

T1566.002.name: Potential CVE-2025-33053 Exploitation
T1566.002.description: Identifies a suspicious Diagnostics Utility for Internet Explorer child process. This may indicate the successful exploitation of the vulnerability CVE-2025-33053.

T1566.002.name: Suspicious Explorer Child Process
T1566.002.description:
Identifies a suspicious Windows explorer child process. Explorer.exe can be abused to launch malicious scripts or
executables from a trusted parent process.

T1566.002.name: Remote XSL Script Execution via COM
T1566.002.description:
Identifies the execution of a hosted XSL script using the Microsoft.XMLDOM COM interface via Microsoft Office processes.
This behavior may indicate adversarial activity to execute malicious JScript or VBScript on the system.

T1566.002.name: Entra ID OAuth Device Code Flow with Concurrent Sign-ins
T1566.002.description:
Identifies concurrent Entra ID sign-in events for the same user and session from multiple sources, and where one of the
authentication event has some suspicious properties often associated to DeviceCode and OAuth phishing. Adversaries may
steal Refresh Tokens (RTs) via phishing to bypass multi-facto

T1566.002.name: Entra ID Concurrent Sign-in with Suspicious Properties
T1566.002.description:
Identifies concurrent azure signin events for the same user and from multiple sources, and where one of the
authentication event has some suspicious properties often associated to DeviceCode and OAuth phishing. Adversaries may
steal Refresh Tokens (RTs) via phishing to bypass multi-factor authentica

T1566.002.name: Entra ID OAuth Device Code Grant by Microsoft Authentication Broker
T1566.002.description: Identifies device code authentication with an Azure broker client for Entra ID. Adversaries abuse Primary Refresh Tokens (PRTs) to bypass multi-factor authentication (MFA) and gain unauthorized access to Azure resources. PRTs are used in Conditional Access policies to enforce device-based controls.

T1566.002.name: Entra ID OAuth Device Code Grant by Unusual User
T1566.002.description:
Identifies when a user is observed for the first time in the last 14 days authenticating using the device code
authentication workflow. This authentication workflow can be abused by attackers to phish users and steal access tokens
to impersonate the victim. By its very nature, device code should onl

T1566.002.name: Entra ID Illicit Consent Grant via Registered Application
T1566.002.description:
Identifies an illicit consent grant request on-behalf-of a registered Entra ID application. Adversaries may create and
register an application in Microsoft Entra ID for the purpose of requesting user consent to access resources. This is
accomplished by tricking a user into granting consent to the ap

T1566.002.name: Entra ID OAuth Flow by Visual Studio Code to Microsoft Graph
T1566.002.description:
Detects potentially suspicious OAuth authorization activity in Microsoft Entra ID where the Visual Studio Code
first-party application (client_id = aebc6443-996d-45c2-90f0-388ff96faa56) is used to request access to Microsoft Graph
resources. While this client ID is legitimately used by Visual Studio

T1566.002.name: Entra ID OAuth Flow by Microsoft Authentication Broker to Device Registration Service (DRS)
T1566.002.description:
Identifies separate OAuth authorization flows in Microsoft Entra ID where the same user principal and session ID are
observed across multiple IP addresses within a 5-minute window. These flows involve the Microsoft Authentication Broker
(MAB) as the client application and the Device Registration Ser

T1566.002.name: Google Workspace Object Copied to External Drive with App Consent
T1566.002.description:
Detects when a user copies a Google spreadsheet, form, document or script from an external drive. Sequence logic has
been added to also detect when a user grants a custom Google application permission via OAuth shortly after. An
adversary may send a phishing email to the victim with a Drive object l

T1566.002.name: M365 Identity OAuth Flow by User Sign-in to Device Registration
T1566.002.description:
Identifies attempts to register a new device in Microsoft Entra ID after OAuth authentication with authorization code
grant. Adversaries may use OAuth phishing techniques to obtain an OAuth authorization code, which can then be exchanged
for access and refresh tokens. This rule detects a sequence of

T1566.002.name: M365 Identity OAuth Flow by Rare Client to Microsoft Graph
T1566.002.description:
Identifies sign-ins on behalf of a principal user to the Microsoft Graph API from multiple IPs using the Microsoft
Authentication Broker or Visual Studio Code application. This behavior may indicate an adversary using a phished OAuth
refresh token.

T1566.002.name: M365 Identity OAuth Illicit Consent Grant by Rare Client and User
T1566.002.description:
Identifies an Microsoft 365 illicit consent grant request on-behalf-of a registered Entra ID application. Adversaries
may create and register an application in Microsoft Entra ID for the purpose of requesting user consent to access
resources in Microsoft 365. This is accomplished by tricking a user

T1566.002.name: M365 Identity OAuth Flow by Visual Studio Code Client to Microsoft Graph
T1566.002.description:
Detects potentially suspicious OAuth authorization activity in Microsoft 365 where the Visual Studio Code first-party
application (client_id = aebc6443-996d-45c2-90f0-388ff96faa56) is used to request access to Microsoft Graph resources.
While this client ID is legitimately used by Visual Studio Code

T1566.002.name: M365 Security Compliance Email Reported by User as Malware or Phish
T1566.002.description:
Detects the occurrence of emails reported as Phishing or Malware by Users. Security Awareness training is essential to
stay ahead of scammers and threat actors, as security products can be bypassed, and the user can still receive a
malicious message. Educating users to report suspicious messages can

T1543.003.name: Anomalous Process For a Linux Population
T1543.003.description:
Searches for rare processes running on multiple Linux hosts in an entire fleet or network. This reduces the detection of
false positives since automated maintenance processes usually only run occasionally on a single machine but are common
to all or many hosts in a fleet.

T1543.003.name: Unusual Process For a Windows Host
T1543.003.description:
Identifies rare processes that do not usually run on individual hosts, which can indicate execution of unauthorized
services, malware, or persistence mechanisms. Processes are considered rare when they only run occasionally as compared
with other processes running on the host.

T1543.003.name: Unusual Windows Path Activity
T1543.003.description:
Identifies processes started from atypical folders in the file system, which might indicate malware execution or
persistence mechanisms. In corporate Windows environments, software installation is centrally managed and it is unusual
for programs to be executed from user or temporary directories. Pro

T1543.003.name: Unusual Windows Service
T1543.003.description:
A machine learning job detected an unusual Windows service, This can indicate execution of unauthorized services,
malware, or persistence mechanisms. In corporate Windows environments, hosts do not generally run many rare or unique
services. This job helps detect malware and persistence mechanisms t

T1543.003.name: Service DACL Modification via sc.exe
T1543.003.description: Identifies DACL modifications to deny access to a service, making it unstoppable, or hide it from system and users.

T1543.003.name: Service Command Lateral Movement
T1543.003.description:
Identifies use of sc.exe to create, modify, or start services on remote hosts. This could be indicative of adversary
lateral movement but will be noisy if commonly done by admins.

T1543.003.name: Remote Windows Service Installed
T1543.003.description:
Identifies a network logon followed by Windows service creation with same LogonId. This could be indicative of lateral
movement, but will be noisy if commonly done by administrators."

T1543.003.name: Unsigned DLL Loaded by Svchost
T1543.003.description:
Identifies an unsigned library created in the last 5 minutes and subsequently loaded by a shared windows service
(svchost). Adversaries may use this technique to maintain persistence or run with System privileges.

T1543.003.name: Suspicious Service was Installed in the System
T1543.003.description:
Identifies the creation of a new Windows service with suspicious Service command values. Windows services typically run
as SYSTEM and can be used for privilege escalation and persistence.

T1543.003.name: Unusual Persistence via Services Registry
T1543.003.description:
Identifies processes modifying the services registry key directly, instead of through the expected Windows APIs. This
could be an indication of an adversary attempting to stealthily persist through abnormal service creation or
modification of an existing service.

T1543.003.name: Suspicious ImagePath Service Creation
T1543.003.description:
Identifies the creation of a suspicious ImagePath value. This could be an indication of an adversary attempting to
stealthily persist or escalate privileges through abnormal service creation.

T1543.003.name: System Shells via Services
T1543.003.description:
Windows services typically run as SYSTEM and can be used as a privilege escalation opportunity. Malware or penetration
testers may run a shell as a service to gain SYSTEM permissions.

T1543.003.name: Persistence via Update Orchestrator Service Hijack
T1543.003.description:
Identifies potential hijacking of the Microsoft Update Orchestrator Service to establish persistence with an integrity
level of SYSTEM.

T1543.003.name: Persistence via WMI Standard Registry Provider
T1543.003.description:
Identifies use of the Windows Management Instrumentation StdRegProv (registry provider) to modify commonly abused
registry locations for persistence.

T1543.003.name: First Time Seen Driver Loaded
T1543.003.description:
Identifies the load of a driver with an original file name and signature values that were observed for the first time
during the last 30 days. This rule type can help baseline drivers installation within your environment.

T1543.003.name: Service Creation via Local Kerberos Authentication
T1543.003.description:
Identifies a suspicious local successful logon event where the Logon Package is Kerberos, the remote address is set to
localhost, followed by a sevice creation from the same LogonId. This may indicate an attempt to leverage a Kerberos
relay attack variant that can be used to elevate privilege locall

T1543.003.name: Potential Privilege Escalation via Service ImagePath Modification
T1543.003.description:
Identifies registry modifications to default services that could enable privilege escalation to SYSTEM. Attackers with
privileges from groups like Server Operators may change the ImagePath of services to executables under their control or
to execute commands.

T1543.003.name: Service Control Spawned via Script Interpreter
T1543.003.description:
Identifies Service Control (sc.exe) spawning from script interpreter processes to create, modify, or start services.
This can potentially indicate an attempt to elevate privileges or maintain persistence.

T1543.003.name: Windows Service Installed via an Unusual Client
T1543.003.description:
Identifies the creation of a Windows service by an unusual client process. Services may be created with administrator
privileges but are executed under SYSTEM privileges, so an adversary may also use a service to escalate privileges from
administrator to SYSTEM.

T1588.001.name: Anomalous Linux Compiler Activity
T1588.001.description:
Looks for compiler activity by a user context which does not normally run compilers. This can be the result of ad-hoc
software changes or unauthorized software deployment. This can also be due to local privilege elevation via locally run
exploits or malware activity.

T1588.name: Anomalous Linux Compiler Activity
T1588.description:
Looks for compiler activity by a user context which does not normally run compilers. This can be the result of ad-hoc
software changes or unauthorized software deployment. This can also be due to local privilege elevation via locally run
exploits or malware activity.

T1568.002.name: Cobalt Strike Command and Control Beacon
T1568.002.description:
Cobalt Strike is a threat emulation platform commonly modified and used by adversaries to conduct network attack and
exploitation campaigns. This rule detects a network activity algorithm leveraged by Cobalt Strike implant beacons for
command and control.

T1568.002.name: Possible FIN7 DGA Command and Control Behavior
T1568.002.description:
This rule detects a known command and control pattern in network events. The FIN7 threat group is known to use this
command and control technique, while maintaining persistence in their target's network.

T1568.002.name: Halfbaked Command and Control Beacon
T1568.002.description:
Halfbaked is a malware family used to establish persistence in a contested network. This rule detects a network activity
algorithm leveraged by Halfbaked implant beacons for command and control.

T1568.002.name: Connection to Commonly Abused Web Services
T1568.002.description:
Adversaries may implement command and control (C2) communications that use common web services to hide their activity.
This attack technique is typically targeted at an organization and uses web services common to the victim network, which
allows the adversary to blend into legitimate traffic activi

T1568.002.name: Machine Learning Detected DGA activity using a known SUNBURST DNS domain
T1568.002.description:
A supervised machine learning model has identified a DNS question name that used by the SUNBURST malware and is
predicted to be the result of a Domain Generation Algorithm.

T1568.002.name: Machine Learning Detected a DNS Request With a High DGA Probability Score
T1568.002.description:
A supervised machine learning model has identified a DNS question name with a high probability of sourcing from a Domain
Generation Algorithm (DGA), which could indicate command and control network activity.

T1568.002.name: Machine Learning Detected a DNS Request Predicted to be a DGA Domain
T1568.002.description:
A supervised machine learning model has identified a DNS question name that is predicted to be the result of a Domain
Generation Algorithm (DGA), which could indicate command and control network activity.

T1568.name: Cobalt Strike Command and Control Beacon
T1568.description:
Cobalt Strike is a threat emulation platform commonly modified and used by adversaries to conduct network attack and
exploitation campaigns. This rule detects a network activity algorithm leveraged by Cobalt Strike implant beacons for
command and control.

T1568.name: Possible FIN7 DGA Command and Control Behavior
T1568.description:
This rule detects a known command and control pattern in network events. The FIN7 threat group is known to use this
command and control technique, while maintaining persistence in their target's network.

T1568.name: Halfbaked Command and Control Beacon
T1568.description:
Halfbaked is a malware family used to establish persistence in a contested network. This rule detects a network activity
algorithm leveraged by Halfbaked implant beacons for command and control.

T1568.name: Connection to Commonly Abused Web Services
T1568.description:
Adversaries may implement command and control (C2) communications that use common web services to hide their activity.
This attack technique is typically targeted at an organization and uses web services common to the victim network, which
allows the adversary to blend into legitimate traffic activi

T1568.name: Machine Learning Detected DGA activity using a known SUNBURST DNS domain
T1568.description:
A supervised machine learning model has identified a DNS question name that used by the SUNBURST malware and is
predicted to be the result of a Domain Generation Algorithm.

T1568.name: Potential DGA Activity
T1568.description:
A population analysis machine learning job detected potential DGA (domain generation algorithm) activity. Such activity
is often used by malware command and control (C2) channels. This machine learning job looks for a source IP address
making DNS requests that have an aggregate high probability of b

T1568.name: Machine Learning Detected a DNS Request With a High DGA Probability Score
T1568.description:
A supervised machine learning model has identified a DNS question name with a high probability of sourcing from a Domain
Generation Algorithm (DGA), which could indicate command and control network activity.

T1568.name: Machine Learning Detected a DNS Request Predicted to be a DGA Domain
T1568.description:
A supervised machine learning model has identified a DNS question name that is predicted to be the result of a Domain
Generation Algorithm (DGA), which could indicate command and control network activity.

T1219.name: VNC (Virtual Network Computing) from the Internet
T1219.description:
This rule detects network events that may indicate the use of VNC traffic from the Internet. VNC is commonly used by
system administrators to remotely control a system for maintenance or to use shared resources. It should almost never be
directly exposed to the Internet, as it is frequently targeted

T1219.name: VNC (Virtual Network Computing) to the Internet
T1219.description:
This rule detects network events that may indicate the use of VNC traffic to the Internet. VNC is commonly used by
system administrators to remotely control a system for maintenance or to use shared resources. It should almost never be
directly exposed to the Internet, as it is frequently targeted a

T1219.name: First Time Seen Commonly Abused Remote Access Tool Execution
T1219.description:
Adversaries may install legitimate remote access tools (RAT) to compromised endpoints for further command-and-control
(C2). Adversaries can rely on installed RATs for persistence, execution of native commands and more. This rule detects
when a process is started whose name or code signature resemble

T1219.name: Potential REMCOS Trojan Execution
T1219.description: Identifies known execution traces of the REMCOS Remote Access Trojan. Remcos RAT is used by attackers to perform actions on infected machines remotely.

T1219.name: NetSupport Manager Execution from an Unusual Path
T1219.description:
Identifies execution of the NetSupport remote access software from non-default paths. Adversaries may abuse NetSupport
Manager to control a target victim machine.

T1219.name: Suspicious ScreenConnect Client Child Process
T1219.description:
Identifies suspicious processes being spawned by the ScreenConnect client processes. This activity may indicate
execution abusing unauthorized access to the ScreenConnect remote access software.

T1219.name: Remote File Copy via TeamViewer
T1219.description: Identifies an executable or script file remotely downloaded via a TeamViewer transfer session.

T1219.name: Attempt to Establish VScode Remote Tunnel
T1219.description:
Detects the execution of the VScode portable binary with the tunnel command line option indicating an attempt to
establish a remote tunnel session to Github or a remote VScode instance.

T1003.001.name: Credential Dumping - Detected - Elastic Endgame
T1003.001.description:
Elastic Endgame detected Credential Dumping. Click the Elastic Endgame icon in the event.module column or the link in
the rule.reference column for additional information.

T1003.001.name: Credential Dumping - Prevented - Elastic Endgame
T1003.001.description:
Elastic Endgame prevented Credential Dumping. Click the Elastic Endgame icon in the event.module column or the link in
the rule.reference column for additional information.

T1003.001.name: Potential Credential Access via Windows Utilities
T1003.001.description:
Identifies the execution of known Windows utilities often abused to dump LSASS memory or the Active Directory database
(NTDS.dit) in preparation for credential access.

T1003.001.name: Full User-Mode Dumps Enabled System-Wide
T1003.001.description:
Identifies the enable of the full user-mode dumps feature system-wide. This feature allows Windows Error Reporting (WER)
to collect data after an application crashes. This setting is a requirement for the LSASS Shtinkering attack, which
fakes the communication of a crash on LSASS, generating a dump

T1003.001.name: Suspicious LSASS Access via MalSecLogon
T1003.001.description:
Identifies suspicious access to LSASS handle from a call trace pointing to seclogon.dll and with a suspicious access
rights value. This may indicate an attempt to leak an LSASS handle via abusing the Secondary Logon service in
preparation for credential access.

T1003.001.name: Suspicious Module Loaded by LSASS
T1003.001.description:
Identifies LSASS loading an unsigned or untrusted DLL. Windows Security Support Provider (SSP) DLLs are loaded into
LSSAS process at system start. Once loaded into the LSA, SSP DLLs have access to encrypted and plaintext passwords that
are stored in Windows, such as any logged-on user's Domain passw

T1003.001.name: LSASS Memory Dump Creation
T1003.001.description:
Identifies the creation of a Local Security Authority Subsystem Service (lsass.exe) default memory dump. This may
indicate a credential access attempt via trusted system utilities such as Task Manager (taskmgr.exe) and SQL Dumper
(sqldumper.exe) or known pentesting tools such as Dumpert and AndrewSp

T1003.001.name: LSASS Memory Dump Handle Access
T1003.001.description:
Identifies handle requests for the Local Security Authority Subsystem Service (LSASS) object access with specific access
masks that many tools with a capability to dump memory to disk use (0x1fffff, 0x1010, 0x120089). This rule is tool
agnostic as it has been validated against a host of various LSAS

T1003.001.name: LSASS Process Access via Windows API
T1003.001.description: Identifies access attempts to the LSASS handle, which may indicate an attempt to dump credentials from LSASS memory.

T1003.001.name: Potential Invoke-Mimikatz PowerShell Script
T1003.001.description:
Mimikatz is a credential dumper capable of obtaining plaintext Windows account logins and passwords, along with many
other features that make it useful for testing the security of networks. This rule detects Invoke-Mimikatz PowerShell
script and alike.

T1003.001.name: Modification of WDigest Security Provider
T1003.001.description:
Identifies attempts to modify the WDigest security provider in the registry to force the user's password to be stored in
clear text in memory. This behavior can be indicative of an adversary attempting to weaken the security configuration of
an endpoint. Once the UseLogonCredential value is modified

T1003.001.name: PowerShell MiniDump Script
T1003.001.description:
This rule detects PowerShell scripts capable of dumping process memory using WindowsErrorReporting or Dbghelp.dll
MiniDumpWriteDump. Attackers can use this tooling to dump LSASS and get access to credentials.

T1003.001.name: Potential Credential Access via DuplicateHandle in LSASS
T1003.001.description:
Identifies suspicious access to an LSASS handle via DuplicateHandle from an unknown call trace module. This may indicate
an attempt to bypass the NtOpenProcess API to evade detection and dump LSASS memory for credential access.

T1003.001.name: Potential Credential Access via Renamed COM+ Services DLL
T1003.001.description:
Identifies suspicious renamed COMSVCS.DLL Image Load, which exports the MiniDump function that can be used to dump a
process memory. This may indicate an attempt to dump LSASS memory while bypassing command-line based detection in
preparation for credential access.

T1003.001.name: Suspicious Lsass Process Access
T1003.001.description: Identifies access attempts to LSASS handle, this may indicate an attempt to dump credentials from Lsass memory.

T1003.001.name: Potential Credential Access via LSASS Memory Dump
T1003.001.description:
Identifies suspicious access to LSASS handle from a call trace pointing to DBGHelp.dll or DBGCore.dll, which both export
the MiniDumpWriteDump method that can be used to dump LSASS memory content in preparation for credential access.

T1003.001.name: Potential LSASS Memory Dump via PssCaptureSnapShot
T1003.001.description:
Identifies suspicious access to an LSASS handle via PssCaptureSnapShot where two successive process accesses are
performed by the same process and target two different instances of LSASS. This may indicate an attempt to evade
detection and dump LSASS memory for credential access.

T1003.001.name: Potential LSASS Clone Creation via PssCaptureSnapShot
T1003.001.description:
Identifies the creation of an LSASS process clone via PssCaptureSnapShot where the parent process is the initial LSASS
process instance. This may indicate an attempt to evade detection and dump LSASS memory for credential access.

T1134.name: Credential Manipulation - Detected - Elastic Endgame
T1134.description:
Elastic Endgame detected Credential Manipulation. Click the Elastic Endgame icon in the event.module column or the link
in the rule.reference column for additional information.

T1134.name: Credential Manipulation - Prevented - Elastic Endgame
T1134.description:
Elastic Endgame prevented Credential Manipulation. Click the Elastic Endgame icon in the event.module column or the link
in the rule.reference column for additional information.

T1134.name: Permission Theft - Detected - Elastic Endgame
T1134.description:
Elastic Endgame detected Permission Theft. Click the Elastic Endgame icon in the event.module column or the link in the
rule.reference column for additional information.

T1134.name: Permission Theft - Prevented - Elastic Endgame
T1134.description:
Elastic Endgame prevented Permission Theft. Click the Elastic Endgame icon in the event.module column or the link in the
rule.reference column for additional information.

T1134.name: Parent Process PID Spoofing
T1134.description:
Identifies parent process spoofing used to thwart detection. Adversaries may spoof the parent process identifier (PPID)
of a new process to evade process-monitoring defenses or to elevate privileges.

T1134.name: Process Creation via Secondary Logon
T1134.description:
Identifies process creation with alternate credentials. Adversaries may create a new process with a different token to
escalate privileges and bypass access controls.

T1134.name: Process Created with a Duplicated Token
T1134.description:
Identifies the creation of a process impersonating the token of another user logon session. Adversaries may create a new
process with a different token to escalate privileges and bypass access controls.

T1134.name: Interactive Logon by an Unusual Process
T1134.description:
Identifies interactive logon attempt with alternate credentials and by an unusual process. Adversaries may create a new
token to escalate privileges and bypass access controls.

T1134.name: Privilege Escalation via Named Pipe Impersonation
T1134.description:
Identifies a privilege escalation attempt via named pipe impersonation. An adversary may abuse this technique by
utilizing a framework such Metasploit's meterpreter getsystem command.

T1134.name: First Time Seen NewCredentials Logon Process
T1134.description:
Identifies a new credentials logon type performed by an unusual process. This may indicate the existence of an access
token forging capability that are often abused to bypass access control restrictions.

T1134.name: PowerShell Script with Token Impersonation Capabilities
T1134.description:
Detects scripts that contain PowerShell functions, structures, or Windows API functions related to token
impersonation/theft. Attackers may duplicate then impersonate another user's token to escalate privileges and bypass
access controls.

T1134.name: Suspicious SeIncreaseBasePriorityPrivilege Use
T1134.description:
Identifies attempts to use the SeIncreaseBasePriorityPrivilege privilege by an unusual process. This could be related to
hijack execution flow of a process via threats priority manipulation.

T1134.name: SeDebugPrivilege Enabled by a Suspicious Process
T1134.description:
Identifies a process running with a non-SYSTEM account that enables the SeDebugPrivilege privilege. Adversaries may
enable this privilege to debug and modify other processes, typically reserved for system-level tasks, to escalate
privileges and bypass access controls.

T1134.name: Privileges Elevation via Parent Process PID Spoofing
T1134.description:
Identifies parent process spoofing used to create an elevated child process. Adversaries may spoof the parent process
identifier (PPID) of a new process to evade process-monitoring defenses or to elevate privileges.

T1134.name: Privilege Escalation via Rogue Named Pipe Impersonation
T1134.description:
Identifies a privilege escalation attempt via rogue named pipe impersonation. An adversary may abuse this technique by
masquerading as a known named pipe and manipulating a privileged process to connect to it.

T1134.name: Process Created with an Elevated Token
T1134.description:
Identifies the creation of a process running as SYSTEM and impersonating a Windows core binary privileges. Adversaries
may create a new process with a different token to escalate privileges and bypass access controls.

T1114.001.name: Suspicious Inter-Process Communication via Outlook
T1114.001.description:
Detects Inter-Process Communication with Outlook via Component Object Model from an unusual process. Adversaries may
target user email to collect sensitive information or send email on their behalf via API.

T1114.001.name: Exchange Mailbox Export via PowerShell
T1114.001.description:
Identifies the use of the Exchange PowerShell cmdlet, New-MailBoxExportRequest, to export the contents of a primary
mailbox or archive to a .pst file. Adversaries may target user email to collect sensitive information.

T1114.001.name: PowerShell Mailbox Collection Script
T1114.001.description:
Detects PowerShell scripts that can be used to collect data from mailboxes. Adversaries may target user email to collect
sensitive information.

T1114.name: Suspicious Inter-Process Communication via Outlook
T1114.description:
Detects Inter-Process Communication with Outlook via Component Object Model from an unusual process. Adversaries may
target user email to collect sensitive information or send email on their behalf via API.

T1114.name: Exporting Exchange Mailbox via PowerShell
T1114.description:
Identifies the use of the Exchange PowerShell cmdlet, New-MailBoxExportRequest, to export the contents of a primary
mailbox or archive to a .pst file. Adversaries may target user email to collect sensitive information.

T1114.name: Exchange Mailbox Export via PowerShell
T1114.description:
Identifies the use of the Exchange PowerShell cmdlet, New-MailBoxExportRequest, to export the contents of a primary
mailbox or archive to a .pst file. Adversaries may target user email to collect sensitive information.

T1114.name: PowerShell Mailbox Collection Script
T1114.description:
Detects PowerShell scripts that can be used to collect data from mailboxes. Adversaries may target user email to collect
sensitive information.

T1114.name: Microsoft Graph Request Email Access by Unusual User and Client
T1114.description:
Identifies access to email resources via Microsoft Graph API using an first-party application on behalf of a user
principal. This behavior may indicate an adversary using a phished OAuth refresh token or a Primary Refresh Token (PRT)
to access email resources. The pattern includes requests to Micros

T1114.name: Google Workspace Custom Gmail Route Created or Modified
T1114.description:
Detects when a custom Gmail route is added or modified in Google Workspace. Adversaries can add a custom e-mail route
for outbound mail to route these e-mails to their own inbox of choice for data gathering. This allows adversaries to
capture sensitive information from e-mail and potential attachmen

T1114.name: M365 Exchange Mailbox Items Accessed Excessively
T1114.description:
Identifies an excessive number of Microsoft 365 mailbox items accessed by a user either via aggregated counts or
throttling. Microsoft audits mailbox access via the MailItemsAccessed event, which is triggered when a user accesses
mailbox items. If more than 1000 mailbox items are accessed within a 2

T1114.name: M365 Exchange Mailbox Accessed by Unusual Client
T1114.description:
Identifies suspicious Microsoft 365 mail access by ClientAppId. This rule detects when a user accesses their mailbox
using a client application that is not typically used by the user, which may indicate potential compromise or
unauthorized access attempts. Adversaries may use custom or third-party a

T1114.name: M365 Exchange Inbox Forwarding Rule Created
T1114.description:
Identifies when a new Inbox forwarding rule is created in Microsoft 365. Inbox rules process messages in the Inbox based
on conditions and take actions. In this case, the rules will forward the emails to a defined address. Attackers can
abuse Inbox Rules to intercept and exfiltrate email data withou

T1559.001.name: Suspicious Inter-Process Communication via Outlook
T1559.001.description:
Detects Inter-Process Communication with Outlook via Component Object Model from an unusual process. Adversaries may
target user email to collect sensitive information or send email on their behalf via API.

T1559.001.name: Potential Command and Control via Internet Explorer
T1559.001.description:
Identifies instances of Internet Explorer (iexplore.exe) being started via the Component Object Model (COM) making
unusual network connections. Adversaries could abuse Internet Explorer via COM to avoid suspicious processes making
network connections and bypass host-based firewall restrictions.

T1559.001.name: Execution of COM object via Xwizard
T1559.001.description:
Windows Component Object Model (COM) is an inter-process communication (IPC) component of the native Windows application
programming interface (API) that enables interaction between software objects or executable code. Xwizard can be used to
run a COM object created in registry to evade defensive co

T1559.001.name: UAC Bypass Attempt with IEditionUpgradeManager Elevated COM Interface
T1559.001.description:
Identifies attempts to bypass User Account Control (UAC) by abusing an elevated COM Interface to launch a rogue Windows
ClipUp program. Attackers may attempt to bypass UAC to stealthily execute code with elevated permissions.

T1559.001.name: UAC Bypass Attempt via Elevated COM Internet Explorer Add-On Installer
T1559.001.description:
Identifies User Account Control (UAC) bypass attempts by abusing an elevated COM Interface to launch a malicious
program. Attackers may attempt to bypass UAC to stealthily execute code with elevated permissions.

T1559.001.name: UAC Bypass via ICMLuaUtil Elevated COM Interface
T1559.001.description:
Identifies User Account Control (UAC) bypass attempts via the ICMLuaUtil Elevated COM interface. Attackers may attempt
to bypass UAC to stealthily execute code with elevated permissions.

T1114.002.name: Exporting Exchange Mailbox via PowerShell
T1114.002.description:
Identifies the use of the Exchange PowerShell cmdlet, New-MailBoxExportRequest, to export the contents of a primary
mailbox or archive to a .pst file. Adversaries may target user email to collect sensitive information.

T1114.002.name: Exchange Mailbox Export via PowerShell
T1114.002.description:
Identifies the use of the Exchange PowerShell cmdlet, New-MailBoxExportRequest, to export the contents of a primary
mailbox or archive to a .pst file. Adversaries may target user email to collect sensitive information.

T1114.002.name: PowerShell Mailbox Collection Script
T1114.002.description:
Detects PowerShell scripts that can be used to collect data from mailboxes. Adversaries may target user email to collect
sensitive information.

T1114.002.name: M365 Exchange Mailbox Items Accessed Excessively
T1114.002.description:
Identifies an excessive number of Microsoft 365 mailbox items accessed by a user either via aggregated counts or
throttling. Microsoft audits mailbox access via the MailItemsAccessed event, which is triggered when a user accesses
mailbox items. If more than 1000 mailbox items are accessed within a 2

T1114.002.name: M365 Exchange Mailbox Accessed by Unusual Client
T1114.002.description:
Identifies suspicious Microsoft 365 mail access by ClientAppId. This rule detects when a user accesses their mailbox
using a client application that is not typically used by the user, which may indicate potential compromise or
unauthorized access attempts. Adversaries may use custom or third-party a

T1123.name: PowerShell Suspicious Script with Audio Capture Capabilities
T1123.description: Detects PowerShell scripts that can record audio, a common feature in popular post-exploitation tooling.

T1056.001.name: PowerShell Keylogging Script
T1056.001.description:
Detects the use of Win32 API Functions that can be used to capture user keystrokes in PowerShell scripts. Attackers use
this technique to capture user input, looking for credentials and/or other valuable data.

T1113.name: PowerShell Suspicious Script with Screenshot Capabilities
T1113.description:
Detects PowerShell scripts that can take screenshots, which is a common feature in post-exploitation kits and remote
access tools (RATs).

T1125.name: PowerShell Script with Webcam Video Capture Capabilities
T1125.description:
Detects PowerShell scripts that can be used to record webcam video. Attackers can capture this information to extort or
spy on victims.

T1567.name: Potential File Transfer via Certreq
T1567.description:
Identifies Certreq making an HTTP Post request. Adversaries could abuse Certreq to download files or upload data to a
remote URL.

T1567.name: Connection to Commonly Abused Web Services
T1567.description:
Adversaries may implement command and control (C2) communications that use common web services to hide their activity.
This attack technique is typically targeted at an organization and uses web services common to the victim network, which
allows the adversary to blend into legitimate traffic activi

T1567.name: AWS DynamoDB Scan by Unusual User
T1567.description:
Identifies when an AWS DynamoDB table is scanned by a user who does not typically perform this action. Adversaries may
use the Scan operation to collect sensitive information or exfiltrate data from DynamoDB tables. This rule detects
unusual user activity by monitoring for the Scan action in CloudTr

T1567.name: AWS DynamoDB Table Exported to S3
T1567.description: Identifies when an AWS DynamoDB table is exported to S3. Adversaries may use the ExportTableToPointInTime operation to collect sensitive information or exfiltrate data from DynamoDB tables. This rule detects unusual user activity by monitoring for the ExportTableToPointInTime action in CloudTrail lo

T1567.name: AWS SNS Rare Protocol Subscription by User
T1567.description: Identifies when a use subscribes to an SNS topic using a new protocol type (ie. email, http, lambda, etc.). SNS allows users to subscribe to recieve topic messages across a broad range of protocols like email, sms, lambda functions, http endpoints, and applications. Adversaries may subscribe to an S

T1567.name: AWS SNS Topic Message Publish by Rare User
T1567.description:
Identifies when an SNS topic message is published by a rare user in AWS. Adversaries may publish messages to SNS topics
for phishing campaigns, data exfiltration, or lateral movement within the AWS environment. SNS topics are used to send
notifications and messages to subscribed endpoints such as ap

T1567.name: Azure Storage Blob Retrieval via AzCopy
T1567.description:
Identifies successful GetBlob operations on Azure Storage Accounts using AzCopy user agent with SAS token
authentication. AzCopy is a command-line utility for copying data to and from Azure Storage. While legitimate for data
migration, adversaries may abuse AzCopy with compromised SAS tokens to exfi

T1090.002.name: Connection to Commonly Abused Web Services
T1090.002.description:
Adversaries may implement command and control (C2) communications that use common web services to hide their activity.
This attack technique is typically targeted at an organization and uses web services common to the victim network, which
allows the adversary to blend into legitimate traffic activi

T1567.001.name: Connection to Commonly Abused Web Services
T1567.001.description:
Adversaries may implement command and control (C2) communications that use common web services to hide their activity.
This attack technique is typically targeted at an organization and uses web services common to the victim network, which
allows the adversary to blend into legitimate traffic activi

T1567.002.name: Connection to Commonly Abused Web Services
T1567.002.description:
Adversaries may implement command and control (C2) communications that use common web services to hide their activity.
This attack technique is typically targeted at an organization and uses web services common to the victim network, which
allows the adversary to blend into legitimate traffic activi

T1567.002.name: AWS DynamoDB Table Exported to S3
T1567.002.description: Identifies when an AWS DynamoDB table is exported to S3. Adversaries may use the ExportTableToPointInTime operation to collect sensitive information or exfiltrate data from DynamoDB tables. This rule detects unusual user activity by monitoring for the ExportTableToPointInTime action in CloudTrail lo

T1567.002.name: Azure Storage Blob Retrieval via AzCopy
T1567.002.description:
Identifies successful GetBlob operations on Azure Storage Accounts using AzCopy user agent with SAS token
authentication. AzCopy is a command-line utility for copying data to and from Azure Storage. While legitimate for data
migration, adversaries may abuse AzCopy with compromised SAS tokens to exfi

T1573.name: Connection to Commonly Abused Free SSL Certificate Providers
T1573.description:
Identifies unusual processes connecting to domains using known free SSL certificates. Adversaries may employ a known
encryption algorithm to conceal command and control traffic.

T1197.name: Ingress Transfer via Windows BITS
T1197.description:
Identifies downloads of executable and archive files via the Windows Background Intelligent Transfer Service (BITS).
Adversaries could leverage Windows BITS transfer jobs to download remote payloads.

T1197.name: Persistence via BITS Job Notify Cmdline
T1197.description:
An adversary can use the Background Intelligent Transfer Service (BITS) SetNotifyCmdLine method to execute a program
that runs after a job finishes transferring data or after a job enters a specified state in order to persist on a
system.

T1137.004.name: Outlook Home Page Registry Modification
T1137.004.description:
Identifies modifications in registry keys associated with abuse of the Outlook Home Page functionality for command and
control or persistence.

T1137.name: Outlook Home Page Registry Modification
T1137.description:
Identifies modifications in registry keys associated with abuse of the Outlook Home Page functionality for command and
control or persistence.

T1137.name: Suspicious Execution via Microsoft Office Add-Ins
T1137.description:
Identifies execution of common Microsoft Office applications to launch an Office Add-In from a suspicious path or with
an unusual parent process. This may indicate an attempt to get initial access via a malicious phishing MS Office Add-In.

T1137.name: Persistence via Microsoft Office AddIns
T1137.description: Detects attempts to establish persistence on an endpoint by abusing Microsoft Office add-ins.

T1137.name: Persistence via Microsoft Outlook VBA
T1137.description: Detects attempts to establish persistence on an endpoint by installing a rogue Microsoft Outlook VBA Template.

T1137.name: Office Test Registry Persistence
T1137.description:
Identifies the modification of the Microsoft Office "Office Test" Registry key, a registry location that can be used to
specify a DLL which will be executed every time an MS Office application is started. Attackers can abuse this to gain
persistence on a compromised host.

T1112.name: Port Forwarding Rule Addition
T1112.description:
Identifies the creation of a new port forwarding rule. An adversary may abuse this technique to bypass network
segmentation restrictions.

T1112.name: Full User-Mode Dumps Enabled System-Wide
T1112.description:
Identifies the enable of the full user-mode dumps feature system-wide. This feature allows Windows Error Reporting (WER)
to collect data after an application crashes. This setting is a requirement for the LSASS Shtinkering attack, which
fakes the communication of a crash on LSASS, generating a dump

T1112.name: Modification of AmsiEnable Registry Key
T1112.description:
Identifies modifications of the AmsiEnable registry key to 0, which disables the Antimalware Scan Interface (AMSI). An
adversary can modify this key to disable AMSI protections.

T1112.name: Code Signing Policy Modification Through Registry
T1112.description:
Identifies attempts to disable the code signing policy through the registry. Code signing provides authenticity on a
program, and grants the user with the ability to check whether the program has been tampered with. By allowing the
execution of unsigned or self-signed code, threat actors can craft a

T1112.name: Windows Defender Disabled via Registry Modification
T1112.description:
Identifies modifications to the Windows Defender registry settings to disable the service or set the service to be
started manually.

T1112.name: Network-Level Authentication (NLA) Disabled
T1112.description:
Identifies the attempt to disable Network-Level Authentication (NLA) via registry modification. Network Level
Authentication (NLA) is a feature on Windows that provides an extra layer of security for Remote Desktop (RDP)
connections, as it requires users to authenticate before allowing a full RDP se

T1112.name: PowerShell Script Block Logging Disabled
T1112.description:
Identifies attempts to disable PowerShell Script Block Logging via registry modification. Attackers may disable this
logging to conceal their activities in the host and evade detection.

T1112.name: DNS-over-HTTPS Enabled via Registry
T1112.description:
Identifies when a user enables DNS-over-HTTPS. This can be used to hide internet activity or the process of exfiltrating
data. With this enabled, an organization will lose visibility into data such as query type, response, and originating
IP, which are used to determine bad actors.

T1112.name: Encoded Executable Stored in the Registry
T1112.description:
Identifies registry write modifications to hide an encoded portable executable. This could be indicative of adversary
defense evasion by avoiding the storing of malicious content directly on disk.

T1112.name: Disabling Lsa Protection via Registry Modification
T1112.description:
LSA protecton is provided to prevent nonprotected processes from reading memory and injecting code. This feature provides added security for the credentials that LSA stores and manages.
Adversaries may modify the RunAsPPL registry and wait or initiate a system restart to enable Lsass credentials acc

T1112.name: Microsoft Windows Defender Tampering
T1112.description:
Identifies when one or more features on Microsoft Defender are disabled. Adversaries may disable or tamper with
Microsoft Defender features to evade detection and conceal malicious behavior.

T1112.name: MS Office Macro Security Registry Modifications
T1112.description:
Microsoft Office Products offer options for users and developers to control the security settings for running and using
Macros. Adversaries may abuse these security settings to modify the default behavior of the Office Application to trust
future macros and/or disable security warnings, which could

T1112.name: Potential NetNTLMv1 Downgrade Attack
T1112.description:
Identifies registry modification to force the system to fall back to NTLMv1 for authentication. This modification is
possible with local administrator privileges and is commonly referred to as a `NetNTLMv1 downgrade attack`.

T1112.name: Local Account TokenFilter Policy Disabled
T1112.description:
Identifies registry modification to the LocalAccountTokenFilterPolicy policy. If this value exists (which doesn't by
default) and is set to 1, then remote connections from all local members of Administrators are granted full
high-integrity tokens during negotiation.

T1112.name: Potential RemoteMonologue Attack
T1112.description:
Identifies attempt to perform session hijack via COM object registry modification by setting the RunAs value to
Interactive User.

T1112.name: SolarWinds Process Disabling Services via Registry
T1112.description:
Identifies a SolarWinds binary modifying the start type of a service to be disabled. An adversary may abuse this
technique to manipulate relevant security services.

T1112.name: Windows Subsystem for Linux Distribution Installed
T1112.description:
Detects changes to the registry that indicates the install of a new Windows Subsystem for Linux distribution by name.
Adversaries may enable and use WSL for Linux to avoid detection.

T1112.name: NullSessionPipe Registry Modification
T1112.description:
Identifies NullSessionPipe registry modifications that specify which pipes can be accessed anonymously. This could be
