indicative of adversary lateral movement preparation by making the added pipe available to everyone.

T1112.name: RDP Enabled via Registry
T1112.description:
Identifies registry write modifications to enable Remote Desktop Protocol (RDP) access. This could be indicative of
adversary lateral movement preparation.

T1112.name: Registry Persistence via AppInit DLL
T1112.description:
AppInit DLLs are dynamic-link libraries (DLLs) that are loaded into every process that creates a user interface (loads
user32.dll) on Microsoft Windows operating systems. The AppInit DLL mechanism is used to load custom code into user-mode
processes, allowing for the customization of the user interf

T1112.name: Image File Execution Options Injection
T1112.description:
The Debugger and SilentProcessExit registry keys can allow an adversary to intercept the execution of files, causing a
different process to be executed. This functionality can be abused by an adversary to establish persistence.

T1112.name: Suspicious Startup Shell Folder Modification
T1112.description:
Identifies suspicious startup shell folder modifications to change the default Startup directory in order to bypass
detections monitoring file creation in the Windows Startup folder.

T1112.name: Office Test Registry Persistence
T1112.description:
Identifies the modification of the Microsoft Office "Office Test" Registry key, a registry location that can be used to
specify a DLL which will be executed every time an MS Office application is started. Attackers can abuse this to gain
persistence on a compromised host.

T1112.name: Netsh Helper DLL
T1112.description:
Identifies the addition of a Netsh Helper DLL, netsh.exe supports the addition of these DLLs to extend its
functionality. Attackers may abuse this mechanism to execute malicious payloads every time the utility is executed,
which can be done by administrators or a scheduled task.

T1112.name: Uncommon Registry Persistence Change
T1112.description:
Detects changes to registry persistence keys that are not commonly used or modified by legitimate programs. This could
be an indication of an adversary's attempt to persist in a stealthy manner.

T1112.name: Unusual Persistence via Services Registry
T1112.description:
Identifies processes modifying the services registry key directly, instead of through the expected Windows APIs. This
could be an indication of an adversary attempting to stealthily persist through abnormal service creation or
modification of an existing service.

T1112.name: Component Object Model Hijacking
T1112.description:
Identifies Component Object Model (COM) hijacking via registry modification. Adversaries may establish persistence by
executing malicious content triggered by hijacked references to COM objects.

T1112.name: Suspicious ImagePath Service Creation
T1112.description:
Identifies the creation of a suspicious ImagePath value. This could be an indication of an adversary attempting to
stealthily persist or escalate privileges through abnormal service creation.

T1112.name: Persistence via Hidden Run Key Detected
T1112.description:
Identifies a persistence mechanism that utilizes the NtSetValueKey native API to create a hidden (null terminated)
registry key. An adversary may use this method to hide from system utilities such as the Registry Editor (regedit).

T1112.name: Installation of Security Support Provider
T1112.description:
Identifies registry modifications related to the Windows Security Support Provider (SSP) configuration. Adversaries may
abuse this to establish persistence in an environment.

T1112.name: Werfault ReflectDebugger Persistence
T1112.description:
Identifies the registration of a Werfault Debugger. Attackers may abuse this mechanism to execute malicious payloads
every time the utility is executed with the "-pr" parameter.

T1112.name: Disabling User Account Control via Registry Modification
T1112.description:
User Account Control (UAC) can help mitigate the impact of malware on Windows hosts. With UAC, apps and tasks always run
in the security context of a non-administrator account, unless an administrator specifically authorizes
administrator-level access to the system. This rule identifies registry val

T1059.005.name: Remote File Download via Script Interpreter
T1059.005.description:
Identifies built-in Windows script interpreters (cscript.exe or wscript.exe) being used to download an executable file
from a remote destination.

T1059.005.name: Suspicious .NET Code Compilation
T1059.005.description:
Identifies executions of .NET compilers with suspicious parent processes, which can indicate an attacker's attempt to
compile code after delivery in order to bypass security mechanisms.

T1059.005.name: Microsoft Build Engine Started by a Script Process
T1059.005.description:
An instance of MSBuild, the Microsoft Build Engine, was started by a script or the Windows command interpreter. This
behavior is unusual and is sometimes used by malicious payloads.

T1059.005.name: Delayed Execution via Ping
T1059.005.description:
Identifies the execution of commonly abused Windows utilities via a delayed Ping execution. This behavior is often
observed during malware installation and is consistent with an attacker attempting to evade detection.

T1059.005.name: Command and Scripting Interpreter via Windows Scripts
T1059.005.description: Identifies PowerShell.exe or Cmd.exe execution spawning from Windows Script Host processes Wscript.exe.

T1059.005.name: Windows Script Execution from Archive
T1059.005.description:
Identifies attempts to execute Jscript/Vbscript files from an archive file. The use of archives is a common delivery method
of malicious scripts.

T1059.005.name: Microsoft Management Console File from Unusual Path
T1059.005.description:
Identifies attempts to open a Microsoft Management Console File from untrusted paths. Adversaries may use MSC files for
initial access and execution.

T1059.005.name: Execution of a Downloaded Windows Script
T1059.005.description:
Identifies the creation of a Windows script downloaded from the internet followed by the execution of a scripting utility.
Adversaries may use Windows script files for initial access and execution.

T1059.005.name: Windows Script Executing PowerShell
T1059.005.description:
Identifies a PowerShell process launched by either cscript.exe or wscript.exe. Observing Windows scripting processes
executing a PowerShell script, may be indicative of malicious activity.

T1059.005.name: Windows Script Interpreter Executing Process via WMI
T1059.005.description:
Identifies use of the built-in Windows script interpreters (cscript.exe or wscript.exe) being used to execute a process
via Windows Management Instrumentation (WMI). This may be indicative of malicious activity.

T1059.005.name: Suspicious Explorer Child Process
T1059.005.description:
Identifies a suspicious Windows explorer child process. Explorer.exe can be abused to launch malicious scripts or
executables from a trusted parent process.

T1059.005.name: Scheduled Task Created by a Windows Script
T1059.005.description:
A scheduled task was created by a Windows script via cscript.exe, wscript.exe or powershell.exe. This can be abused by
an adversary to establish persistence.

T1059.005.name: Web Shell Detection: Script Process Child of Common Web Processes
T1059.005.description: Identifies suspicious commands executed via a web server, which may suggest a vulnerability and remote shell access.

T1059.005.name: Service Control Spawned via Script Interpreter
T1059.005.description:
Identifies Service Control (sc.exe) spawning from script interpreter processes to create, modify, or start services.
This can potentially indicate an attempt to elevate privileges or maintain persistence.

T1557.name: Potential ADIDNS Poisoning via Wildcard Record Creation
T1557.description:
Active Directory Integrated DNS (ADIDNS) is one of the core components of AD DS, leveraging AD's access control and
replication to maintain domain consistency. It stores DNS zones as AD objects, a feature that, while robust, introduces
some security issues, such as wildcard records, mainly because o

T1557.name: Potential WPAD Spoofing via DNS Record Creation
T1557.description:
Identifies the creation of a DNS record that is potentially meant to enable WPAD spoofing. Attackers can disable the
Global Query Block List (GQBL) and create a "wpad" record to exploit hosts running WPAD with default settings for
privilege escalation and lateral movement.

T1557.name: Creation of a DNS-Named Record
T1557.description:
Active Directory Integrated DNS (ADIDNS) is one of the core components of AD DS, leveraging AD's access control and
replication to maintain domain consistency. It stores DNS zones as AD objects, a feature that, while robust, introduces
some security issues because of the default permission (Any auth

T1557.name: Potential Computer Account NTLM Relay Activity
T1557.description:
Identifies potential relay activities against a Computer account by identifying authentication events using the computer
account coming from from hosts other than the server that owns the account. Attackers may relay the computer account
hash after capturing it using forced authentication.

T1557.name: Potential Kerberos Relay Attack against a Computer Account
T1557.description:
Detects potential relay attacks by identifying coercion attempts followed by authentication events using a target
server's computer account, originating from a different host. This may indicate that an attacker has captured and
relayed the server's computer account hash to execute code on behalf of

T1557.name: Potential NTLM Relay Attack against a Computer Account
T1557.description:
Detects potential relay attacks by identifying coercion attempts followed by authentication events using a target
server's computer account, originating from a different host. This may indicate that an attacker has captured and
relayed the server's computer account hash to execute code on behalf of

T1557.name: Potential Kerberos Coercion via DNS-Based SPN Spoofing
T1557.description:
Identifies the creation of a DNS record containing a base64-encoded blob matching the pattern "UWhRCA...BAAAA". This
pattern corresponds to a marshaled CREDENTIAL_TARGET_INFORMATION structure, commonly used in Kerberos coercion attacks.
It is associated with tools and techniques that exploit SPN spo

T1557.name: Potential Kerberos SPN Spoofing via Suspicious DNS Query
T1557.description:
Identifies queries to a DNS record containing a base64-encoded blob matching the pattern "UWhRCA...BAAAA". This pattern
corresponds to a marshaled CREDENTIAL_TARGET_INFORMATION structure, commonly used in Kerberos coercion attacks. It is
associated with tools and techniques that exploit SPN spoofing

T1557.name: Potential Machine Account Relay Attack via SMB
T1557.description:
Identifies potential relay attacks against a machine account by identifying network share access events coming from a
remote source.ip but using the target server computer account. This may indicate a successful SMB relay attack.

T1557.name: Potential PowerShell Pass-the-Hash/Relay Script
T1557.description:
Detects PowerShell scripts that can execute pass-the-hash (PtH) attacks, intercept and relay NTLM challenges, and carry
out other man-in-the-middle (MitM) attacks.

T1557.name: DNS Global Query Block List Modified or Disabled
T1557.description:
Identifies changes to the DNS Global Query Block List (GQBL), a security feature that prevents the resolution of certain
DNS names often exploited in attacks like WPAD spoofing. Attackers with certain privileges, such as DNSAdmins, can
modify or disable the GQBL, allowing exploitation of hosts runni

T1003.003.name: Potential Credential Access via Windows Utilities
T1003.003.description:
Identifies the execution of known Windows utilities often abused to dump LSASS memory or the Active Directory database
(NTDS.dit) in preparation for credential access.

T1003.003.name: NTDS or SAM Database File Copied
T1003.003.description:
Identifies a copy operation of the Active Directory Domain Database (ntds.dit) or Security Account Manager (SAM) files.
Those files contain sensitive information including hashed domain and/or local credentials.

T1003.003.name: PowerShell Invoke-NinjaCopy script
T1003.003.description:
Detects PowerShell scripts that contain the default exported functions used on Invoke-NinjaCopy. Attackers can use
Invoke-NinjaCopy to read SYSTEM files that are normally locked, such as the NTDS.dit file or registry hives.

T1003.003.name: Symbolic Link to Shadow Copy Created
T1003.003.description:
Identifies the creation of symbolic links to a shadow copy. Symbolic links can be used to access files in the shadow
copy, including sensitive files such as ntds.dit, System Boot Key and browser offline credentials.

T1003.003.name: NTDS Dump via Wbadmin
T1003.003.description:
Identifies the execution of wbadmin to access the NTDS.dit file in a domain controller. Attackers with privileges from
groups like Backup Operators can abuse the utility to perform credential access and compromise the domain.

T1218.011.name: Potential Credential Access via Windows Utilities
T1218.011.description:
Identifies the execution of known Windows utilities often abused to dump LSASS memory or the Active Directory database
(NTDS.dit) in preparation for credential access.

T1218.011.name: Potential Local NTLM Relay via HTTP
T1218.011.description:
Identifies attempt to coerce a local NTLM authentication via HTTP using the Windows Printer Spooler service as a target.
An adversary may use this primitive in combination with other techniques to elevate privileges on a compromised system.

T1218.011.name: Potential Credential Access via Renamed COM+ Services DLL
T1218.011.description:
Identifies suspicious renamed COMSVCS.DLL Image Load, which exports the MiniDump function that can be used to dump a
process memory. This may indicate an attempt to dump LSASS memory while bypassing command-line based detection in
preparation for credential access.

T1218.011.name: Unusual Child Processes of RunDLL32
T1218.011.description:
Identifies child processes of unusual instances of RunDLL32 where the command line parameters were suspicious. Misuse of
RunDLL32 could indicate malicious activity.

T1218.011.name: Script Execution via Microsoft HTML Application
T1218.011.description:
Identifies the execution of scripts via HTML applications using Windows utilities rundll32.exe or mshta.exe. Adversaries
may bypass process and/or signature-based defenses by proxying execution of malicious content with signed binaries.

T1218.011.name: Suspicious Execution from a Mounted Device
T1218.011.description:
Identifies when a script interpreter or signed binary is launched via a non-standard working directory. An attacker may
use this technique to evade defenses.

T1218.011.name: Unusual Network Connection via RunDLL32
T1218.011.description:
Identifies unusual instances of rundll32.exe making outbound network connections. This may indicate adversarial Command
and Control activity.

T1218.011.name: Command Shell Activity Started via RunDLL32
T1218.011.description: Identifies command shell activity started via RunDLL32, which is commonly abused by attackers to host malicious code.

T1218.011.name: Delayed Execution via Ping
T1218.011.description:
Identifies the execution of commonly abused Windows utilities via a delayed Ping execution. This behavior is often
observed during malware installation and is consistent with an attacker attempting to evade detection.

T1218.011.name: Service Control Spawned via Script Interpreter
T1218.011.description:
Identifies Service Control (sc.exe) spawning from script interpreter processes to create, modify, or start services.
This can potentially indicate an attempt to elevate privileges or maintain persistence.

T1003.002.name: NTDS or SAM Database File Copied
T1003.002.description:
Identifies a copy operation of the Active Directory Domain Database (ntds.dit) or Security Account Manager (SAM) files.
Those files contain sensitive information including hashed domain and/or local credentials.

T1003.002.name: Potential Credential Access via Trusted Developer Utility
T1003.002.description:
An instance of MSBuild, the Microsoft Build Engine, loaded DLLs (dynamically linked libraries) responsible for Windows
credential management. This technique is sometimes used for credential dumping.

T1003.002.name: Credential Acquisition via Registry Hive Dumping
T1003.002.description: Identifies attempts to export a registry hive which may contain credentials using the Windows reg.exe tool.

T1003.002.name: Windows Registry File Creation in SMB Share
T1003.002.description:
Identifies the creation or modification of a medium-size registry hive file on a Server Message Block (SMB) share, which
may indicate an exfiltration attempt of a previously dumped Security Account Manager (SAM) registry hive for credential
extraction on an attacker-controlled system.

T1003.002.name: PowerShell Invoke-NinjaCopy script
T1003.002.description:
Detects PowerShell scripts that contain the default exported functions used on Invoke-NinjaCopy. Attackers can use
Invoke-NinjaCopy to read SYSTEM files that are normally locked, such as the NTDS.dit file or registry hives.

T1003.002.name: Sensitive Registry Hive Access via RegBack
T1003.002.description: Identifies attempts to access sensitive registry hives which contain credentials from the registry backup folder.

T1003.002.name: Potential Remote Credential Access via Registry
T1003.002.description:
Identifies remote access to the registry to potentially dump credential data from the Security Account Manager (SAM)
registry hive in preparation for credential access and privileges elevation.

T1003.002.name: Suspicious Remote Registry Access via SeBackupPrivilege
T1003.002.description:
Identifies remote access to the registry using an account with Backup Operators group membership. This may indicate an
attempt to exfiltrate credentials by dumping the Security Account Manager (SAM) registry hive in preparation for
credential access and privileges elevation.

T1003.002.name: Symbolic Link to Shadow Copy Created
T1003.002.description:
Identifies the creation of symbolic links to a shadow copy. Symbolic links can be used to access files in the shadow
copy, including sensitive files such as ntds.dit, System Boot Key and browser offline credentials.

T1003.002.name: NTDS Dump via Wbadmin
T1003.002.description:
Identifies the execution of wbadmin to access the NTDS.dit file in a domain controller. Attackers with privileges from
groups like Backup Operators can abuse the utility to perform credential access and compromise the domain.

T1555.004.name: Potential Credential Access via Trusted Developer Utility
T1555.004.description:
An instance of MSBuild, the Microsoft Build Engine, loaded DLLs (dynamically linked libraries) responsible for Windows
credential management. This technique is sometimes used for credential dumping.

T1555.004.name: Multiple Vault Web Credentials Read
T1555.004.description:
Windows Credential Manager allows you to create, view, or delete saved credentials for signing into websites, connected
applications, and networks. An adversary may abuse this to list or dump credentials stored in the Credential Manager for
saved usernames and passwords. This may also be performed i

T1555.004.name: Searching for Saved Credentials via VaultCmd
T1555.004.description:
Windows Credential Manager allows you to create, view, or delete saved credentials for signing into websites, connected
applications, and networks. An adversary may abuse this to list or dump credentials stored in the Credential Manager for
saved usernames and passwords. This may also be performed i

T1127.001.name: Potential Credential Access via Trusted Developer Utility
T1127.001.description:
An instance of MSBuild, the Microsoft Build Engine, loaded DLLs (dynamically linked libraries) responsible for Windows
credential management. This technique is sometimes used for credential dumping.

T1127.001.name: Microsoft Build Engine Started by an Office Application
T1127.001.description:
An instance of MSBuild, the Microsoft Build Engine, was started by Excel or Word. This is unusual behavior for the Build
Engine and could have been caused by an Excel or Word document executing a malicious script payload.

T1127.001.name: Microsoft Build Engine Started by a Script Process
T1127.001.description:
An instance of MSBuild, the Microsoft Build Engine, was started by a script or the Windows command interpreter. This
behavior is unusual and is sometimes used by malicious payloads.

T1127.001.name: Microsoft Build Engine Started by a System Process
T1127.001.description:
An instance of MSBuild, the Microsoft Build Engine, was started by Explorer or the WMI (Windows Management
Instrumentation) subsystem. This behavior is unusual and is sometimes used by malicious payloads.

T1127.001.name: Microsoft Build Engine Using an Alternate Name
T1127.001.description:
An instance of MSBuild, the Microsoft Build Engine, was started after being renamed. This is uncommon behavior and may
indicate an attempt to run unnoticed or undetected.

T1127.001.name: Microsoft Build Engine Started an Unusual Process
T1127.001.description:
An instance of MSBuild, the Microsoft Build Engine, started a PowerShell script or the Visual C# Command Line Compiler.
This technique is sometimes used to deploy a malicious payload using the Build Engine.

T1127.001.name: Process Injection by the Microsoft Build Engine
T1127.001.description:
An instance of MSBuild, the Microsoft Build Engine, created a thread in another process. This technique is sometimes
used to evade detection or elevate privileges.

T1127.001.name: MsBuild Making Network Connections
T1127.001.description:
Identifies MsBuild.exe making outbound network connections. This may indicate adversarial activity as MsBuild is often
leveraged by adversaries to execute code and evade detection.

T1127.001.name: Unusual Network Activity from a Windows System Binary
T1127.001.description:
Identifies network activity from unexpected system applications. This may indicate adversarial activity as these
applications are often leveraged by adversaries to execute code and evade detection.

T1003.006.name: FirstTime Seen Account Performing DCSync
T1003.006.description:
This rule identifies when a User Account starts the Active Directory Replication Process for the first time. Attackers
can use the DCSync technique to get credential information of individual accounts or the entire domain, thus
compromising the entire domain.

T1003.006.name: Potential Credential Access via DCSync
T1003.006.description:
This rule identifies when a User Account starts the Active Directory Replication Process. Attackers can use the DCSync
technique to get credential information of individual accounts or the entire domain, thus compromising the entire
domain.

T1003.006.name: Potential Active Directory Replication Account Backdoor
T1003.006.description:
Identifies the modification of the nTSecurityDescriptor attribute in a domain object with rights related to DCSync to a
user/computer account. Attackers can use this backdoor to re-obtain access to hashes of any user/computer.

T1558.004.name: Kerberos Pre-authentication Disabled for User
T1558.004.description:
Identifies the modification of an account's Kerberos pre-authentication options. An adversary with
GenericWrite/GenericAll rights over the account can maliciously modify these settings to perform offline password
cracking attacks such as AS-REP roasting.

T1557.001.name: Potential Computer Account NTLM Relay Activity
T1557.001.description:
Identifies potential relay activities against a Computer account by identifying authentication events using the computer
account coming from from hosts other than the server that owns the account. Attackers may relay the computer account
hash after capturing it using forced authentication.

T1557.001.name: Potential Kerberos Relay Attack against a Computer Account
T1557.001.description:
Detects potential relay attacks by identifying coercion attempts followed by authentication events using a target
server's computer account, originating from a different host. This may indicate that an attacker has captured and
relayed the server's computer account hash to execute code on behalf of

T1557.001.name: Potential NTLM Relay Attack against a Computer Account
T1557.001.description:
Detects potential relay attacks by identifying coercion attempts followed by authentication events using a target
server's computer account, originating from a different host. This may indicate that an attacker has captured and
relayed the server's computer account hash to execute code on behalf of

T1557.001.name: Potential Kerberos Coercion via DNS-Based SPN Spoofing
T1557.001.description:
Identifies the creation of a DNS record containing a base64-encoded blob matching the pattern "UWhRCA...BAAAA". This
pattern corresponds to a marshaled CREDENTIAL_TARGET_INFORMATION structure, commonly used in Kerberos coercion attacks.
It is associated with tools and techniques that exploit SPN spo

T1557.001.name: Potential Kerberos SPN Spoofing via Suspicious DNS Query
T1557.001.description:
Identifies queries to a DNS record containing a base64-encoded blob matching the pattern "UWhRCA...BAAAA". This pattern
corresponds to a marshaled CREDENTIAL_TARGET_INFORMATION structure, commonly used in Kerberos coercion attacks. It is
associated with tools and techniques that exploit SPN spoofing

T1557.001.name: Potential Machine Account Relay Attack via SMB
T1557.001.description:
Identifies potential relay attacks against a machine account by identifying network share access events coming from a
remote source.ip but using the target server computer account. This may indicate a successful SMB relay attack.

T1552.004.name: Creation or Modification of Domain Backup DPAPI private key
T1552.004.description:
Identifies the creation or modification of Domain Backup private keys. Adversaries may extract the Data Protection API
(DPAPI) domain backup key from a Domain Controller (DC) to be able to decrypt any domain user master key file.

T1552.004.name: Access to a Sensitive LDAP Attribute
T1552.004.description:
Identify access to sensitive Active Directory object attributes that contains credentials and decryption keys such as
unixUserPassword, ms-PKI-AccountCredentials and msPKI-CredentialRoamingTokens.

T1552.004.name: Google Workspace Drive Encryption Key(s) Accessed from Anonymous User
T1552.004.description:
Detects when an external (anonymous) user has viewed, copied or downloaded an encryption key file from a Google
Workspace drive. Adversaries may gain access to encryption keys stored in private drives from rogue access links that do
not have an expiration. Access to encryption keys may allow adversa

T1003.004.name: Credential Acquisition via Registry Hive Dumping
T1003.004.description: Identifies attempts to export a registry hive which may contain credentials using the Windows reg.exe tool.

T1003.004.name: Sensitive Registry Hive Access via RegBack
T1003.004.description: Identifies attempts to access sensitive registry hives which contain credentials from the registry backup folder.

T1003.004.name: Suspicious Remote Registry Access via SeBackupPrivilege
T1003.004.description:
Identifies remote access to the registry using an account with Backup Operators group membership. This may indicate an
attempt to exfiltrate credentials by dumping the Security Account Manager (SAM) registry hive in preparation for
credential access and privileges elevation.

T1550.002.name: Potential PowerShell Pass-the-Hash/Relay Script
T1550.002.description:
Detects PowerShell scripts that can execute pass-the-hash (PtH) attacks, intercept and relay NTLM challenges, and carry
out other man-in-the-middle (MitM) attacks.

T1550.002.name: Local Account TokenFilter Policy Disabled
T1550.002.description:
Identifies registry modification to the LocalAccountTokenFilterPolicy policy. If this value exists (which doesn't by
default) and is set to 1, then remote connections from all local members of Administrators are granted full
high-integrity tokens during negotiation.

T1550.002.name: Potential Pass-the-Hash (PtH) Attempt
T1550.002.description:
Adversaries may pass the hash using stolen password hashes to move laterally within an environment, bypassing normal
system access controls. Pass the hash (PtH) is a method of authenticating as a user without having access to the user's
cleartext password.

T1222.001.name: Adding Hidden File Attribute via Attrib
T1222.001.description: Adversaries can add the 'hidden' attribute to files to hide them from the user in an attempt to evade detection.

T1222.001.name: System File Ownership Change
T1222.001.description: Adversaries may modify file or directory ownership to evade access control lists (ACLs) and access protected files.

T1574.001.name: Suspicious Antimalware Scan Interface DLL
T1574.001.description:
Identifies the creation of the Antimalware Scan Interface (AMSI) DLL in an unusual location. This may indicate an
attempt to bypass AMSI by loading a rogue AMSI module instead of the legit one.

T1574.001.name: Potential DLL Side-Loading via Trusted Microsoft Programs
T1574.001.description:
Identifies an instance of a Windows trusted program that is known to be vulnerable to DLL Search Order Hijacking
starting after being renamed or from a non-standard path. This is uncommon behavior and may indicate an attempt to evade
defenses via side loading a malicious DLL within the memory space

T1574.001.name: Suspicious Microsoft Antimalware Service Execution
T1574.001.description:
Identifies suspicious execution of the Microsoft Antimalware Service Executable (MsMpEng.exe) from non-standard paths or
renamed instances. This may indicate an attempt to evade defenses through DLL side-loading or by masquerading as the
antimalware process.

T1574.001.name: Unsigned DLL Side-Loading from a Suspicious Folder
T1574.001.description:
Identifies a Windows trusted program running from locations often abused by adversaries to masquerade as a trusted
program and loading a recently dropped DLL. This behavior may indicate an attempt to evade defenses via side-loading a
malicious DLL within the memory space of a signed processes.

T1574.001.name: Suspicious DLL Loaded for Persistence or Privilege Escalation
T1574.001.description:
Identifies the loading of a non Microsoft signed DLL that is missing on a default Windows install (phantom DLL) or one
that can be loaded from a different location by a native Windows process. This may be abused to persist or elevate
privileges via privileged file write vulnerabilities.

T1574.001.name: UAC Bypass Attempt via Privileged IFileOperation COM Interface
T1574.001.description:
Identifies attempts to bypass User Account Control (UAC) via DLL side-loading. Attackers may attempt to bypass UAC to
stealthily execute code with elevated permissions.

T1070.001.name: Sensitive Audit Policy Sub-Category Disabled
T1070.001.description:
Identifies attempts to disable auditing for some security sensitive audit policy sub-categories. This is often done by
attackers in an attempt to evade detection and forensics on a system.

T1070.001.name: Clearing Windows Event Logs
T1070.001.description:
Identifies attempts to clear or disable Windows event log stores using Windows wevetutil command. This is often done by
attackers in an attempt to evade detection or destroy forensic evidence on a system.

T1070.001.name: Windows Event Logs Cleared
T1070.001.description:
Identifies attempts to clear Windows event log stores. This is often done by attackers in an attempt to evade detection
or destroy forensic evidence on a system.

T1070.001.name: Disable Windows Event and Security Logs Using Built-in Tools
T1070.001.description:
Identifies attempts to disable EventLog via the logman Windows utility, PowerShell, or auditpol. This is often done by
attackers in an attempt to evade detection on a system.

T1562.002.name: Sensitive Audit Policy Sub-Category Disabled
T1562.002.description:
Identifies attempts to disable auditing for some security sensitive audit policy sub-categories. This is often done by
attackers in an attempt to evade detection and forensics on a system.

T1562.002.name: Clearing Windows Event Logs
T1562.002.description:
Identifies attempts to clear or disable Windows event log stores using Windows wevetutil command. This is often done by
attackers in an attempt to evade detection or destroy forensic evidence on a system.

T1562.002.name: PowerShell Script Block Logging Disabled
T1562.002.description:
Identifies attempts to disable PowerShell Script Block Logging via registry modification. Attackers may disable this
logging to conceal their activities in the host and evade detection.

T1562.002.name: Disable Windows Event and Security Logs Using Built-in Tools
T1562.002.description:
Identifies attempts to disable EventLog via the logman Windows utility, PowerShell, or auditpol. This is often done by
attackers in an attempt to evade detection on a system.

T1562.002.name: IIS HTTP Logging Disabled
T1562.002.description:
Identifies when Internet Information Services (IIS) HTTP Logging is disabled on a server. An attacker with IIS server
access via a webshell or other mechanism can disable HTTP Logging as an effective anti-forensics measure.

T1553.006.name: Code Signing Policy Modification Through Built-in tools
T1553.006.description:
Identifies attempts to disable/modify the code signing policy through system native utilities. Code signing provides
authenticity on a program, and grants the user with the ability to check whether the program has been tampered with. By
allowing the execution of unsigned or self-signed code, threat

T1553.006.name: Code Signing Policy Modification Through Registry
T1553.006.description:
Identifies attempts to disable the code signing policy through the registry. Code signing provides authenticity on a
program, and grants the user with the ability to check whether the program has been tampered with. By allowing the
execution of unsigned or self-signed code, threat actors can craft a

T1036.001.name: Suspicious Communication App Child Process
T1036.001.description:
Identifies suspicious child processes of communications apps, which can indicate a potential masquerading as the
communication app or the exploitation of a vulnerability on the application causing it to execute code.

T1036.001.name: Potential Masquerading as Business App Installer
T1036.001.description:
Identifies executables with names resembling legitimate business applications but lacking signatures from the original
developer. Attackers may trick users into downloading malicious executables that masquerade as legitimate applications
via malicious ads, forum posts, and tutorials, effectively gai

T1036.001.name: Potential Masquerading as Communication Apps
T1036.001.description:
Identifies suspicious instances of communications apps, both unsigned and renamed ones, that can indicate an attempt to
conceal malicious activity, bypass security features such as allowlists, or trick users into executing malware.

T1036.001.name: Deprecated - Process Termination followed by Deletion
T1036.001.description:
Identifies a process termination event quickly followed by the deletion of its executable file. Malware tools and other
non-native files dropped or created on a system by an adversary may leave traces to indicate to what occurred. Removal
of these files can occur during an intrusion, or as part of a

T1036.001.name: Unsigned DLL Side-Loading from a Suspicious Folder
T1036.001.description:
Identifies a Windows trusted program running from locations often abused by adversaries to masquerade as a trusted
program and loading a recently dropped DLL. This behavior may indicate an attempt to evade defenses via side-loading a
malicious DLL within the memory space of a signed processes.

T1036.001.name: Untrusted Driver Loaded
T1036.001.description:
Identifies attempt to load an untrusted driver. Adversaries may modify code signing policies to enable execution of
unsigned or self-signed code.

T1036.001.name: Unsigned DLL Loaded by Svchost
T1036.001.description:
Identifies an unsigned library created in the last 5 minutes and subsequently loaded by a shared windows service
(svchost). Adversaries may use this technique to maintain persistence or run with System privileges.

T1036.001.name: Startup Folder Persistence via Unsigned Process
T1036.001.description:
Identifies files written or modified in the startup folder by unsigned processes. Adversaries may abuse this technique
to maintain persistence in an environment.

T1036.001.name: Expired or Revoked Driver Loaded
T1036.001.description:
Identifies an attempt to load a revoked or expired driver. Adversaries may bring outdated drivers with vulnerabilities
to gain code execution in kernel mode or abuse revoked certificates to sign their drivers.

T1036.001.name: Suspicious DLL Loaded for Persistence or Privilege Escalation
T1036.001.description:
Identifies the loading of a non Microsoft signed DLL that is missing on a default Windows install (phantom DLL) or one
that can be loaded from a different location by a native Windows process. This may be abused to persist or elevate
privileges via privileged file write vulnerabilities.

T1553.002.name: Windows CryptoAPI Spoofing Vulnerability (CVE-2020-0601 - CurveBall)
T1553.002.description:
A spoofing vulnerability exists in the way Windows CryptoAPI (Crypt32.dll) validates Elliptic Curve Cryptography (ECC)
certificates. An attacker could exploit the vulnerability by using a spoofed code-signing certificate to sign a
malicious executable, making it appear the file was from a trusted, l

T1562.004.name: Disable Windows Firewall Rules via Netsh
T1562.004.description:
Identifies use of the netsh.exe to disable or weaken the local firewall. Attackers will use this command line tool to
disable the firewall during troubleshooting or to enable network mobility.

T1562.004.name: Remote Desktop Enabled in Windows Firewall by Netsh
T1562.004.description:
Identifies use of the network shell utility (netsh.exe) to enable inbound Remote Desktop Protocol (RDP) connections in
the Windows Firewall.

T1562.004.name: Enable Host Network Discovery via Netsh
T1562.004.description:
Identifies use of the netsh.exe program to enable host discovery via the network. Attackers can use this command-line
tool to weaken the host firewall settings.

T1562.004.name: Windows Firewall Disabled via PowerShell
T1562.004.description:
Identifies when the Windows Firewall is disabled using PowerShell cmdlets, which can help attackers evade network
constraints, like internet and network lateral communication restrictions.

T1562.004.name: Potential Evasion via Windows Filtering Platform
T1562.004.description:
Identifies multiple Windows Filtering Platform block events and where the process name is related to an endpoint
security software. Adversaries may add malicious WFP rules to prevent Endpoint security from sending telemetry.

T1218.002.name: Control Panel Process with Unusual Arguments
T1218.002.description:
Identifies unusual instances of Control Panel with suspicious keywords or paths in the process command line value.
Adversaries may abuse control.exe to proxy execution of malicious code.

T1059.003.name: Microsoft Build Engine Started by a Script Process
T1059.003.description:
An instance of MSBuild, the Microsoft Build Engine, was started by a script or the Windows command interpreter. This
behavior is unusual and is sometimes used by malicious payloads.

T1059.003.name: Suspicious Execution from a Mounted Device
T1059.003.description:
Identifies when a script interpreter or signed binary is launched via a non-standard working directory. An attacker may
use this technique to evade defenses.

T1059.003.name: Command Execution via SolarWinds Process
T1059.003.description: A suspicious SolarWinds child process (Cmd.exe or Powershell.exe) was detected.

T1059.003.name: Command Shell Activity Started via RunDLL32
T1059.003.description: Identifies command shell activity started via RunDLL32, which is commonly abused by attackers to host malicious code.

T1059.003.name: Execution from Unusual Directory - Command Line
T1059.003.description:
Identifies process execution from suspicious default Windows directories. This may be abused by adversaries to hide
malware in trusted paths.

T1059.003.name: Command and Scripting Interpreter via Windows Scripts
T1059.003.description: Identifies PowerShell.exe or Cmd.exe execution spawning from Windows Script Host processes Wscript.exe.

T1059.003.name: Potential Command Shell via NetCat
T1059.003.description:
Identifies potential attempt to execute via a reverse shell using the netcat utility to execute Windows commands using
the default interpreters like Cmd.exe and Powershell.

T1059.003.name: Suspicious Cmd Execution via WMI
T1059.003.description:
Identifies suspicious command execution (cmd) via Windows Management Instrumentation (WMI) on a remote host. This could
be indicative of adversary lateral movement.

T1059.003.name: Suspicious Windows Command Shell Arguments
T1059.003.description:
Identifies the execution of the Windows Command Shell process (cmd.exe) with suspicious argument values. This behavior
is often observed during malware installation.

T1059.003.name: Potential Fake CAPTCHA Phishing Attack
T1059.003.description:
Identifies potential fake CAPTCHA phishing attack based on PowerShell or Cmd argument values. Adversaries employ this
technique via compromised websites with browser injects, posing either as fake CAPTCHAs to access the site or as a page
loading error requiring a fix to display the page. The victim

T1059.003.name: Potential Execution via FileFix Phishing Attack
T1059.003.description:
Identifies the execution of Windows commands or downloaded files via the browser's dialog box. Adversaries may use phishing
to instruct the victim to copy and paste malicious commands for execution via crafted phsihing web pages.

T1059.003.name: Execution of a Downloaded Windows Script
T1059.003.description:
Identifies the creation of a Windows script downloaded from the internet followed by the execution of a scripting utility.
Adversaries may use Windows script files for initial access and execution.

T1059.003.name: Suspicious JetBrains TeamCity Child Process
T1059.003.description:
Identifies suspicious processes being spawned by the JetBrain TeamCity process. This activity could be related to
JetBrains remote code execution vulnerabilities.

T1059.003.name: Microsoft Exchange Worker Spawning Suspicious Processes
T1059.003.description:
Identifies suspicious processes being spawned by the Microsoft Exchange Server worker process (w3wp). This activity may
indicate exploitation activity or access to an existing web shell backdoor.

T1059.003.name: Suspicious MS Office Child Process
T1059.003.description:
Identifies suspicious child processes of frequently targeted Microsoft Office applications (Word, PowerPoint, Excel).
These child processes are often launched during exploitation of Office applications or from documents with malicious
macros.

T1059.003.name: Suspicious MS Outlook Child Process
T1059.003.description:
Identifies suspicious child processes of Microsoft Outlook. These child processes are often associated with spear
phishing activity.

T1059.003.name: Windows Server Update Service Spawning Suspicious Processes
T1059.003.description:
Identifies suspicious processes being spawned by the Windows Server Update Service. This activity may indicate exploitation
activity or access to an existing web shell backdoor.

T1059.003.name: Suspicious Explorer Child Process
T1059.003.description:
Identifies a suspicious Windows explorer child process. Explorer.exe can be abused to launch malicious scripts or
executables from a trusted parent process.

T1059.003.name: ScreenConnect Server Spawning Suspicious Processes
T1059.003.description:
Identifies suspicious processes being spawned by the ScreenConnect server process (ScreenConnect.Service.exe). This
activity may indicate exploitation activity or access to an existing web shell backdoor.

T1059.003.name: System Shells via Services
T1059.003.description:
Windows services typically run as SYSTEM and can be used as a privilege escalation opportunity. Malware or penetration
testers may run a shell as a service to gain SYSTEM permissions.

T1059.003.name: Execution via MSSQL xp_cmdshell Stored Procedure
T1059.003.description:
Identifies execution via MSSQL xp_cmdshell stored procedure. Malicious users may attempt to elevate their privileges by
using xp_cmdshell, which is disabled by default, thus, it's important to review the context of it's use.

T1059.003.name: Web Shell Detection: Script Process Child of Common Web Processes
T1059.003.description: Identifies suspicious commands executed via a web server, which may suggest a vulnerability and remote shell access.

T1059.003.name: Service Control Spawned via Script Interpreter
T1059.003.description:
Identifies Service Control (sc.exe) spawning from script interpreter processes to create, modify, or start services.
This can potentially indicate an attempt to elevate privileges or maintain persistence.

T1036.007.name: Executable File Creation with Multiple Extensions
T1036.007.description:
Masquerading can allow an adversary to evade defenses and better blend in with the environment. One way it occurs is
when the name or location of a file is manipulated as a means of tricking a user into executing what they think is a
benign file type but is actually executable code.

T1218.004.name: InstallUtil Process Making Network Connections
T1218.004.description:
Identifies InstallUtil.exe making outbound network connections. This may indicate adversarial activity as InstallUtil is
often leveraged by adversaries to execute code and evade detection.

T1218.004.name: Delayed Execution via Ping
T1218.004.description:
Identifies the execution of commonly abused Windows utilities via a delayed Ping execution. This behavior is often
observed during malware installation and is consistent with an attacker attempting to evade detection.

T1546.012.name: Suspicious WerFault Child Process
T1546.012.description:
A suspicious WerFault child process was detected, which may indicate an attempt to run via the SilentProcessExit
registry key manipulation. Verify process details such as command line, network connections and file writes.

T1546.012.name: Image File Execution Options Injection
T1546.012.description:
The Debugger and SilentProcessExit registry keys can allow an adversary to intercept the execution of files, causing a
different process to be executed. This functionality can be abused by an adversary to establish persistence.

T1218.007.name: MsiExec Service Child Process With Network Connection
T1218.007.description:
Identifies the execution of an MsiExec service child process followed by network or dns lookup activity. Adversaries may
abuse Windows Installers for initial access and delivery of malware.

T1218.007.name: Potential Remote Install via MsiExec
T1218.007.description: Identifies attempts to install a file from a remote server using MsiExec. Adversaries may abuse Windows Installers for initial access and delivery of malware.

T1218.007.name: Potential Remote File Execution via MSIEXEC
T1218.007.description:
Identifies the execution of the built-in Windows Installer, msiexec.exe, to install a remote package. Adversaries may
abuse msiexec.exe to launch local or network accessible MSI files.

T1218.007.name: Persistence via a Windows Installer
T1218.007.description: Identifies when the Windows installer process msiexec.exe creates a new persistence entry via scheduled tasks or startup.

T1218.007.name: Potential Escalation via Vulnerable MSI Repair
T1218.007.description:
Identifies when a browser process navigates to the Microsoft Help page followed by spawning an elevated process. This
may indicate a successful exploitation for privilege escalation abusing a vulnerable Windows Installer repair setup.

T1220.name: Network Connection via MsXsl
T1220.description:
Identifies msxsl.exe making a network connection. This may indicate adversarial activity as msxsl.exe is often leveraged
by adversaries to execute malicious scripts and evade detection.

T1220.name: Suspicious WMIC XSL Script Execution
T1220.description:
Identifies WMIC allowlist bypass techniques by alerting on suspicious execution of scripts. When WMIC loads scripting
libraries it may be indicative of an allowlist bypass.

T1220.name: Delayed Execution via Ping
T1220.description:
Identifies the execution of commonly abused Windows utilities via a delayed Ping execution. This behavior is often
observed during malware installation and is consistent with an attacker attempting to evade detection.

T1220.name: Remote XSL Script Execution via COM
T1220.description:
Identifies the execution of a hosted XSL script using the Microsoft.XMLDOM COM interface via Microsoft Office processes.
This behavior may indicate adversarial activity to execute malicious JScript or VBScript on the system.

T1027.010.name: Command Obfuscation via Unicode Modifier Letters
T1027.010.description:
Identifies the presence of unicode modifier letters in the process command_line. Adversaries sometimes replace ASCII characters
with visually similar Unicode modifier letters or combining marks to evade simple string-based detections.

T1134.004.name: Parent Process PID Spoofing
T1134.004.description:
Identifies parent process spoofing used to thwart detection. Adversaries may spoof the parent process identifier (PPID)
of a new process to evade process-monitoring defenses or to elevate privileges.

T1134.004.name: Privileges Elevation via Parent Process PID Spoofing
T1134.004.description:
Identifies parent process spoofing used to create an elevated child process. Adversaries may spoof the parent process
identifier (PPID) of a new process to evade process-monitoring defenses or to elevate privileges.

T1055.001.name: Suspicious .NET Reflection via PowerShell
T1055.001.description:
Detects the use of Reflection.Assembly to load PEs and DLLs in memory in PowerShell scripts. Attackers use this method
to load executables and DLLs without writing to the disk, bypassing security solutions.

T1055.001.name: Potential Process Injection via PowerShell
T1055.001.description:
Detects the use of Windows API functions that are commonly abused by malware and security tools to load malicious code
or inject it into remote processes.

T1055.002.name: Suspicious .NET Reflection via PowerShell
T1055.002.description:
Detects the use of Reflection.Assembly to load PEs and DLLs in memory in PowerShell scripts. Attackers use this method
to load executables and DLLs without writing to the disk, bypassing security solutions.

T1055.002.name: Potential Process Injection via PowerShell
T1055.002.description:
Detects the use of Windows API functions that are commonly abused by malware and security tools to load malicious code
or inject it into remote processes.

T1620.name: Suspicious .NET Reflection via PowerShell
T1620.description:
Detects the use of Reflection.Assembly to load PEs and DLLs in memory in PowerShell scripts. Attackers use this method
to load executables and DLLs without writing to the disk, bypassing security solutions.

T1620.name: Memory Threat - Detected - Elastic Defend
T1620.description:
Generates a detection alert each time an Elastic Defend alert for memory signatures are received. Enabling this rule
allows you to immediately begin investigating your Endpoint memory signature alerts. This rule identifies Elastic Defend
memory signature detections only, and does not include prevent

T1620.name: Memory Threat - Prevented- Elastic Defend
T1620.description:
Generates a detection alert each time an Elastic Defend alert for memory signatures are received. Enabling this rule
allows you to immediately begin investigating your Endpoint memory signature alerts. This rule identifies Elastic Defend
memory signature preventions only, and does not include detect

T1036.002.name: File with Right-to-Left Override Character (RTLO) Created/Executed
T1036.002.description:
Identifies the creation or execution of files or processes with names containing the Right-to-Left Override (RTLO)
character, which can be used to disguise the file extension and trick users into executing malicious files.

T1564.004.name: Alternate Data Stream Creation/Execution at Volume Root Directory
T1564.004.description:
Identifies the creation of an Alternate Data Stream (ADS) at a volume root directory, which can indicate the attempt to
hide tools and malware, as ADSs created in this directory are not displayed by system utilities.

T1564.004.name: Unusual File Creation - Alternate Data Stream
T1564.004.description:
Identifies suspicious creation of Alternate Data Streams on highly targeted files. This is uncommon for legitimate files
and sometimes done by adversaries to hide malware.

T1564.004.name: Unusual Process Execution Path - Alternate Data Stream
T1564.004.description:
Identifies processes running from an Alternate Data Stream. This is uncommon for legitimate processes and sometimes done
by adversaries to hide malware.

T1564.006.name: Windows Sandbox with Sensitive Configuration
T1564.006.description:
Identifies Windows sanfbox processes indicating the start of a new container with sensitive configurations like write
access to the host file system, network connection and automatic execution via logon command. Malware may abuse the
sandbox feature to evade detection.

T1553.003.name: SIP Provider Modification
T1553.003.description:
Identifies modifications to the registered Subject Interface Package (SIP) providers. SIP providers are used by the
Windows cryptographic system to validate file signatures on the system. This may be an attempt to bypass signature
validation checks or inject code into critical processes.

T1218.010.name: Suspicious Execution from a Mounted Device
T1218.010.description:
Identifies when a script interpreter or signed binary is launched via a non-standard working directory. An attacker may
use this technique to evade defenses.

T1218.010.name: Suspicious Script Object Execution
T1218.010.description:
Identifies scrobj.dll loaded into unusual Microsoft processes. This usually means a malicious scriptlet is being
executed in the target process.

T1218.010.name: Delayed Execution via Ping
T1218.010.description:
Identifies the execution of commonly abused Windows utilities via a delayed Ping execution. This behavior is often
observed during malware installation and is consistent with an attacker attempting to evade detection.

T1218.010.name: Network Connection via Registration Utility
T1218.010.description:
Identifies the native Windows tools regsvr32.exe, regsvr64.exe, RegSvcs.exe, or RegAsm.exe making a network connection.
This may be indicative of an attacker bypassing allowlists or running arbitrary scripts via a signed Microsoft binary.

T1218.010.name: Service Control Spawned via Script Interpreter
T1218.010.description:
Identifies Service Control (sc.exe) spawning from script interpreter processes to create, modify, or start services.
This can potentially indicate an attempt to elevate privileges or maintain persistence.

T1047.name: Suspicious WMIC XSL Script Execution
T1047.description:
Identifies WMIC allowlist bypass techniques by alerting on suspicious execution of scripts. When WMIC loads scripting
libraries it may be indicative of an allowlist bypass.

T1047.name: Enumeration Command Spawned via WMIPrvSE
T1047.description:
Identifies native Windows host and network enumeration commands spawned by the Windows Management Instrumentation
Provider Service (WMIPrvSE).

T1047.name: Mofcomp Activity
T1047.description:
Managed Object Format (MOF) files can be compiled locally or remotely through mofcomp.exe. Attackers may leverage MOF
files to build their own namespaces and classes into the Windows Management Instrumentation (WMI) repository, or
establish persistence using WMI Event Subscription.

T1047.name: Suspicious Cmd Execution via WMI
T1047.description:
Identifies suspicious command execution (cmd) via Windows Management Instrumentation (WMI) on a remote host. This could
be indicative of adversary lateral movement.

T1047.name: Suspicious WMI Image Load from MS Office
T1047.description:
Identifies a suspicious image load (wmiutils.dll) from Microsoft Office processes. This behavior may indicate
adversarial activity where child processes are spawned via Windows Management Instrumentation (WMI). This technique can
be used to execute code and evade traditional parent/child processes s

T1047.name: Volume Shadow Copy Deletion via WMIC
T1047.description:
Identifies use of wmic.exe for shadow copy deletion on endpoints. This commonly occurs in tandem with ransomware or
other destructive attacks.

T1047.name: Windows Script Interpreter Executing Process via WMI
T1047.description:
Identifies use of the built-in Windows script interpreters (cscript.exe or wscript.exe) being used to execute a process
via Windows Management Instrumentation (WMI). This may be indicative of malicious activity.

T1047.name: WMI Incoming Lateral Movement
T1047.description:
Identifies processes executed via Windows Management Instrumentation (WMI) on a remote host. This could be indicative of
adversary lateral movement, but could be noisy if administrators use WMI to remotely manage hosts.

T1047.name: Persistence via WMI Event Subscription
T1047.description:
An adversary can use Windows Management Instrumentation (WMI) to install event filters, providers, consumers, and
bindings that execute code when a defined event occurs. Adversaries may use the capabilities of WMI to subscribe to an
event and execute arbitrary code when that event occurs, providing

T1047.name: Persistence via WMI Standard Registry Provider
T1047.description:
Identifies use of the Windows Management Instrumentation StdRegProv (registry provider) to modify commonly abused
registry locations for persistence.

T1047.name: Web Shell Detection: Script Process Child of Common Web Processes
T1047.description: Identifies suspicious commands executed via a web server, which may suggest a vulnerability and remote shell access.

T1047.name: Service Control Spawned via Script Interpreter
T1047.description:
Identifies Service Control (sc.exe) spawning from script interpreter processes to create, modify, or start services.
This can potentially indicate an attempt to elevate privileges or maintain persistence.

T1018.name: Potential Enumeration via Active Directory Web Service
T1018.description:
Identifies processes loading Active Directory related modules followed by a network connection to the ADWS dedicated TCP
port. Adversaries may abuse the ADWS Windows service that allows Active Directory to be queried via this web service.

T1018.name: Active Directory Discovery using AdExplorer
T1018.description:
This rule detects the use of ADExplorer utility. Active Directory Explorer (AD Explorer) is an advanced Active Directory (AD)
viewer and editor. AD Explorer also includes the ability to save snapshots of an AD database for off-line viewing and comparisons.
Adversaries may abuse this utility to perfo

T1018.name: AdFind Command Activity
T1018.description:
This rule detects the Active Directory query tool, AdFind.exe. AdFind has legitimate purposes, but it is frequently
leveraged by threat actors to perform post-exploitation Active Directory reconnaissance. The AdFind tool has been
observed in Trickbot, Ryuk, Maze, and FIN6 campaigns. For Winlogbeat,

T1018.name: Enumerating Domain Trusts via DSQUERY.EXE
T1018.description:
Identifies the use of dsquery.exe for domain trust discovery purposes. Adversaries may use this command-line utility to
enumerate trust relationships that may be used for Lateral Movement opportunities in Windows multi-domain forest
environments.

T1018.name: Enumerating Domain Trusts via NLTEST.EXE
T1018.description:
Identifies the use of nltest.exe for domain trust discovery purposes. Adversaries may use this command-line utility to
enumerate domain trusts and gain insight into trust relationships, as well as the state of Domain Controller (DC)
replication in a Microsoft Windows NT Domain.

T1018.name: Enumeration Command Spawned via WMIPrvSE
T1018.description:
Identifies native Windows host and network enumeration commands spawned by the Windows Management Instrumentation
Provider Service (WMIPrvSE).

T1069.002.name: Active Directory Discovery using AdExplorer
T1069.002.description:
This rule detects the use of ADExplorer utility. Active Directory Explorer (AD Explorer) is an advanced Active Directory (AD)
viewer and editor. AD Explorer also includes the ability to save snapshots of an AD database for off-line viewing and comparisons.
Adversaries may abuse this utility to perfo

T1069.002.name: AdFind Command Activity
T1069.002.description:
This rule detects the Active Directory query tool, AdFind.exe. AdFind has legitimate purposes, but it is frequently
leveraged by threat actors to perform post-exploitation Active Directory reconnaissance. The AdFind tool has been
observed in Trickbot, Ryuk, Maze, and FIN6 campaigns. For Winlogbeat,

T1069.002.name: Enumeration of Administrator Accounts
T1069.002.description:
Identifies instances of lower privilege accounts enumerating Administrator accounts or groups using built-in Windows
tools.

T1087.002.name: Active Directory Discovery using AdExplorer
T1087.002.description:
This rule detects the use of ADExplorer utility. Active Directory Explorer (AD Explorer) is an advanced Active Directory (AD)
viewer and editor. AD Explorer also includes the ability to save snapshots of an AD database for off-line viewing and comparisons.
Adversaries may abuse this utility to perfo

T1087.002.name: AdFind Command Activity
T1087.002.description:
This rule detects the Active Directory query tool, AdFind.exe. AdFind has legitimate purposes, but it is frequently
leveraged by threat actors to perform post-exploitation Active Directory reconnaissance. The AdFind tool has been
observed in Trickbot, Ryuk, Maze, and FIN6 campaigns. For Winlogbeat,

T1087.002.name: Enumeration of Administrator Accounts
T1087.002.description:
Identifies instances of lower privilege accounts enumerating Administrator accounts or groups using built-in Windows
tools.

T1087.002.name: Mounting Hidden or WebDav Remote Shares
T1087.002.description:
Identifies the use of net.exe to mount a WebDav or hidden remote share. This may indicate lateral movement or
preparation for data exfiltration.

T1482.name: Active Directory Discovery using AdExplorer
T1482.description:
This rule detects the use of ADExplorer utility. Active Directory Explorer (AD Explorer) is an advanced Active Directory (AD)
viewer and editor. AD Explorer also includes the ability to save snapshots of an AD database for off-line viewing and comparisons.
Adversaries may abuse this utility to perfo

T1482.name: AdFind Command Activity
T1482.description:
This rule detects the Active Directory query tool, AdFind.exe. AdFind has legitimate purposes, but it is frequently
leveraged by threat actors to perform post-exploitation Active Directory reconnaissance. The AdFind tool has been
observed in Trickbot, Ryuk, Maze, and FIN6 campaigns. For Winlogbeat,

T1482.name: Enumerating Domain Trusts via DSQUERY.EXE
T1482.description:
Identifies the use of dsquery.exe for domain trust discovery purposes. Adversaries may use this command-line utility to
enumerate trust relationships that may be used for Lateral Movement opportunities in Windows multi-domain forest
environments.

T1482.name: Enumerating Domain Trusts via NLTEST.EXE
T1482.description:
Identifies the use of nltest.exe for domain trust discovery purposes. Adversaries may use this command-line utility to
enumerate domain trusts and gain insight into trust relationships, as well as the state of Domain Controller (DC)
replication in a Microsoft Windows NT Domain.

T1482.name: PowerShell Suspicious Discovery Related Windows API Functions
T1482.description:
This rule detects the use of discovery-related Windows API functions in PowerShell Scripts. Attackers can use these
functions to perform various situational awareness related activities, like enumerating users, shares, sessions, domain
trusts, groups, etc.

T1615.name: Group Policy Discovery via Microsoft GPResult Utility
T1615.description:
Detects the usage of gpresult.exe to query group policy objects. Attackers may query group policy objects during the
reconnaissance phase after compromising a system to gain a better understanding of the active directory environment and
possible methods to escalate privileges or move laterally.

T1120.name: Peripheral Device Discovery
T1120.description:
Identifies use of the Windows file system utility (fsutil.exe) to gather information about attached peripheral devices
and components connected to a computer system.

T1135.name: PowerShell Share Enumeration Script
T1135.description:
Detects scripts that contain PowerShell functions, structures, or Windows API functions related to windows share
enumeration activities. Attackers, mainly ransomware groups, commonly identify and inspect network shares, looking for
critical information for encryption and/or exfiltration.

T1135.name: PowerShell Suspicious Discovery Related Windows API Functions
T1135.description:
This rule detects the use of discovery-related Windows API functions in PowerShell Scripts. Attackers can use these
functions to perform various situational awareness related activities, like enumerating users, shares, sessions, domain
trusts, groups, etc.

T1039.name: PowerShell Share Enumeration Script
T1039.description:
Detects scripts that contain PowerShell functions, structures, or Windows API functions related to windows share
enumeration activities. Attackers, mainly ransomware groups, commonly identify and inspect network shares, looking for
critical information for encryption and/or exfiltration.

T1039.name: PowerShell Suspicious Discovery Related Windows API Functions
T1039.description:
This rule detects the use of discovery-related Windows API functions in PowerShell Scripts. Attackers can use these
functions to perform various situational awareness related activities, like enumerating users, shares, sessions, domain
trusts, groups, etc.

T1216.name: Delayed Execution via Ping
T1216.description:
Identifies the execution of commonly abused Windows utilities via a delayed Ping execution. This behavior is often
observed during malware installation and is consistent with an attacker attempting to evade detection.

T1218.003.name: Delayed Execution via Ping
T1218.003.description:
Identifies the execution of commonly abused Windows utilities via a delayed Ping execution. This behavior is often
observed during malware installation and is consistent with an attacker attempting to evade detection.

T1497.003.name: Delayed Execution via Ping
T1497.003.description:
Identifies the execution of commonly abused Windows utilities via a delayed Ping execution. This behavior is often
observed during malware installation and is consistent with an attacker attempting to evade detection.

T1016.001.name: Enumeration Command Spawned via WMIPrvSE
T1016.001.description:
Identifies native Windows host and network enumeration commands spawned by the Windows Management Instrumentation
Provider Service (WMIPrvSE).

T1218.001.name: Network Connection via Compiled HTML File
T1218.001.description:
Compiled HTML files (.chm) are commonly distributed as part of the Microsoft HTML Help system. Adversaries may conceal
malicious code in a CHM file and deliver it to a victim for execution. CHM content is loaded by the HTML Help executable
program (hh.exe).

T1218.001.name: Process Activity via Compiled HTML File
T1218.001.description:
Compiled HTML files (.chm) are commonly distributed as part of the Microsoft HTML Help system. Adversaries may conceal
malicious code in a CHM file and deliver it to a victim for execution. CHM content is loaded by the HTML Help executable
program (hh.exe).

T1546.003.name: Mofcomp Activity
T1546.003.description:
Managed Object Format (MOF) files can be compiled locally or remotely through mofcomp.exe. Attackers may leverage MOF
files to build their own namespaces and classes into the Windows Management Instrumentation (WMI) repository, or
establish persistence using WMI Event Subscription.

T1546.003.name: Suspicious WMI Event Subscription Created
T1546.003.description:
Detects the creation of a WMI Event Subscription. Attackers can abuse this mechanism for persistence or to elevate to
SYSTEM privileges.

T1546.003.name: Persistence via WMI Event Subscription
T1546.003.description:
An adversary can use Windows Management Instrumentation (WMI) to install event filters, providers, consumers, and
bindings that execute code when a defined event occurs. Adversaries may use the capabilities of WMI to subscribe to an
event and execute arbitrary code when that event occurs, providing

T1569.002.name: PsExec Network Connection
T1569.002.description:
Identifies use of the SysInternals tool PsExec.exe making a network connection. This could be an indication of lateral
movement.

T1569.002.name: Suspicious Process Execution via Renamed PsExec Executable
T1569.002.description:
Identifies suspicious psexec activity which is executing from the psexec service that has been renamed, possibly to
evade detection.

T1569.002.name: Service Command Lateral Movement
T1569.002.description:
Identifies use of sc.exe to create, modify, or start services on remote hosts. This could be indicative of adversary
lateral movement but will be noisy if commonly done by admins.

T1569.002.name: Unsigned DLL Loaded by Svchost
T1569.002.description:
Identifies an unsigned library created in the last 5 minutes and subsequently loaded by a shared windows service
(svchost). Adversaries may use this technique to maintain persistence or run with System privileges.

T1569.002.name: Potential Privilege Escalation via Service ImagePath Modification
T1569.002.description:
Identifies registry modifications to default services that could enable privilege escalation to SYSTEM. Attackers with
privileges from groups like Server Operators may change the ImagePath of services to executables under their control or
to execute commands.

T1569.name: PsExec Network Connection
T1569.description:
Identifies use of the SysInternals tool PsExec.exe making a network connection. This could be an indication of lateral
movement.

T1569.name: Suspicious Process Execution via Renamed PsExec Executable
T1569.description:
Identifies suspicious psexec activity which is executing from the psexec service that has been renamed, possibly to
evade detection.

T1569.name: Service Command Lateral Movement
T1569.description:
Identifies use of sc.exe to create, modify, or start services on remote hosts. This could be indicative of adversary
lateral movement but will be noisy if commonly done by admins.

T1569.name: Unsigned DLL Loaded by Svchost
T1569.description:
Identifies an unsigned library created in the last 5 minutes and subsequently loaded by a shared windows service
(svchost). Adversaries may use this technique to maintain persistence or run with System privileges.

T1569.name: Potential Privilege Escalation via Service ImagePath Modification
T1569.description:
Identifies registry modifications to default services that could enable privilege escalation to SYSTEM. Attackers with
privileges from groups like Server Operators may change the ImagePath of services to executables under their control or
to execute commands.

T1053.005.name: Outbound Scheduled Task Activity via PowerShell
T1053.005.description:
Identifies the PowerShell process loading the Task Scheduler COM DLL followed by an outbound RPC network connection
within a short time period. This may indicate lateral movement or remote discovery via scheduled tasks.

T1053.005.name: Remote Scheduled Task Creation via RPC
T1053.005.description: Identifies scheduled task creation from a remote source. This could be indicative of adversary lateral movement.

T1053.005.name: Remote Scheduled Task Creation
T1053.005.description: Identifies remote scheduled task creations on a target host. This could be indicative of adversary lateral movement.

T1053.005.name: Persistence via Scheduled Job Creation
T1053.005.description:
A job can be used to schedule programs or scripts to be executed at a specified date and time. Adversaries may abuse
task scheduling functionality to facilitate initial or recurring execution of malicious code.

T1053.005.name: Local Scheduled Task Creation
T1053.005.description:
Indicates the creation of a scheduled task. Adversaries can use these to establish persistence, move laterally, and/or
escalate privileges.

T1053.005.name: Scheduled Task Created by a Windows Script
T1053.005.description:
A scheduled task was created by a Windows script via cscript.exe, wscript.exe or powershell.exe. This can be abused by
an adversary to establish persistence.

T1053.005.name: Persistence via a Windows Installer
T1053.005.description: Identifies when the Windows installer process msiexec.exe creates a new persistence entry via scheduled tasks or startup.

T1053.005.name: A scheduled task was created
T1053.005.description:
Indicates the creation of a scheduled task using Windows event logs. Adversaries can use these to establish persistence,
move laterally, and/or escalate privileges.

T1053.005.name: Unusual Scheduled Task Update
T1053.005.description: Identifies first-time modifications to scheduled tasks by user accounts, excluding system activity and machine accounts.

T1053.005.name: Suspicious Image Load (taskschd.dll) from MS Office
T1053.005.description:
Identifies a suspicious image load (taskschd.dll) from Microsoft Office processes. This behavior may indicate
adversarial activity where a scheduled task is configured via Windows Component Object Model (COM). This technique can
be used to configure persistence and evade monitoring by avoiding the u

T1053.005.name: Suspicious Execution via Scheduled Task
T1053.005.description: Identifies execution of a suspicious program via scheduled tasks by looking at process lineage and command line usage.

T1053.005.name: Temporarily Scheduled Task Creation
T1053.005.description:
Indicates the creation and deletion of a scheduled task within a short time interval. Adversaries can use these to proxy
malicious execution via the schedule service and perform clean up.

T1053.005.name: Persistence via TelemetryController Scheduled Task Hijack
T1053.005.description:
Detects the successful hijack of Microsoft Compatibility Appraiser scheduled task to establish persistence with an
integrity level of system.

T1053.005.name: Creation or Modification of a new GPO Scheduled Task or Service
T1053.005.description:
Detects the creation or modification of a new Group Policy based scheduled task or service. These methods are used for
legitimate system administration, but can also be abused by an attacker with domain admin permissions to execute a
malicious payload remotely on all or a subset of the domain joined

T1053.005.name: Scheduled Task Execution at Scale via GPO
T1053.005.description:
Detects the modification of Group Policy Object attributes to execute a scheduled task in the objects controlled by the
GPO.

T1053.005.name: UAC Bypass via DiskCleanup Scheduled Task Hijack
T1053.005.description:
Identifies User Account Control (UAC) bypass via hijacking DiskCleanup Scheduled Task. Attackers bypass UAC to
stealthily execute code with elevated permissions.

T1129.name: Execution via local SxS Shared Module
T1129.description:
Identifies the creation, change, or deletion of a DLL module within a Windows SxS local folder. Adversaries may abuse
shared modules to execute malicious payloads by instructing the Windows module loader to load DLLs from arbitrary local
paths.

T1218.014.name: Microsoft Management Console File from Unusual Path
T1218.014.description:
Identifies attempts to open a Microsoft Management Console File from untrusted paths. Adversaries may use MSC files for
initial access and execution.

T1218.014.name: Incoming DCOM Lateral Movement with MMC
T1218.014.description:
Identifies the use of Distributed Component Object Model (DCOM) to run commands from a remote host, which are launched
via the MMC20 Application COM Object. This behavior may indicate an attacker abusing a DCOM application to move
laterally.

T1218.014.name: UAC Bypass via Windows Firewall Snap-In Hijack
T1218.014.description:
Identifies attempts to bypass User Account Control (UAC) by hijacking the Microsoft Management Console (MMC) Windows
Firewall snap-in. Attackers bypass UAC to stealthily execute code with elevated permissions.

T1490.name: Third-party Backup Files Deleted via Unexpected Process
T1490.description:
Identifies the deletion of backup files, saved using third-party software, by a process outside of the backup suite.
Adversaries may delete Backup files to ensure that recovery from a ransomware attack is less likely.

T1490.name: Backup Deletion with Wbadmin
T1490.description:
Detects use of wbadmin.exe to delete backup catalogs, system state backups, or other backup data. Ransomware and other
malware may do this to prevent system recovery.

T1490.name: Potential System Tampering via File Modification
T1490.description:
Identifies attempts to delete or modify critical files used during the boot process to prevent the system from booting.
This may indicate a destructive attack behavior.

T1490.name: Modification of Boot Configuration
T1490.description:
Identifies use of bcdedit.exe to delete boot configuration data. This tactic is sometimes used as by malware or an
attacker as a destructive technique.

T1490.name: Suspicious File Renamed via SMB
T1490.description:
Identifies an incoming SMB connection followed by a suspicious file rename operation. This may indicate a remote
ransomware attack via the SMB protocol.

T1490.name: Potential Ransomware Note File Dropped via SMB
T1490.description:
Identifies an incoming SMB connection followed by the creation of a file with a name similar to ransomware note files.
This may indicate a remote ransomware attack via the SMB protocol.

T1490.name: Volume Shadow Copy Deleted or Resized via VssAdmin
T1490.description:
Identifies use of vssadmin.exe for shadow copy deletion or resizing on endpoints. This commonly occurs in tandem with
ransomware or other destructive attacks.

T1490.name: Volume Shadow Copy Deletion via PowerShell
T1490.description:
Identifies the use of the Win32_ShadowCopy class and related cmdlets to achieve shadow copy deletion. This commonly
occurs in tandem with ransomware or other destructive attacks.

T1490.name: Volume Shadow Copy Deletion via WMIC
T1490.description:
Identifies use of wmic.exe for shadow copy deletion on endpoints. This commonly occurs in tandem with ransomware or
other destructive attacks.

T1490.name: AWS S3 Bucket Configuration Deletion
T1490.description:
Identifies the deletion of critical Amazon S3 bucket configurations such as bucket policies, lifecycle configurations
or encryption settings. These actions are typically administrative but may also represent
adversarial attempts to remove security controls, disable data retention mechanisms, or conc

T1490.name: AWS EC2 EBS Snapshot Access Removed
T1490.description:
