Identifies the removal of access permissions from a shared AWS EC2 EBS snapshot. EBS snapshots are essential for data
retention and disaster recovery. Adversaries may revoke or modify snapshot permissions to prevent legitimate users from
accessing backups, thereby obstructing recovery efforts after

T1490.name: AWS S3 Object Versioning Suspended
T1490.description: Identifies when object versioning is suspended for an Amazon S3 bucket. Object versioning allows for multiple versions of an object to exist in the same bucket. This allows for easy recovery of deleted or overwritten objects. When object versioning is suspended for a bucket, it could indicate an adv

T1490.name: Azure Compute Restore Point Collection Deleted by Unusual User
T1490.description:
Identifies the deletion of Azure Restore Point Collections by a user who has not previously performed this activity.
Restore Point Collections contain recovery points for virtual machines, enabling point-in-time recovery capabilities.
Adversaries may delete these collections to prevent recovery duri

T1490.name: Azure Compute Restore Point Collections Deleted
T1490.description:
Identifies multiple Azure Restore Point Collections being deleted by a single user within a short time period. Restore
Point Collections contain recovery points for virtual machines, enabling point-in-time recovery capabilities. Mass
deletion of these collections is a common tactic used by adversari

T1490.name: Azure Compute Snapshot Deletion by Unusual User and Resource Group
T1490.description:
Identifies when an Azure disk snapshot is deleted by an unusual user in a specific resource group. Snapshots are
critical for backup, disaster recovery, and forensic analysis. Adversaries may delete snapshots to prevent data
recovery, eliminate forensic evidence, or disrupt backup strategies before

T1490.name: Azure Compute Snapshot Deletions by User
T1490.description:
Identifies when a single user or service principal deletes multiple Azure disk snapshots within a short time period.
This behavior may indicate an adversary attempting to inhibit system recovery capabilities, destroy backup evidence, or
prepare for a ransomware attack. Mass deletion of snapshots eli

T1027.006.name: Suspicious HTML File Creation
T1027.006.description:
Identifies the execution of a browser process to open an HTML file with high entropy and size. Adversaries may smuggle
data and files past content filters by hiding malicious payloads inside of seemingly benign HTML files.

T1137.006.name: Suspicious Execution via Microsoft Office Add-Ins
T1137.006.description:
Identifies execution of common Microsoft Office applications to launch an Office Add-In from a suspicious path or with
an unusual parent process. This may indicate an attempt to get initial access via a malicious phishing MS Office Add-In.

T1137.006.name: Persistence via Microsoft Office AddIns
T1137.006.description: Detects attempts to establish persistence on an endpoint by abusing Microsoft Office add-ins.

T1021.003.name: Incoming DCOM Lateral Movement via MSHTA
T1021.003.description:
Identifies the use of Distributed Component Object Model (DCOM) to execute commands from a remote host, which are
launched via the HTA Application COM Object. This behavior may indicate an attacker abusing a DCOM application to move
laterally while attempting to evade detection.

T1021.003.name: Incoming DCOM Lateral Movement with MMC
T1021.003.description:
Identifies the use of Distributed Component Object Model (DCOM) to run commands from a remote host, which are launched
via the MMC20 Application COM Object. This behavior may indicate an attacker abusing a DCOM application to move
laterally.

T1021.003.name: Incoming DCOM Lateral Movement with ShellBrowserWindow or ShellWindows
T1021.003.description:
Identifies use of Distributed Component Object Model (DCOM) to run commands from a remote host, which are launched via
the ShellBrowserWindow or ShellWindows Application COM Object. This behavior may indicate an attacker abusing a DCOM
application to stealthily move laterally.

T1021.001.name: Potential Remote Desktop Shadowing Activity
T1021.001.description:
Identifies the modification of the Remote Desktop Protocol (RDP) Shadow registry or the execution of processes
indicative of an active RDP shadowing session. An adversary may abuse the RDP Shadowing feature to spy on or control
other users active RDP sessions.

T1021.001.name: Execution via TSClient Mountpoint
T1021.001.description:
Identifies execution from the Remote Desktop Protocol (RDP) shared mountpoint tsclient on the target host. This may
indicate a lateral movement attempt.

T1021.001.name: RDP Enabled via Registry
T1021.001.description:
Identifies registry write modifications to enable Remote Desktop Protocol (RDP) access. This could be indicative of
adversary lateral movement preparation.

T1021.001.name: Potential SharpRDP Behavior
T1021.001.description:
Identifies potential behavior of SharpRDP, which is a tool that can be used to perform authenticated command execution
against a remote target via Remote Desktop Protocol (RDP) for the purposes of lateral movement.

T1021.001.name: Suspicious RDP ActiveX Client Loaded
T1021.001.description:
Identifies suspicious Image Loading of the Remote Desktop Services ActiveX Client (mstscax), this may indicate the
presence of RDP lateral movement capability.

T1021.001.name: Lateral Movement via Startup Folder
T1021.001.description:
Identifies suspicious file creations in the startup folder of a remote system. An adversary could abuse this to move
laterally by dropping a malicious script or executable that will be executed after a reboot or user logon.

T1021.006.name: Incoming Execution via WinRM Remote Shell
T1021.006.description:
Identifies remote execution via Windows Remote Management (WinRM) remote shell on a target host. This could be an
indication of lateral movement.

T1021.006.name: Incoming Execution via PowerShell Remoting
T1021.006.description:
Identifies remote execution via Windows PowerShell remoting. Windows PowerShell remoting allows a user to run any
Windows PowerShell command on one or more remote computers. This could be an indication of lateral movement.

T1547.001.name: Lateral Movement via Startup Folder
T1547.001.description:
Identifies suspicious file creations in the startup folder of a remote system. An adversary could abuse this to move
laterally by dropping a malicious script or executable that will be executed after a reboot or user logon.

T1547.001.name: Suspicious Startup Shell Folder Modification
T1547.001.description:
Identifies suspicious startup shell folder modifications to change the default Startup directory in order to bypass
detections monitoring file creation in the Windows Startup folder.

T1547.001.name: Uncommon Registry Persistence Change
T1547.001.description:
Detects changes to registry persistence keys that are not commonly used or modified by legitimate programs. This could
be an indication of an adversary's attempt to persist in a stealthy manner.

T1547.001.name: Startup or Run Key Registry Modification
T1547.001.description:
Identifies run key or startup key registry modifications. In order to survive reboots and other system interrupts,
attackers will modify run keys within the registry or leverage startup folder items as a form of persistence.

T1547.001.name: Execution of Persistent Suspicious Program
T1547.001.description:
Identifies execution of suspicious persistent programs (scripts, rundll32, etc.) by looking at process lineage and
command line usage.

T1547.001.name: Startup Persistence by a Suspicious Process
T1547.001.description:
Identifies files written to or modified in the startup folder by commonly abused processes. Adversaries may use this
technique to maintain persistence.

T1547.001.name: Startup Folder Persistence via Unsigned Process
T1547.001.description:
Identifies files written or modified in the startup folder by unsigned processes. Adversaries may abuse this technique
to maintain persistence in an environment.

T1547.001.name: Persistent Scripts in the Startup Directory
T1547.001.description:
Identifies script engines creating files in the Startup folder, or the creation of script files in the Startup folder.
Adversaries may abuse this technique to maintain persistence in an environment.

T1547.001.name: Persistence via Hidden Run Key Detected
T1547.001.description:
Identifies a persistence mechanism that utilizes the NtSetValueKey native API to create a hidden (null terminated)
registry key. An adversary may use this method to hide from system utilities such as the Registry Editor (regedit).

T1547.001.name: Persistence via WMI Standard Registry Provider
T1547.001.description:
Identifies use of the Windows Management Instrumentation StdRegProv (registry provider) to modify commonly abused
registry locations for persistence.

T1574.010.name: Adobe Hijack Persistence
T1574.010.description: Detects writing executable files that will be automatically launched by Adobe on launch.

T1546.011.name: Installation of Custom Shim Databases
T1546.011.description:
Identifies the installation of custom Application Compatibility Shim databases. This Windows functionality has been
abused by attackers to stealthily gain persistence and arbitrary code execution in legitimate Windows processes.

T1546.011.name: Potential Application Shimming via Sdbinst
T1546.011.description:
The Application Shim was created to allow for backward compatibility of software as the operating system codebase
changes over time. This Windows functionality has been abused by attackers to stealthily gain persistence and arbitrary
code execution in legitimate Windows processes.

T1546.009.name: Registry Persistence via AppCert DLL
T1546.009.description:
Detects attempts to maintain persistence by creating registry keys using AppCert DLLs. AppCert DLLs are loaded by every
process using the common API functions to create processes.

T1546.010.name: Registry Persistence via AppInit DLL
T1546.010.description:
AppInit DLLs are dynamic-link libraries (DLLs) that are loaded into every process that creates a user interface (loads
user32.dll) on Microsoft Windows operating systems. The AppInit DLL mechanism is used to load custom code into user-mode
processes, allowing for the customization of the user interf

T1176.name: Browser Extension Install
T1176.description:
Identifies the install of browser extensions. Malicious browser extensions can be installed via app store downloads
masquerading as legitimate extensions, social engineering, or by an adversary that has already compromised a system.

T1137.002.name: Office Test Registry Persistence
T1137.002.description:
Identifies the modification of the Microsoft Office "Office Test" Registry key, a registry location that can be used to
specify a DLL which will be executed every time an MS Office application is started. Attackers can abuse this to gain
persistence on a compromised host.

T1546.007.name: Netsh Helper DLL
T1546.007.description:
Identifies the addition of a Netsh Helper DLL, netsh.exe supports the addition of these DLLs to extend its
functionality. Attackers may abuse this mechanism to execute malicious payloads every time the utility is executed,
which can be done by administrators or a scheduled task.

T1098.002.name: New ActiveSyncAllowedDeviceID Added via PowerShell
T1098.002.description:
Identifies the use of the Exchange PowerShell cmdlet, Set-CASMailbox, to add a new ActiveSync allowed device.
Adversaries may target user email to collect sensitive information.

T1098.002.name: M365 Exchange Mailbox High-Risk Permission Delegated
T1098.002.description:
Identifies the assignment of rights to access content from another mailbox. An adversary may use the compromised account
to send messages to other accounts in the network of the target organization while creating inbox rules, so messages can
evade spam/phishing detection mechanisms.

T1546.013.name: Persistence via PowerShell profile
T1546.013.description:
Identifies the creation or modification of a PowerShell profile. PowerShell profile is a script that is executed when
PowerShell starts to customize the user environment, which can be abused by attackers to persist in a environment where
PowerShell is common.

T1546.008.name: Potential Modification of Accessibility Binaries
T1546.008.description:
Windows contains accessibility features that may be launched with a key combination before a user has logged in. An
adversary can modify the way these programs are launched to get a command prompt or backdoor without logging in to the
system.

T1531.name: Account Password Reset Remotely
T1531.description:
Identifies an attempt to reset a potentially privileged account password remotely. Adversaries may manipulate account
passwords to maintain access or evade password duration policies and preserve compromised credentials.

T1531.name: AWS IAM Deactivation of MFA Device
T1531.description:
Detects the deactivation of a Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) device in AWS Identity and Access Management (IAM). MFA
provides critical protection against unauthorized access by requiring a second factor for authentication. Adversaries or
compromised administrators may deactivate MFA devices to we

T1531.name: AWS IAM Group Deletion
T1531.description:
Detects when an IAM group is deleted using the DeleteGroup API call. Deletion of an IAM group may represent a malicious
attempt to remove audit trails, disrupt operations, or hide adversary activity (for example after using the group
briefly for privileged access). This can be an indicator of impact

T1531.name: Deprecated - AWS RDS Security Group Deletion
T1531.description:
Identifies the deletion of an Amazon Relational Database Service (RDS) Security group. Modern RDS deployments run in a
VPC and use standard EC2 security groups instead. This rule should be retained only for historical log analysis on
legacy CloudTrail data. We recommend relying on "AWS EC2 Security

T1531.name: GCP IAM Role Deletion
T1531.description:
Identifies an Identity and Access Management (IAM) role deletion in Google Cloud Platform (GCP). A role contains a set
of permissions that allows you to perform specific actions on Google Cloud resources. An adversary may delete an IAM
role to inhibit access to accounts utilized by legitimate users.

T1531.name: GCP Service Account Deletion
T1531.description:
Identifies when a service account is deleted in Google Cloud Platform (GCP). A service account is a special type of
account used by an application or a virtual machine (VM) instance, not a person. Applications use service accounts to
make authorized API calls, authorized as either the service accoun

T1531.name: GCP Service Account Disabled
T1531.description:
Identifies when a service account is disabled in Google Cloud Platform (GCP). A service account is a special type of
account used by an application or a virtual machine (VM) instance, not a person. Applications use service accounts to
make authorized API calls, authorized as either the service accou

T1531.name: Google Workspace Admin Role Deletion
T1531.description:
Detects when a custom admin role is deleted. An adversary may delete a custom admin role in order to impact the
permissions or capabilities of system administrators.

T1531.name: Google Workspace MFA Enforcement Disabled
T1531.description:
Detects when multi-factor authentication (MFA) enforcement is disabled for Google Workspace users. An adversary may
disable MFA enforcement in order to weaken an organization’s security controls.

T1531.name: Attempt to Revoke Okta API Token
T1531.description:
Identifies attempts to revoke an Okta API token. An adversary may attempt to revoke or delete an Okta API token to
disrupt an organization's business operations.

T1547.009.name: Persistent Scripts in the Startup Directory
T1547.009.description:
Identifies script engines creating files in the Startup folder, or the creation of script files in the Startup folder.
Adversaries may abuse this technique to maintain persistence in an environment.

T1546.015.name: Component Object Model Hijacking
T1546.015.description:
Identifies Component Object Model (COM) hijacking via registry modification. Adversaries may establish persistence by
executing malicious content triggered by hijacked references to COM objects.

T1547.003.name: Potential Persistence via Time Provider Modification
T1547.003.description:
Identifies modification of the Time Provider. Adversaries may establish persistence by registering and enabling a
malicious DLL as a time provider. Windows uses the time provider architecture to obtain accurate time stamps from other
network devices or clients in the network. Time providers are impl

T1547.005.name: Installation of Security Support Provider
T1547.005.description:
Identifies registry modifications related to the Windows Security Support Provider (SSP) configuration. Adversaries may
abuse this to establish persistence in an environment.

T1505.001.name: Execution via MSSQL xp_cmdshell Stored Procedure
T1505.001.description:
Identifies execution via MSSQL xp_cmdshell stored procedure. Malicious users may attempt to elevate their privileges by
using xp_cmdshell, which is disabled by default, thus, it's important to review the context of it's use.

T1134.002.name: Process Creation via Secondary Logon
T1134.002.description:
Identifies process creation with alternate credentials. Adversaries may create a new process with a different token to
escalate privileges and bypass access controls.

T1134.002.name: Process Created with a Duplicated Token
T1134.002.description:
Identifies the creation of a process impersonating the token of another user logon session. Adversaries may create a new
process with a different token to escalate privileges and bypass access controls.

T1134.002.name: Interactive Logon by an Unusual Process
T1134.002.description:
Identifies interactive logon attempt with alternate credentials and by an unusual process. Adversaries may create a new
token to escalate privileges and bypass access controls.

T1134.002.name: Privileges Elevation via Parent Process PID Spoofing
T1134.002.description:
Identifies parent process spoofing used to create an elevated child process. Adversaries may spoof the parent process
identifier (PPID) of a new process to evade process-monitoring defenses or to elevate privileges.

T1134.002.name: Process Created with an Elevated Token
T1134.002.description:
Identifies the creation of a process running as SYSTEM and impersonating a Windows core binary privileges. Adversaries
may create a new process with a different token to escalate privileges and bypass access controls.

T1134.003.name: Process Creation via Secondary Logon
T1134.003.description:
Identifies process creation with alternate credentials. Adversaries may create a new process with a different token to
escalate privileges and bypass access controls.

T1134.003.name: Interactive Logon by an Unusual Process
T1134.003.description:
Identifies interactive logon attempt with alternate credentials and by an unusual process. Adversaries may create a new
token to escalate privileges and bypass access controls.

T1134.001.name: Process Created with a Duplicated Token
T1134.001.description:
Identifies the creation of a process impersonating the token of another user logon session. Adversaries may create a new
process with a different token to escalate privileges and bypass access controls.

T1134.001.name: First Time Seen NewCredentials Logon Process
T1134.001.description:
Identifies a new credentials logon type performed by an unusual process. This may indicate the existence of an access
token forging capability that are often abused to bypass access control restrictions.

T1134.001.name: PowerShell Script with Token Impersonation Capabilities
T1134.001.description:
Detects scripts that contain PowerShell functions, structures, or Windows API functions related to token
impersonation/theft. Attackers may duplicate then impersonate another user's token to escalate privileges and bypass
access controls.

T1484.001.name: Creation or Modification of a new GPO Scheduled Task or Service
T1484.001.description:
Detects the creation or modification of a new Group Policy based scheduled task or service. These methods are used for
legitimate system administration, but can also be abused by an attacker with domain admin permissions to execute a
malicious payload remotely on all or a subset of the domain joined

T1484.001.name: Startup/Logon Script added to Group Policy Object
T1484.001.description: Detects the modification of Group Policy Objects (GPO) to add a startup/logon script to users or computer objects.

T1484.001.name: Group Policy Abuse for Privilege Addition
T1484.001.description:
Detects the first occurrence of a modification to Group Policy Object Attributes to add privileges to user accounts or
use them to add users as local admins.

T1484.001.name: Scheduled Task Execution at Scale via GPO
T1484.001.description:
Detects the modification of Group Policy Object attributes to execute a scheduled task in the objects controlled by the
GPO.

T1484.name: Creation or Modification of a new GPO Scheduled Task or Service
T1484.description:
Detects the creation or modification of a new Group Policy based scheduled task or service. These methods are used for
legitimate system administration, but can also be abused by an attacker with domain admin permissions to execute a
malicious payload remotely on all or a subset of the domain joined

T1484.name: Startup/Logon Script added to Group Policy Object
T1484.description: Detects the modification of Group Policy Objects (GPO) to add a startup/logon script to users or computer objects.

T1484.name: Group Policy Abuse for Privilege Addition
T1484.description:
Detects the first occurrence of a modification to Group Policy Object Attributes to add privileges to user accounts or
use them to add users as local admins.

T1484.name: Scheduled Task Execution at Scale via GPO
T1484.description:
Detects the modification of Group Policy Object attributes to execute a scheduled task in the objects controlled by the
GPO.

T1484.name: AWS IAM SAML Provider Updated
T1484.description:
Detects when an AWS IAM SAML provider is updated, which manages federated authentication between AWS and external
identity providers (IdPs). Adversaries with administrative access may modify a SAML provider’s metadata or certificate
to redirect authentication flows, enable unauthorized federation, o

T1484.name: M365 Exchange Federated Domain Created or Modified
T1484.description:
Identifies a new or modified federation domain, which can be used to create a trust between O365 and an external
identity provider.

T1547.010.name: Potential Port Monitor or Print Processor Registration Abuse
T1547.010.description:
Identifies port monitor and print processor registry modifications. Adversaries may abuse port monitor and print
processors to run malicious DLLs during system boot that will be executed as SYSTEM for privilege escalation and/or
persistence, if permissions allow writing a fully-qualified pathname fo

T1547.012.name: Potential Port Monitor or Print Processor Registration Abuse
T1547.012.description:
Identifies port monitor and print processor registry modifications. Adversaries may abuse port monitor and print
processors to run malicious DLLs during system boot that will be executed as SYSTEM for privilege escalation and/or
persistence, if permissions allow writing a fully-qualified pathname fo

T1574.011.name: Potential Privilege Escalation via Service ImagePath Modification
T1574.011.description:
Identifies registry modifications to default services that could enable privilege escalation to SYSTEM. Attackers with
privileges from groups like Server Operators may change the ImagePath of services to executables under their control or
to execute commands.

T1574.009.name: Potential Exploitation of an Unquoted Service Path Vulnerability
T1574.009.description:
Adversaries may leverage unquoted service path vulnerabilities to escalate privileges. By placing an executable in a
higher-level directory within the path of an unquoted service executable, Windows will natively launch this executable
from its defined path variable instead of the benign one in a de

T1055.012.name: Unusual Parent-Child Relationship
T1055.012.description:
Identifies Windows programs run from unexpected parent processes. This could indicate masquerading or other strange
activity on a system.

T1055.012.name: Unusual Service Host Child Process - Childless Service
T1055.012.description:
Identifies unusual child processes of Service Host (svchost.exe) that traditionally do not spawn any child processes.
This may indicate a code injection or an equivalent form of exploitation.

T1530.name: AWS CloudTrail Log Created
T1530.description:
Detects creation of a new AWS CloudTrail trail via CreateTrail API. While legitimate during onboarding or auditing
improvements, adversaries can create trails that write to attacker-controlled destinations, limit regions, or otherwise
subvert monitoring objectives. New trails should be validated for

T1530.name: AWS S3 Unauthenticated Bucket Access by Rare Source
T1530.description:
Identifies AWS CloudTrail events where an unauthenticated source is attempting to access an S3 bucket. This activity may
indicate a misconfigured S3 bucket policy that allows public access to the bucket, potentially exposing sensitive data
to unauthorized users. Adversaries can specify --no-sign-req

T1530.name: AWS DynamoDB Scan by Unusual User
T1530.description:
Identifies when an AWS DynamoDB table is scanned by a user who does not typically perform this action. Adversaries may
use the Scan operation to collect sensitive information or exfiltrate data from DynamoDB tables. This rule detects
unusual user activity by monitoring for the Scan action in CloudTr

T1530.name: AWS EC2 Export Task
T1530.description:
Identifies successful export tasks of EC2 instances via the APIs CreateInstanceExportTask, ExportImage, or
CreateStoreImageTask. These exports can be used by administrators for legitimate VM migration or backup workflows
however, an attacker with access to an EC2 instance or AWS credentials can expo

T1530.name: AWS S3 Bucket Policy Added to Share with External Account
T1530.description:
Detects when an Amazon S3 bucket policy is modified to share access with an external AWS account. This rule analyzes
PutBucketPolicy events and compares the S3 bucket’s account ID to any account IDs referenced in the policy’s
Effect=Allow statements. If the policy includes principals from accounts o

T1530.name: AWS S3 Bucket Policy Added to Allow Public Access
T1530.description:
Detects when an Amazon S3 bucket policy is modified to grant public access using a wildcard (Principal:"*") statement.
This rule analyzes PutBucketPolicy events that include both Effect=Allow and Principal:"*" in the request parameters,
indicating that permissions were extended to all identities, po

T1530.name: AWS SNS Rare Protocol Subscription by User
T1530.description: Identifies when a use subscribes to an SNS topic using a new protocol type (ie. email, http, lambda, etc.). SNS allows users to subscribe to recieve topic messages across a broad range of protocols like email, sms, lambda functions, http endpoints, and applications. Adversaries may subscribe to an S

T1530.name: AWS S3 Bucket Enumeration or Brute Force
T1530.description:
Identifies a high number of failed S3 operations against a single bucket from a single source address within a short timeframe. 
This activity can indicate attempts to collect bucket objects or cause an increase in billing to an account via internal "AccessDenied" errors.

T1530.name: AWS CloudTrail Log Updated
T1530.description:
Detects updates to an existing CloudTrail trail via UpdateTrail API which may reduce visibility, change destinations, or
weaken integrity (e.g., removing global events, moving the S3 destination, or disabling validation). Adversaries can
modify trails to evade detection while maintaining a semblance

T1530.name: Azure Storage Account Blob Public Access Enabled
T1530.description:
Identifies when Azure Storage Account Blob public access is enabled, allowing external access to blob containers. This
technique was observed in cloud ransom-based campaigns where threat actors modified storage accounts to expose
non-remotely accessible accounts to the internet for data exfiltration

T1530.name: GCP Pub/Sub Subscription Creation
T1530.description:
Identifies the creation of a subscription in Google Cloud Platform (GCP). In GCP, the publisher-subscriber relationship
(Pub/Sub) is an asynchronous messaging service that decouples event-producing and event-processing services. A
subscription is a named resource representing the stream of messages

T1530.name: GCP Pub/Sub Topic Creation
T1530.description:
Identifies the creation of a topic in Google Cloud Platform (GCP). In GCP, the publisher-subscriber relationship
(Pub/Sub) is an asynchronous messaging service that decouples event-producing and event-processing services. A topic is
used to forward messages from publishers to subscribers.

T1530.name: M365 OneDrive Excessive File Downloads with OAuth Token
T1530.description:
Identifies when an excessive number of files are downloaded from OneDrive using OAuth authentication. Adversaries may
conduct phishing campaigns to steal OAuth tokens and impersonate users. These access tokens can then be used to download
files from OneDrive.

T1619.name: AWS S3 Unauthenticated Bucket Access by Rare Source
T1619.description:
Identifies AWS CloudTrail events where an unauthenticated source is attempting to access an S3 bucket. This activity may
indicate a misconfigured S3 bucket policy that allows public access to the bucket, potentially exposing sensitive data
to unauthorized users. Adversaries can specify --no-sign-req

T1619.name: AWS S3 Bucket Enumeration or Brute Force
T1619.description:
Identifies a high number of failed S3 operations against a single bucket from a single source address within a short timeframe. 
This activity can indicate attempts to collect bucket objects or cause an increase in billing to an account via internal "AccessDenied" errors.

T1619.name: Azure Blob Storage Container Access Level Modified
T1619.description:
Identifies changes to container access levels in Azure. Anonymous public read access to containers and blobs in Azure is
a way to share data broadly, but can present a security risk if access to sensitive data is not managed judiciously.

T1562.008.name: AWS CloudTrail Log Evasion
T1562.008.description: Identifies the evasion of cloudtrail logging for IAM actions involving policy creation, modification or attachment. When making certain policy-related API calls, an adversary may pad the associated policy document with whitespaces to trigger CloudTrail’s logging size constraints, resulting in incomp

T1562.008.name: AWS Config Resource Deletion
T1562.008.description:
Identifies attempts to delete AWS Config resources. AWS Config provides continuous visibility into resource
configuration changes and compliance posture across an account. Deleting Config components can significantly reduce
security visibility and auditability. Adversaries may delete or disable Conf

T1562.008.name: AWS Configuration Recorder Stopped
T1562.008.description:
Identifies when an AWS Config configuration recorder is stopped. AWS Config recorders continuously track and record
configuration changes across supported AWS resources. Stopping the recorder immediately reduces visibility into
infrastructure changes and can be abused by adversaries to evade detecti

T1562.008.name: AWS VPC Flow Logs Deletion
T1562.008.description: Identifies the deletion of one or more flow logs in AWS Elastic Compute Cloud (EC2). An adversary may delete flow logs in an attempt to evade defenses.

T1562.008.name: AWS Route 53 Resolver Query Log Configuration Deleted
T1562.008.description:
Identifies the deletion of an Amazon Route 53 Resolver Query Log Configuration. Resolver query logs provide critical
visibility into DNS activity across VPCs, including lookups made by EC2 instances, containers, Lambda functions, and
other AWS resources. Deleting a query log configuration immediatel

T1562.008.name: AWS S3 Bucket Configuration Deletion
T1562.008.description:
Identifies the deletion of critical Amazon S3 bucket configurations such as bucket policies, lifecycle configurations
or encryption settings. These actions are typically administrative but may also represent
adversarial attempts to remove security controls, disable data retention mechanisms, or conc

T1562.008.name: AWS S3 Bucket Expiration Lifecycle Configuration Added
T1562.008.description:
Identifies the addition of an expiration lifecycle configuration to an Amazon S3 bucket. S3 lifecycle rules can
automatically delete or transition objects after a defined period. Adversaries can abuse them by configuring
auto-deletion of logs, forensic evidence, or sensitive objects to cover their t

T1562.008.name: AWS S3 Bucket Server Access Logging Disabled
T1562.008.description:
Identifies when server access logging is disabled for an Amazon S3 bucket. Server access logs provide a detailed record
of requests made to an S3 bucket. When server access logging is disabled for a bucket, it could indicate an adversary's
attempt to impair defenses by disabling logs that contain ev

T1562.008.name: AWS SQS Queue Purge
T1562.008.description:
Identifies when an AWS Simple Queue Service (SQS) queue is purged. Purging an SQS queue permanently deletes all messages
currently in the queue. Adversaries may use this action to disrupt application workflows, destroy operational data, or
impair monitoring and alerting by removing messages that con

T1562.008.name: Azure Event Hub Deleted
T1562.008.description:
Identifies an Event Hub deletion in Azure. An Event Hub is an event processing service that ingests and processes large
volumes of events and data. An adversary may delete an Event Hub in an attempt to evade detection.

T1562.008.name: Azure Diagnostic Settings Deleted
T1562.008.description:
Identifies the deletion of diagnostic settings in Azure, which send platform logs and metrics to different destinations.
An adversary may delete diagnostic settings in an attempt to evade defenses.

T1562.008.name: M365 Exchange Mailbox Audit Logging Bypass Added
T1562.008.description:
Detects the occurrence of mailbox audit bypass associations. The mailbox audit is responsible for logging specified
mailbox events (like accessing a folder or a message or permanently deleting a message). However, actions taken by some
authorized accounts, such as accounts used by third-party tools

T1562.007.name: AWS EC2 Network Access Control List Deletion
T1562.007.description: Identifies the deletion of an Amazon Elastic Compute Cloud (EC2) network access control list (ACL) or one of its ingress/egress entries.

T1562.007.name: Deprecated - AWS ElastiCache Security Group Created
T1562.007.description:
Identifies when an ElastiCache security group has been created. Amazon EC2-Classic and ElastiCache CacheSecurityGroups
have been retired. Modern ElastiCache deployments run in a VPC and use standard EC2 security groups instead. This rule
should be retained only for historical log analysis on legacy

T1562.007.name: Deprecated - AWS ElastiCache Security Group Modified or Deleted
T1562.007.description:
Identifies when an ElastiCache security group has been modified or deleted. Amazon EC2-Classic and ElastiCache
CacheSecurityGroups have been retired. Modern ElastiCache deployments run in a VPC and use standard EC2 security groups
instead. This rule should be retained only for historical log analysi

T1562.007.name: Insecure AWS EC2 VPC Security Group Ingress Rule Added
T1562.007.description: Identifies when a specified inbound (ingress) rule is added or adjusted for a VPC security group in AWS EC2. This rule detects when a security group rule is added that allows traffic from any IP address or from a specific IP address to common remote access ports, such as 22 (SSH) or 3389 (RDP). Adve

T1562.007.name: AWS WAF Access Control List Deletion
T1562.007.description:
Identifies the deletion of an AWS Web Application Firewall (WAF) Web ACL. Web ACLs are the core enforcement objects in
AWS WAF, defining which traffic is inspected, allowed, or blocked for protected applications. Deleting a Web ACL removes
all associated rules, protections, and logging configuration

T1562.007.name: AWS WAF Rule or Rule Group Deletion
T1562.007.description:
Identifies the deletion of an AWS Web Application Firewall (WAF) rule or rule group. WAF rules and rule groups enforce
critical protections for web applications by filtering malicious HTTP requests, blocking known attack patterns, and
enforcing access controls. Deleting these rules—even briefly—can

T1562.007.name: AWS EC2 Network Access Control List Creation
T1562.007.description: Identifies the creation of an AWS EC2 network access control list (ACL) or an entry in a network ACL with a specified rule number. Adversaries may exploit ACLs to establish persistence or exfiltrate data by creating permissive rules.

T1562.007.name: AWS EC2 Security Group Configuration Change
T1562.007.description: Identifies a change to an AWS Security Group Configuration. A security group is like a virtual firewall, and modifying configurations may allow unauthorized access. Threat actors may abuse this to establish persistence, exfiltrate data, or pivot in an AWS environment.

T1562.007.name: Azure VNet Firewall Policy Deleted
T1562.007.description:
Identifies the deletion of a firewall policy in Azure. An adversary may delete a firewall policy in an attempt to evade
defenses and/or to eliminate barriers to their objective.

T1562.007.name: Azure VNet Firewall Front Door WAF Policy Deleted
T1562.007.description:
Identifies the deletion of a Frontdoor Web Application Firewall (WAF) Policy in Azure. An adversary may delete a
Frontdoor Web Application Firewall (WAF) Policy in an attempt to evade defenses and/or to eliminate barriers to their
objective.

T1562.007.name: GCP Virtual Private Cloud Network Deletion
T1562.007.description:
Identifies when a Virtual Private Cloud (VPC) network is deleted in Google Cloud Platform (GCP). A VPC network is a
virtual version of a physical network within a GCP project. Each VPC network has its own subnets, routes, and firewall,
as well as other elements. An adversary may delete a VPC network

T1562.007.name: GCP Virtual Private Cloud Route Creation
T1562.007.description:
Identifies when a virtual private cloud (VPC) route is created in Google Cloud Platform (GCP). Google Cloud routes
define the paths that network traffic takes from a virtual machine (VM) instance to other destinations. These
destinations can be inside a Google VPC network or outside it. An adversary

T1562.007.name: GCP Virtual Private Cloud Route Deletion
T1562.007.description:
Identifies when a Virtual Private Cloud (VPC) route is deleted in Google Cloud Platform (GCP). Google Cloud routes
define the paths that network traffic takes from a virtual machine (VM) instance to other destinations. These
destinations can be inside a Google VPC network or outside it. An adversary

T1562.007.name: Domain Added to Google Workspace Trusted Domains
T1562.007.description:
Detects when a domain is added to the list of trusted Google Workspace domains. An adversary may add a trusted domain in
order to collect and exfiltrate data from their target’s organization with less restrictive security controls.

T1562.007.name: Attempt to Deactivate an Okta Network Zone
T1562.007.description:
Detects attempts to deactivate an Okta network zone. Okta network zones can be configured to limit or restrict access to
a network based on IP addresses or geolocations. An adversary may attempt to modify, delete, or deactivate an Okta
network zone in order to remove or weaken an organization's secu

T1562.007.name: Attempt to Delete an Okta Network Zone
T1562.007.description:
Detects attempts to delete an Okta network zone. Okta network zones can be configured to limit or restrict access to a
network based on IP addresses or geolocations. An adversary may attempt to modify, delete, or deactivate an Okta network
zone in order to remove or weaken an organization's security

T1562.007.name: Attempt to Deactivate an Okta Policy
T1562.007.description:
Detects attempts to deactivate an Okta policy. An adversary may attempt to deactivate an Okta policy in order to weaken
an organization's security controls. For example, an adversary may attempt to deactivate an Okta multi-factor
authentication (MFA) policy in order to weaken the authentication requ

T1562.007.name: Attempt to Deactivate an Okta Policy Rule
T1562.007.description:
Detects attempts to deactivate a rule within an Okta policy. An adversary may attempt to deactivate a rule within an
Okta policy in order to remove or weaken an organization's security controls.

T1562.007.name: Attempt to Delete an Okta Policy
T1562.007.description:
Detects attempts to delete an Okta policy. An adversary may attempt to delete an Okta policy in order to weaken an
organization's security controls. For example, an adversary may attempt to delete an Okta multi-factor authentication
(MFA) policy in order to weaken the authentication requirements for

T1562.007.name: Attempt to Delete an Okta Policy Rule
T1562.007.description:
Detects attempts to delete a rule within an Okta policy. An adversary may attempt to delete an Okta policy rule in order
to weaken an organization's security controls.

T1562.007.name: Attempt to Modify an Okta Network Zone
T1562.007.description:
Detects attempts to modify an Okta network zone. Okta network zones can be configured to limit or restrict access to a
network based on IP addresses or geolocations. An adversary may attempt to modify, delete, or deactivate an Okta network
zone in order to remove or weaken an organization's security

T1562.007.name: Attempt to Modify an Okta Policy
T1562.007.description:
Detects attempts to modify an Okta policy. An adversary may attempt to modify an Okta policy in order to weaken an
organization's security controls. For example, an adversary may attempt to modify an Okta multi-factor authentication
(MFA) policy in order to weaken the authentication requirements for

T1562.007.name: Attempt to Modify an Okta Policy Rule
T1562.007.description:
Detects attempts to modify a rule within an Okta policy. An adversary may attempt to modify an Okta policy rule in order
to weaken an organization's security controls.

T1578.002.name: AWS RDS DB Instance Restored
T1578.002.description:
Identifies the restoration of an AWS RDS database instance from a snapshot or S3 backup. Adversaries with access to
valid credentials may restore copies of existing databases to bypass logging and monitoring controls or to exfiltrate
sensitive data from a duplicated environment. This rule detects su

T1578.004.name: AWS RDS DB Instance Restored
T1578.004.description:
Identifies the restoration of an AWS RDS database instance from a snapshot or S3 backup. Adversaries with access to
valid credentials may restore copies of existing databases to bypass logging and monitoring controls or to exfiltrate
sensitive data from a duplicated environment. This rule detects su

T1578.name: AWS RDS DB Instance Restored
T1578.description:
Identifies the restoration of an AWS RDS database instance from a snapshot or S3 backup. Adversaries with access to
valid credentials may restore copies of existing databases to bypass logging and monitoring controls or to exfiltrate
sensitive data from a duplicated environment. This rule detects su

T1578.name: GCP Storage Bucket Configuration Modification
T1578.description:
Identifies when the configuration is modified for a storage bucket in Google Cloud Platform (GCP). An adversary may
modify the configuration of a storage bucket in order to weaken the security controls of their target's environment.

T1485.001.name: AWS S3 Bucket Expiration Lifecycle Configuration Added
T1485.001.description:
Identifies the addition of an expiration lifecycle configuration to an Amazon S3 bucket. S3 lifecycle rules can
automatically delete or transition objects after a defined period. Adversaries can abuse them by configuring
auto-deletion of logs, forensic evidence, or sensitive objects to cover their t

T1098.001.name: AWS First Occurrence of STS GetFederationToken Request by User
T1098.001.description:
Identifies the first occurrence of an AWS Security Token Service (STS) GetFederationToken request made by a user. The GetFederationToken API call allows users to request temporary security credentials to
access AWS resources. The maximum expiration period for these tokens is 36 hours and they can be

T1098.001.name: AWS IAM User Created Access Keys For Another User
T1098.001.description:
An adversary with access to a set of compromised credentials may attempt to persist or escalate privileges by creating a
new set of credentials for an existing user. This rule looks for use of the IAM `CreateAccessKey` API operation to
create new programmatic access keys for another IAM user.

T1098.001.name: AWS RDS DB Instance or Cluster Password Modified
T1098.001.description:
Identifies the modification of the master password for an AWS RDS DB instance or cluster. Changing the master password
is a legitimate recovery action when access is lost, but adversaries with sufficient permissions may modify it to regain
access, establish persistence, bypass existing controls, or

T1098.001.name: Azure Storage Account Key Regenerated
T1098.001.description:
Identifies a rotation to storage account access keys in Azure. Regenerating access keys can affect any applications or
Azure services that are dependent on the storage account key. Adversaries may regenerate a key as a means of acquiring
credentials to access systems and resources.

T1098.001.name: Entra ID Application Credential Modified
T1098.001.description:
Identifies when a new credential is added to an application in Azure. An application may use a certificate or secret
string to prove its identity when requesting a token. Multiple certificates and secrets can be added for an application
and an adversary may abuse this by creating an additional authe

T1098.001.name: Entra ID Service Principal Credentials Created by Unusual User
T1098.001.description:
Identifies when new Service Principal credentials have been added in Microsoft Entra ID. In most organizations,
credentials will be added to service principals infrequently. Hijacking an application (by adding a rogue secret or
certificate) with granted permissions will allow the attacker to access

T1087.004.name: AWS IAM Principal Enumeration via UpdateAssumeRolePolicy
T1087.004.description:
Detects repeated failed attempts to update an IAM role’s trust policy in an AWS account, consistent with role and user
enumeration techniques. In this technique, an attacker who controls credentials in the current account repeatedly calls
UpdateAssumeRolePolicy on a single role, cycling through gues

T1087.004.name: AWS STS GetCallerIdentity API Called for the First Time
T1087.004.description:
An adversary with access to a set of compromised credentials may attempt to verify that the credentials are valid and
determine what account they are using. This rule looks for the first time an identity has called the
STS GetCallerIdentity API, which may be an indicator of compromised credentials.

T1087.004.name: Entra ID Sign-in BloodHound Suite User-Agent Detected
T1087.004.description:
Identifies potential enumeration activity using AzureHound, SharpHound, or BloodHound across Microsoft cloud services.
These tools are often used by red teamers and adversaries to map users, groups, roles, applications, and access
relationships within Microsoft Entra ID (Azure AD) and Microsoft 365.

T1087.004.name: Entra ID Sign-in TeamFiltration User-Agent Detected
T1087.004.description:
Identifies potential enumeration or password spraying activity using TeamFiltration tool. TeamFiltration is an
open-source enumeration, password spraying and exfiltration tool designed for Entra ID and Microsoft 365. Adversaries
are known to use TeamFiltration in-the-wild to enumerate users, groups,

T1648.name: AWS Lambda Layer Added to Existing Function
T1648.description:

dependencies, or runtime modifications to be injected into a function’s execution environment. Adversaries with the

T1648.name: First Time AWS CloudFormation Stack Creation
T1648.description: This rule detects the first time a principal calls AWS CloudFormation CreateStack, CreateStackSet or CreateStackInstances API. CloudFormation is used to create a collection of cloud resources called a stack, via a defined template file. An attacker with the appropriate privileges could leverage Clou

T1648.name: Azure Automation Runbook Created or Modified
T1648.description:
Identifies when an Azure Automation runbook is created or modified. An adversary may create or modify an Azure
Automation runbook to execute malicious code and maintain persistence in their target's environment.

T1648.name: GitHub App Deleted
T1648.description: Detects the deletion of a GitHub app either from a repo or an organization.

T1648.name: High Number of Cloned GitHub Repos From PAT
T1648.description:
Detects a high number of unique private repo clone events originating from a single personal access token within a short
time period.

T1119.name: AWS EC2 Export Task
T1119.description:

however, an attacker with access to an EC2 instance or AWS credentials can expo

T1020.name: AWS EC2 Full Network Packet Capture Detected
T1020.description:
Detects successful creation of an Amazon EC2 Traffic Mirroring session. A session copies full packets from a source
Elastic Network Interface (ENI) to a mirror target (e.g., an ENI or NLB) using a mirror filter (ingress/egress rules).
While used for diagnostics and NDR/IDS tooling, adversaries can a

T1213.006.description:
Identifies the export of a DB snapshot or DB cluster data to Amazon S3. Snapshot exports can be used for analytics or
migration workflows, but adversaries may abuse them to exfiltrate sensitive data outside of RDS-managed storage.
Exporting a snapshot creates a portable copy of the database contents

T1213.description:

T1496.004.description: Identifies when a use subscribes to an SNS topic using a new protocol type (ie. email, http, lambda, etc.). SNS allows users to subscribe to recieve topic messages across a broad range of protocols like email, sms, lambda functions, http endpoints, and applications. Adversaries may subscribe to an S

T1496.004.name: AWS SNS Topic Message Publish by Rare User
T1496.004.description:
Identifies when an SNS topic message is published by a rare user in AWS. Adversaries may publish messages to SNS topics
for phishing campaigns, data exfiltration, or lateral movement within the AWS environment. SNS topics are used to send
notifications and messages to subscribed endpoints such as ap

T1496.004.name: AWS SNS Topic Created by Rare User
T1496.004.description:

when this behavior is observed for the first time by a u

T1657.name: AWS S3 Bucket Enumeration or Brute Force
T1657.description:
Identifies a high number of failed S3 operations against a single bucket from a single source address within a short timeframe. 
This activity can indicate attempts to collect bucket objects or cause an increase in billing to an account via internal "AccessDenied" errors.

T1556.006.name: AWS IAM Deactivation of MFA Device
T1556.006.description:

T1556.006.description:

sessions, so using them to register or enable MFA devices is u

T1556.006.name: AWS STS AssumeRole with New MFA Device
T1556.006.description:

While a new MFA device is not always indicative of malicious behavior it should be verified

T1556.006.name: Entra ID MFA Disabled for User
T1556.006.description:
Identifies when multi-factor authentication (MFA) is disabled for an Entra ID user account. An adversary may disable MFA
for a user account in order to weaken the authentication requirements for the account.

T1556.006.name: MFA Deactivation with no Re-Activation for Okta User Account
T1556.006.description:
Detects multi-factor authentication (MFA) deactivation with no subsequent re-activation for an Okta user account. An
adversary may deactivate MFA for an Okta user account in order to weaken the authentication requirements for the
account.

T1556.006.name: Stolen Credentials Used to Login to Okta Account After MFA Reset
T1556.006.description:

undermine multi-factor authentication (MFA) and single sign-on (SSO) mechanisms for an Okta user account.

T1078.004.description: Identifies a successful login to the AWS Management Console by the Root user.

Detects a sequence of events in Microsoft Entra ID indicative of a suspicious cloud-based device registration,
potentially using ROADtools. This behavior involves adding a device via the Device Registration Service, followed by the
assignment of registered users and owners — a pattern consistent wit

T1098.005.name: Entra ID User Sign-in with Unusual Non-Managed Device
T1098.005.description:
Identifies when a Microsoft Entra ID user signs in from a device that is not typically used by the user and is not managed, which may
indicate potential compromise or unauthorized access attempts. This rule detects unusual sign-in activity by comparing
the device used for the sign-in against the use

T1098.005.name: Entra ID Protection User Alert and Device Registration
T1098.005.description: Identifies sequence of events where a Microsoft Entra ID protection alert is followed by an attempt to register a new device by the same user principal. This behavior may indicate an adversary using a compromised account to register a device, potentially leading to unauthorized access to resources o

T1098.005.name: M365 Identity OAuth Flow by User Sign-in to Device Registration
T1098.005.description:
Identifies attempts to register a new device in Microsoft Entra ID after OAuth authentication with authorization code
grant. Adversaries may use OAuth phishing techniques to obtain an OAuth authorization code, which can then be exchanged
for access and refresh tokens. This rule detects a sequence of

T1136.003.name: AWS IAM Create User via Assumed Role on EC2 Instance
T1136.003.description:
Detects the creation of an AWS Identity and Access Management (IAM) user initiated by an assumed role on an EC2
instance. Assumed roles allow users or services to temporarily adopt different AWS permissions, but the creation of IAM
users through these roles, particularly from within EC2 instances, m

T1136.003.name: AWS IAM Group Creation
T1136.003.description:
Identifies the creation of a group in AWS Identity and Access Management (IAM). Groups specify permissions for multiple
users. Any user in a group automatically has the permissions that are assigned to the group. Adversaries who obtain
credentials with IAM write privileges may create a new group as

T1136.003.name: Deprecated - AWS RDS Security Group Creation
T1136.003.description:
Identifies the creation of an Amazon Relational Database Service (RDS) Security group. Modern RDS deployments run in a
VPC and use standard EC2 security groups instead. This rule should be retained only for historical log analysis on
legacy CloudTrail data. We recommend relying on "AWS EC2 Security

T1136.003.name: Entra ID Service Principal Created
T1136.003.description:
Identifies when a new service principal is added in Microsoft Entra ID. An application, hosted service, or automated
tool that accesses or modifies resources needs an identity created. This identity is known as a service principal. For
security reasons, it's always recommended to use service princip

T1136.003.name: New GitHub Owner Added
T1136.003.description:
Detects when a new member is added to a GitHub organization as an owner. This role provides admin level privileges. Any
new owner roles should be investigated to determine it's validity. Unauthorized owner roles could indicate compromise
within your organization and provide unlimited access to data

T1556.009.name: AWS RDS DB Instance Made Public
T1556.009.description:
Identifies the creation or modification of an Amazon RDS DB instance or cluster where the "publiclyAccessible" attribute
is set to "true". Publicly accessible RDS instances expose a network endpoint on the public internet, which may allow
unauthorized access if combined with overly permissive securi

T1556.009.name: Entra ID Conditional Access Policy (CAP) Modified
T1556.009.description: Identifies a modification to a conditional access policy (CAP) in Microsoft Entra ID. Adversaries may modify existing CAPs to loosen access controls and maintain persistence in the environment with a compromised identity or entity.

T1556.009.name: Entra ID OIDC Discovery URL Modified
T1556.009.description:
Detects a change to the OpenID Connect (OIDC) discovery URL in the Entra ID Authentication Methods Policy. This behavior
may indicate an attempt to federate Entra ID with an attacker-controlled identity provider, enabling bypass of
multi-factor authentication (MFA) and unauthorized access through br

T1556.009.name: Entra ID External Authentication Methods (EAM) Modified
T1556.009.description:
Identifies when an external authentication method (EAM) is added or modified in Entra ID. EAM may allow adversaries to
bypass multi-factor authentication (MFA) requirements, potentially leading to unauthorized access to user accounts and
sensitive resources by using bring-your-own IdP (BYOIDP) metho

T1584.001.name: AWS Route 53 Domain Transfer Lock Disabled
T1584.001.description:
Identifies when the transfer lock on an AWS Route 53 domain is disabled. The transfer lock protects domains from being
moved to another registrar or AWS account without authorization. Disabling this lock removes an important safeguard
against domain hijacking. Adversaries who gain access to domain-m

T1584.001.name: AWS Route 53 Domain Transferred to Another Account
T1584.001.description:
Identifies when an AWS Route 53 domain is transferred to another AWS account. Transferring a domain changes
administrative control of the DNS namespace, enabling the receiving account to modify DNS records, route traffic,
request certificates, and potentially hijack operational workloads. Adversarie

T1584.name: AWS Route 53 Domain Transfer Lock Disabled
T1584.description:
Identifies when the transfer lock on an AWS Route 53 domain is disabled. The transfer lock protects domains from being
moved to another registrar or AWS account without authorization. Disabling this lock removes an important safeguard
against domain hijacking. Adversaries who gain access to domain-m

T1584.name: AWS Route 53 Domain Transferred to Another Account
T1584.description:

T1583.001.name: AWS Route 53 Private Hosted Zone Associated With a VPC
T1583.001.description:
Identifies when an AWS Route 53 private hosted zone is associated with a new Virtual Private Cloud (VPC). Private hosted
zones restrict DNS resolution to specific VPCs, and associating additional VPCs expands the scope of what networks can
resolve internal DNS records. Adversaries with sufficient pe

T1583.name: AWS Route 53 Private Hosted Zone Associated With a VPC
T1583.description:
Identifies when an AWS Route 53 private hosted zone is associated with a new Virtual Private Cloud (VPC). Private hosted
zones restrict DNS resolution to specific VPCs, and associating additional VPCs expands the scope of what networks can
resolve internal DNS records. Adversaries with sufficient pe

T1548.005.name: AWS IAM Customer-Managed Policy Attached to Role by Rare User
T1548.005.description:
Detects when an AWS Identity and Access Management (IAM) customer-managed policy is attached to a role by an unusual or
unauthorized user. Customer-managed policies are policies created and controlled within an AWS account, granting
specific permissions to roles or users when attached. This rule ide

T1548.005.name: AWS STS AssumeRoot by Rare User and Member Account
T1548.005.description:
Identifies when the STS AssumeRoot action is performed by a rare user in AWS. The AssumeRoot action allows users to
assume the root member account role, granting elevated but specific permissions based on the task policy specified.
Adversaries who have compromised user credentials can use this techn

T1484.002.name: AWS IAM SAML Provider Updated
T1484.002.description:
Detects when an AWS IAM SAML provider is updated, which manages federated authentication between AWS and external
identity providers (IdPs). Adversaries with administrative access may modify a SAML provider’s metadata or certificate
to redirect authentication flows, enable unauthorized federation, o

T1484.002.name: M365 Exchange Federated Domain Created or Modified
T1484.002.description:
Identifies a new or modified federation domain, which can be used to create a trust between O365 and an external
identity provider.

T1608.name: AWS SNS Topic Created by Rare User
T1608.description:
Identifies when an SNS topic is created by a user who does not typically perform this action. Adversaries may create SNS
topics to stage capabilities for data exfiltration or other malicious activities. This is a New Terms rule that only flags
when this behavior is observed for the first time by a u

T1608.name: Azure Automation Webhook Created
T1608.description:
Identifies when an Azure Automation webhook is created. Azure Automation runbooks can be configured to execute via a
webhook. A webhook uses a custom URL passed to Azure Automation along with a data payload specific to the runbook. An
adversary may create a webhook in order to trigger a runbook that

T1608.name: M365 OneDrive Malware File Upload
T1608.description:
Identifies the occurence of files uploaded to OneDrive being detected as Malware by the file scanning engine. Attackers
can use File Sharing and Organization Repositories to spread laterally within the company and amplify their access.
Users can inadvertently share these files without knowing their

T1608.name: M365 SharePoint Malware File Detected

Identifies the occurence of files uploaded to SharePoint being detected as Malware by the file scanning engine.
Attackers can use File Sharing and Organization Repositories to spread laterally within the company and amplify their

T1213.002.name: Entra ID SharePoint Accessed by Unusual User and Microsoft Authentication Broker Client
T1213.002.description:
This rule detects non-interactive authentication activity against SharePoint Online (`Office 365 SharePoint Online`) by
a user principal via the `Microsoft Authentication Broker` application. The session leverages a refresh token or Primary
Refresh Token (PRT) without interactive sign-in, often used

T1110.004.name: Entra ID User Sign-in Brute Force Attempted
T1110.004.description:
Identifies potential brute-force attacks targeting user accounts by analyzing failed sign-in patterns in Microsoft Entra
ID Sign-In Logs. This detection focuses on a high volume of failed interactive or non-interactive authentication
attempts within a short time window, often indicative of password

T1110.004.name: Entra ID Excessive Account Lockouts Detected
T1110.004.description:
Identifies a high count of failed Microsoft Entra ID sign-in attempts as the result of the target user account being
locked out. Adversaries may attempt to brute-force user accounts by repeatedly trying to authenticate with incorrect
credentials, leading to account lockouts by Entra ID Smart Lockout

T1110.004.name: Entra ID Sign-in Brute Force Attempted (Microsoft 365)
T1110.004.description:
Identifies potential brute-force attacks targeting Microsoft 365 user accounts by analyzing failed sign-in patterns in
Microsoft Entra ID Sign-In Logs. This detection focuses on a high volume of failed interactive or non-interactive

T1110.004.name: M365 Identity User Brute Force Attempted
T1110.004.description:

match password spraying, credential stuffing, or password guessing behavior. Adversaries may attempt brute-force
authentication with credentials obtained from previous breaches, le

T1110.004.name: M365 Identity User Account Lockouts
T1110.004.description:
Detects a burst of Microsoft 365 user account lockouts within a short 5-minute window. A high number of IdsLocked login
errors across multiple user accounts may indicate brute-force attempts for the same users resulting in lockouts.

T1110.004.name: Multiple Okta User Auth Events with Same Device Token Hash Behind a Proxy
T1110.004.description:
Detects when Okta user authentication events are reported for multiple users with the same device token hash behind a
proxy.

T1110.004.name: Multiple Okta User Authentication Events with Client Address
T1110.004.description:

using a list of known usernames and passwords to gain unauthor

T1110.004.name: Multiple Okta User Authentication Events with Same Device Token Hash
T1110.004.description:
Detects when a high number of Okta user authentication events are reported for multiple users in a short time frame.
Adversaries may attempt to launch a credential stuffing or password spraying attack from the same device by using a list
of known usernames and passwords to gain unauthorized access t

T1110.004.name: High Number of Okta Device Token Cookies Generated for Authentication
T1110.004.description:

password spraying attack from the same device by using a list of known

T1069.003.name: Entra ID Sign-in BloodHound Suite User-Agent Detected
T1069.003.description:
Identifies potential enumeration activity using AzureHound, SharpHound, or BloodHound across Microsoft cloud services.
These tools are often used by red teamers and adversaries to map users, groups, roles, applications, and access
relationships within Microsoft Entra ID (Azure AD) and Microsoft 365.

T1069.003.name: Entra ID Sign-in TeamFiltration User-Agent Detected
T1069.003.description:

T1529.name: Azure Kubernetes Services (AKS) Kubernetes Pods Deleted
T1529.description:
Identifies the deletion of Azure Kubernetes Pods. Adversaries may delete a Kubernetes pod to disrupt the normal behavior
of the environment.

T1656.name: Entra ID OAuth user_impersonation Scope for Unusual User and Client
T1656.description:
Identifies rare occurrences of OAuth workflow for a user principal that is single factor authenticated, with an OAuth
scope containing user_impersonation for a token issued by Entra ID. Adversaries may use this scope to gain unauthorized
access to user accounts, particularly when the sign-in session

T1102.002.name: Statistical Model Detected C2 Beaconing Activity
T1102.002.description:
A statistical model has identified command-and-control (C2) beaconing activity. Beaconing can help attackers maintain
stealthy communication with their C2 servers, receive instructions and payloads, exfiltrate data and maintain
persistence in a network.

T1102.002.name: Statistical Model Detected C2 Beaconing Activity with High Confidence
T1102.002.description:
A statistical model has identified command-and-control (C2) beaconing activity with high confidence. Beaconing can help
attackers maintain stealthy communication with their C2 servers, receive instructions and payloads, exfiltrate data and
maintain persistence in a network.

T1011.name: Spike in Bytes Sent to an External Device via Airdrop
T1011.description:
A machine learning job has detected high bytes of data written to an external device via Airdrop. In a typical
operational setting, there is usually a predictable pattern or a certain range of data that is written to external
devices. An unusually large amount of data being written is anomalous and

T1072.name: New GitHub App Installed
T1072.description:
This rule detects when a new GitHub App has been installed in your organization account. GitHub Apps extend GitHub's
functionality both within and outside of GitHub. When an app is installed it is granted permissions to read or modify
your repository and organization data. Only trusted apps should b

T1074.002.name: Google Drive Ownership Transferred via Google Workspace
T1074.002.description:
Drive and Docs is a Google Workspace service that allows users to leverage Google Drive and Google Docs. Access to files
is based on inherited permissions from the child organizational unit the user belongs to which is scoped by
administrators. Typically if a user is removed, their files can be tran

T1114.003.name: Google Workspace Custom Gmail Route Created or Modified
T1114.003.description:
Detects when a custom Gmail route is added or modified in Google Workspace. Adversaries can add a custom e-mail route
for outbound mail to route these e-mails to their own inbox of choice for data gathering. This allows adversaries to
capture sensitive information from e-mail and potential attachmen

T1114.003.name: M365 Exchange Inbox Forwarding Rule Created
T1114.003.description:
Identifies when a new Inbox forwarding rule is created in Microsoft 365. Inbox rules process messages in the Inbox based

abuse Inbox Rules to intercept and exfiltrate email data withou

T1078.001.name: Kubernetes Anonymous Request Authorized
T1078.001.description:
This rule detects when an unauthenticated user request is authorized within the cluster. Attackers may attempt to use
anonymous accounts to gain initial access to the cluster or to avoid attribution of their activities within the cluster.
This rule excludes the /healthz, /livez and /readyz endpoints

T1078.001.name: Kubernetes Suspicious Assignment of Controller Service Account
T1078.001.description:
This rule detects a request to attach a controller service account to an existing or new pod running in the kube-system
namespace. By default, controllers running as part of the API Server utilize admin-equivalent service accounts hosted in
the kube-system namespace. Controller service accounts aren

T1564.008.name: M365 Exchange Inbox Phishing Evasion Rule Created
T1564.008.description:
Identifies when a user creates a new inbox rule in Microsoft 365 that deletes or moves emails containing suspicious
keywords. Adversaries who have compromised accounts often create inbox rules to hide alerts, security notifications, or
other sensitive messages by automatically deleting them or movin

T1080.name: M365 OneDrive Malware File Upload
T1080.description:

can use File Sharing and Organization Repositories to spread laterally within the company and amplify their access.
Users can inadvertently share these files without knowing their

T1608.001.name: M365 SharePoint Malware File Detected

Attackers can use File Sharing and Organization Repositories to spread laterally within the company and amplify their

T1111.name: Attempted Bypass of Okta MFA
T1111.description:
Detects attempts to bypass Okta multi-factor authentication (MFA). An adversary may attempt to bypass the Okta MFA
policies configured for an organization in order to obtain unauthorized access to an application.

T1621.name: Potential Okta MFA Bombing via Push Notifications
T1621.description:
Detects when an attacker abuses the Multi-Factor authentication mechanism by repeatedly issuing login requests until the
user eventually accepts the Okta push notification. An adversary may attempt to bypass the Okta MFA policies configured
for an organization to obtain unauthorized access.

T1621.name: Potentially Successful Okta MFA Bombing via Push Notifications
T1621.description:
Detects when an attacker abuses the Multi-Factor authentication mechanism by repeatedly issuing login requests until the
user eventually accepts the Okta push notification. An adversary may attempt to bypass the Okta MFA policies configured
for an organization to obtain unauthorized access.

T1199.name: Okta Sign-In Events via Third-Party IdP
T1199.description: Detects sign-in events where authentication is carried out via a third-party Identity Provider (IdP).

T1550.004.name: Multiple Okta Sessions Detected for a Single User
T1550.004.description:
Detects when a user has started multiple Okta sessions with the same user account and different session IDs. This may
indicate that an attacker has stolen the user's session cookie and is using it to access the user's account from a
different location.

T1556.007.name: New Okta Identity Provider (IdP) Added by Admin
T1556.007.description:
Detects the creation of a new Identity Provider (IdP) by a Super Administrator or Organization Administrator within
Okta.
